topics.title: Power Diodes - Types, Characteristics (Static and Dynamic), Effects of Reverse Recovery Transient, Ratings, Schottky Diodes - Features and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives > - **Module:** Module-I > - **Topic:** Power Diodes - Types, Characteristics (Static and Dynamic), Effects of Reverse Recovery Transient, Ratings, Schottky Diodes - Features and Applications # ⚡ Power Diodes — The Unsung Heroes of Power Conversion ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Standard Wording):** > A **Power Diode** is a two-terminal, uncontrolled semiconductor switching device designed to handle high currents (typically from **1 A to several thousand amperes**) and high blocking voltages (from **50 V to 10 kV**). It conducts current only in the forward direction when forward-biased, and blocks it in the reverse direction when reverse-biased. It is the simplest, most rugged, and most widely used power semiconductor device, serving as the foundational building block of uncontrolled rectifiers, freewheeling paths, and snubber circuits. Unlike signal-level diodes, a power diode is specifically engineered and optimized to handle: - **High forward current density** with low conduction loss. - **High reverse blocking voltage** with negligible leakage. - **Fast dynamic switching** to minimize losses in high-frequency PWM applications. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hydraulic Check Valve" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Water-Pipe Analogy:** > Imagine a heavy industrial water pipeline. A **Power Diode behaves exactly like a one-way check valve** in this pipeline. > - **Forward bias (valve open):** Water (current) flows freely in one direction with minimal friction drop (analogous to a low forward voltage drop, $V_F \approx 0.7$–$2.5\text{ V}$). > - **Reverse bias (valve closed):** The valve slams shut and blocks water completely — but in reality, a tiny trickle leaks (reverse leakage current $I_R$, measured in **mA or µA**). > - **The "Reverse Recovery" trap:** When you suddenly try to reverse the water flow, the valve does not snap shut *instantly*. There is a brief, violent backflow as the mechanical flap decelerates and seats itself. This is the **reverse recovery transient** — a critical inefficiency that engineers must account for. This is *exactly* how a power diode behaves electrically: a one-way street for current, but with a finite "deceleration" period during turn-off. --- ## 1.3 Why Power Diodes are Studied in Power Electronics > [!TIP] > **Engineering Significance:** > - They form the heart of **uncontrolled rectifiers** (single-phase and three-phase diode bridge rectifiers). > - They are used as **freewheeling diodes** to protect switches (IGBT, BJT, SCR) from inductive kickback. > - They act as **snubber diodes** and **clamping devices** in chopper and inverter circuits. > - They are essential in **regenerative braking** paths of motor drives. > - **Schottky diodes**, a special class, dominate in **low-voltage, high-frequency** applications like SMPS and solar converters. --- ## 1.4 Standard Symbolic Representation The schematic symbol and the physical construction (a $PN$-junction with a large cross-sectional area) are universal. The arrow points from anode (A) to cathode (K), indicating the **conventional direction of forward current flow**. | Terminal | Symbol | Role | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Anode (A) | A | Positive terminal in forward bias; current enters here | | Cathode (K) | K | Negative terminal in forward bias; current exits here | # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Types, Characteristics & Physics ## 2.1 Classification of Power Diodes Power diodes are broadly classified based on their **reverse recovery characteristics** and **intended application**: | Type | Reverse Recovery Time ($t_{rr}$) | Typical Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **General Purpose Rectifier Diodes** | $t_{rr} \approx 25$–$100\ \mu s$ | Line-frequency ($50$/$60\ Hz$) rectifiers, battery chargers | | **Fast Recovery Diodes (FRD)** | $t_{rr} \approx 1$–$5\ \mu s$ | High-frequency rectifiers, freewheeling in choppers | | **Ultrafast Recovery Diodes** | $t_{rr} \approx 100$–$500\ ns$ | SMPS, high-frequency inverters, snubber circuits | | **Schottky Diodes** (Majority carrier) | $t_{rr} \approx$ negligible (ps range) | Low-voltage, high-current, high-frequency DC-DC converters | | **Line-Frequency Diodes** | $t_{rr}$ very high (slow) | HVDC, industrial welding rectifiers | --- ## 2.2 Static (V-I) Characteristics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > The **Static Characteristics** describe the steady-state voltage-current relationship of the diode when it is *not* switching. There are two distinct regions: the **forward conduction region** and the **reverse blocking region**. ### 2.2.1 Forward Conduction Region When $V_{AK} > 0$ (anode positive w.r.t. cathode), the diode conducts. The governing equation is the classic Shockley diode equation, but for power diodes, a simplified linear model is used: $$V_F = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_F$$ Where: - $V_F$ = forward voltage drop (volts) - $V_{T0}$ = threshold (or offset) voltage, typically **$0.6$–$0.9\ V$** for silicon - $r_T$ = dynamic (slope) resistance of the diode, typically **milliohms** - $I_F$ = forward current (amperes) > [!NOTE] > For **Schottky diodes**, $V_{T0}$ is much lower (around **$0.3$ V**), which is why they are preferred in low-voltage, high-current applications. ### 2.2.2 Reverse Blocking Region When $V_{AK} < 0$, the diode ideally blocks all current. In practice, a tiny reverse leakage current $I_R$ (typically **µA to mA**) flows. As reverse voltage increases up to the **breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$**, the diode remains in blocking mode. Beyond $V_{BR}$, **avalanche breakdown** occurs and the diode is destroyed unless current is externally limited. --- ## 2.3 Dynamic Characteristics — The Switching Transients > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > **Dynamic characteristics** describe the *transient behavior* of a power diode during turn-on and turn-off. For power electronics, **turn-off (reverse recovery)** is far more critical than turn-on because it dictates switching losses and generates electromagnetic interference (EMI). ### 2.3.1 Turn-On Transient When a diode is suddenly forward-biased, the forward voltage overshoots to a value $V_{FP}$ (forward peak voltage) due to the **conductivity modulation** of the drift region. This transient is typically very short (sub-microsecond) and is rarely a design concern. ### 2.3.2 Turn-Off Transient — The Reverse Recovery Phenomenon This is the **single most important dynamic behavior** of a power diode and a favorite KTU exam topic. When a conducting diode is suddenly reverse-biased, the diode does **not** turn off instantly. Instead, a large **reverse current** flows for a brief period as stored minority carriers are swept out of the junction. **Three distinct phases of reverse recovery:** | Phase | Time Interval | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Storage Phase ($t_s$ or $t_a$)** | $0$ to $t_1$ | Diode is in storage mode; reverse current rises to $I_{RM}$ as stored charge is removed | | **Transition Phase ($t_t$ or $t_b$)** | $t_1$ to $t_2$ | $PN$ junction regains blocking capability; reverse current decays from $I_{RM}$ to $0$ | | **Total Reverse Recovery Time ($t_{rr}$)** | $0$ to $t_2$ | $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$ | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board Exam Cheat-Codes) > [!TIP] > **Memorize these formulas — they are repeatedly asked in KTU exams.** | Formula | Description | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $V_F = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_F$ | Static forward voltage drop model | Volts (V) | | $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$ | Total reverse recovery time | seconds (s) | | $Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr}$ | Reverse recovery charge (assumes triangular area) | Coulombs (C) | | $I_{RM} = \dfrac{2 \cdot Q_{rr}}{t_{rr}}$ | Peak reverse recovery current | Amperes (A) | | $S = \dfrac{t_b}{t_a}$ | Softness factor (or recovery factor) | dimensionless | | $E_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot Q_{rr} \cdot V_R$ | Reverse recovery energy loss per switching cycle | Joules (J) | | $P_{rr} = f_s \cdot E_{rr}$ | Reverse recovery power loss | Watts (W) | | $\dfrac{dv}{dt} \big\vert_{\max} = \dfrac{I_{RM}}{C_j}$ | Maximum $dv/dt$ stress on the diode | V/s | Where: - $Q_{rr}$ = **Reverse Recovery Charge** (Coulombs) - $I_{RM}$ = **Peak Reverse Recovery Current** (Amperes) - $V_R$ = **Reverse Blocking Voltage** (Volts) - $f_s$ = **Switching Frequency** (Hz) - $C_j$ = **Junction Capacitance** (Farads) - $S$ = **Softness Factor** (ratio of transition to storage time) > [!WARNING] > **Note on $S$ (Softness Factor):** > - $S = 1$: ideal **soft recovery** (gradual decay of current — preferred for EMI) > - $S < 1$: **abrupt (snappy) recovery** — causes high $dv/dt$, EMI, and voltage spikes > - $S > 1$: **oscillatory recovery** — generally undesirable --- ## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Real-World Importance > [!NOTE] > The reverse recovery transient is **not** a minor curiosity. In a three-phase bridge rectifier feeding a $10\ kW$ motor drive, the reverse recovery of the diodes generates: > 1. **Conduction losses** proportional to $Q_{rr}$ and switching frequency > 2. **Voltage spikes** that can exceed the rated blocking voltage (causing catastrophic failure) > 3. **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)** due to high $di/dt$ and $dv/dt$ > 4. **Cross-conduction** in half-bridge configurations (shoot-through) > > This is why **fast-recovery and Schottky diodes** are mandatory in modern high-frequency converters. # 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions ## 3.1 Derivation of Reverse Recovery Charge ($Q_{rr}$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theoretical Foundation:** > During the storage phase $t_a$, the reverse current rises linearly from zero to its peak value $I_{RM}$. During the transition phase $t_b$, the current decays from $I_{RM}$ to zero (approximately linearly, for an idealized analysis). ### Derivation Step-by-Step: **Step 1:** The reverse recovery charge $Q_{rr}$ is the total charge that flows in the *reverse* direction during the turn-off transient. Mathematically, it is the area under the reverse current waveform: $$Q_{rr} = \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} i_R(t)\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Approximating the reverse current waveform as a **triangle** with: - Base length = $t_{rr}$ (along the time axis) - Height = $I_{RM}$ (peak reverse current) **Step 3:** Area of a triangle is $\dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$: $$Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot I_{RM}$$ **Step 4:** This is the standard textbook result used in KTU problems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Peak Reverse Recovery Current Starting from the charge equation and solving for $I_{RM}$: $$Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot I_{RM}$$ Rearranging: $$I_{RM} = \dfrac{2 \cdot Q_{rr}}{t_{rr}}$$ **Physical interpretation:** A diode with a *larger* stored charge and *shorter* recovery time will have a *higher* peak reverse current, which is undesirable. --- ## 3.3 Energy Loss During Reverse Recovery The energy dissipated in the diode during one turn-off cycle is the integral of the product of the reverse voltage (which approximates the supply voltage $V_R$) and the reverse current: $$E_{rr} = \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} V_R \cdot i_R(t)\, dt$$ Assuming $V_R$ is approximately constant (equal to the DC link voltage) and using the triangular approximation for $i_R(t)$: $$E_{rr} = V_R \cdot \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} i_R(t)\, dt = V_R \cdot Q_{rr}$$ Substituting $Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot I_{RM}$: $$E_{rr} = V_R \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot I_{RM} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr}$$ A more commonly cited conservative approximation (used in KTU exam solutions) is: $$E_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot Q_{rr} \cdot V_R = \dfrac{1}{8} \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr} \cdot V_R$$ The factor $\dfrac{1}{2}$ vs $\dfrac{1}{4}$ depends on whether the waveform is treated as a full triangle or a different geometric approximation. **For KTU board exam purposes, use:** $$E_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot Q_{rr} \cdot V_R$$ The **average power loss** due to reverse recovery at switching frequency $f_s$ is: $$P_{rr} = f_s \cdot E_{rr} = f_s \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot Q_{rr} \cdot V_R$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem:** A power diode has a reverse recovery time $t_{rr} = 5\ \mu s$ and a peak reverse recovery current $I_{RM} = 40\ A$. It operates in a circuit with a reverse voltage $V_R = 200\ V$ and a switching frequency $f_s = 10\ kHz$. Calculate: > (a) Reverse recovery charge $Q_{rr}$ > (b) Average power loss due to reverse recovery $P_{rr}$ **Solution:** **Part (a):** Using the triangular approximation: $$Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr}$$ $$Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 40\ \text{A} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$$ $$Q_{rr} = 100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C} = 100\ \mu\text{C}$$ **Part (b):** Energy loss per cycle: $$E_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot Q_{rr} \cdot V_R$$ $$E_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{C} \cdot 200\ \text{V}$$ $$E_{rr} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J} = 5\ \text{mJ}$$ Average power loss: $$P_{rr} = f_s \cdot E_{rr}$$ $$P_{rr} = 10{,}000\ \text{Hz} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{J}$$ $$P_{rr} = 50\ \text{W}$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always include units and show every algebraic step. KTU examiners award partial credit for method even if the final answer is incorrect. --- ## 3.5 Softness Factor Analytical Implications The **softness factor** $S$ is defined as: $$S = \dfrac{t_b}{t_a}$$ Where: - $t_a$ = storage time (current rises from $0$ to $I_{RM}$) - $t_b$ = transition time (current falls from $I_{RM}$ to $0$) **Implication for circuit design:** If $S \geq 1$, the current decay is gradual, producing low $di/dt$ and hence low $dv/dt$ across the parasitic inductances of the circuit. This is the **ideal** behavior. If $S \ll 1$, the current snaps off abruptly, inducing large voltage transients: $$V_{spike} = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$$ Such diodes are called **"snappy"** and require **snubber circuits** for protection. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reverse Recovery Waveform — Phase-by-Phase Functional Map > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram maps the **time progression of current** through a power diode during a turn-off transient. This is the classic KTU exam waveform you must be able to sketch by hand. ```mermaid flowchart TD StartA["Time t = 0
Diode Conducting
IF = +50 A (Forward)"] Phase1["Phase 1: Reverse Current Rises
0 to t1

iR rises from 0 to IRM"] PeakR["Time t = t1
Peak Reverse Current
iR = IRM = -40 A (peak)"] Phase2["Phase 2: Reverse Current Falls
t1 to t2

iR falls from IRM to 0"] EndA["Time t = t2
Diode Off
Reverse leakage IR only"] StartA -->|"Reverse bias applied"| Phase1 Phase1 --> PeakR PeakR --> Phase2 Phase2 --> EndA Note1["Storage Charge Qrr = 0.5 x IRM x trr"] Phase1 -.-> Note1 ``` --- ## 4.2 Static V-I Characteristic Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR RegionA["REGION A: Reverse Blocking
VAK less than 0

trr 25 to 100 us
Low frequency line rectifiers"] PD --> FR["Fast Recovery
trr 1 to 5 us
Choppers, snubbers"] PD --> UF["Ultrafast Recovery
trr 100 to 500 ns
SMPS, inverters"] PD --> SK["Schottky
Majority carrier, no trr
Low VF, low voltage high frequency"] GP --> AppA["Battery chargers
HVDC
Uncontrolled rectifiers"] FR --> AppB["DC choppers
Freewheeling in converters"] UF --> AppC["PWM inverters
High frequency SMPS"] SK --> AppD["Solar MPPT
DC-DC buck boost
Laptop adapters"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Reverse Recovery Energy Loss Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Forward Current IF flows through diode"] B["Reverse bias applied at t = 0"] C["Stored minority carriers swept out"] D["Reverse current IR rises to peak IRM"] E["Junction regains blocking"] F["IR falls to zero at t = trr"] G["Total charge Qrr flowed in reverse"] H["Energy lost: Err = 0.25 x Qrr x VR"] I["Power loss: Prr = fs x Err"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I ``` # 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank > [!NOTE] > The following questions are modeled on actual KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) patterns under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme. Each question is tagged with its expected marks, cognitive level, and Course Outcome (CO) mapping. --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **Q: With a neat sketch, explain the static V-I characteristics of a power diode. Mark the important regions.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The static V-I characteristics of a power diode consist of **three distinct regions**: 1. **Forward Conduction Region (Quadrant I):** When the anode-to-cathode voltage $V_{AK}$ is positive and exceeds the threshold voltage $V_{T0}$ (typically $0.7$ to $0.9\ V$ for Si), the diode conducts heavily. The forward current $I_F$ rises sharply and is limited only by the external load. The relationship is $V_F = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_F$. 2. **Reverse Blocking Region (Quadrant III):** When $V_{AK}$ is negative, a very small reverse leakage current $I_R$ (order of $\mu A$ to $m A$) flows. The diode blocks the applied reverse voltage up to the **breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$**. 3. **Breakdown Region (Beyond $V_{BR}$):** If the reverse voltage exceeds $V_{BR}$, avalanche multiplication occurs and the diode conducts in reverse — usually destructive. **Sketch (verbal description):** The V-I curve resembles an "L" shape lying in the first and third quadrants, with a sharp knee at $V_{T0}$ in forward direction and a near-zero slope in reverse direction until $V_{BR}$. **Key Points for Full Marks:** - Label axes: $V_{AK}$ (x-axis) and $I_{AK}$ (y-axis) - Mark $V_{T0}$, $I_F$, $I_R$, and $V_{BR}$ - Mention $V_F$ equation --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Q: What is reverse recovery time of a power diode? Why is it important in high-frequency applications?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition:** The **reverse recovery time ($t_{rr}$)** is the time interval between the instant the forward current of a conducting diode passes through zero (when the diode is reverse-biased) and the instant the reverse current decays back to $25\%$ of its peak reverse value $I_{RM}$. It is the sum of the **storage time ($t_a$)** and the **transition time ($t_b$)**: $$t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$$ **Importance in High-Frequency Applications:** 1. **Switching Losses:** Power loss in the diode due to reverse recovery is $P_{rr} = f_s \cdot E_{rr}$. As switching frequency $f_s$ increases (e.g., in SMPS at $100\ kHz$+), the cumulative switching loss becomes a major portion of total losses. 2. **Voltage Spikes:** The abrupt interruption of reverse current at the end of $t_{rr}$ generates high $di/dt$, which produces voltage spikes $V = L \cdot (di/dt)$ across circuit inductances — these can exceed the diode's rated $V_{BR}$ and destroy it. 3. **EMI Generation:** The fast-changing reverse current is a major source of electromagnetic interference, requiring additional filtering. 4. **Circuit Malfunction:** In bridge configurations, slow recovery can cause **cross-conduction (shoot-through)**, where one diode is still conducting while the complementary switch in the other leg turns on, creating a short circuit. **Conclusion:** For high-frequency applications, diodes with **low $t_{rr}$** (fast or ultrafast recovery diodes, or Schottky diodes) are essential. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question carries **internal choice**. You must attempt **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each alternative has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: With the help of neat waveforms, explain the reverse recovery characteristics of a power diode. Define the terms: (i) Peak reverse recovery current, (ii) Reverse recovery charge, (iii) Softness factor. Hence derive the expression for the average power loss due to reverse recovery.** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Solution:** **Reverse Recovery Process (Waveform Description):** Assume a diode is conducting forward current $I_F$ at time $t = 0$. Suddenly, the circuit conditions are reversed (e.g., by closing a switch in a half-bridge configuration). The forward current $I_F$ begins to decrease linearly. At time $t = 0$ (the zero-crossing of current), the diode does not turn off — instead, the current reverses direction. The reverse recovery waveform has **four key intervals**: - **Interval 1 ($0$ to $t_1$):** Reverse current $i_R$ rises from $0$ to its peak value $I_{RM}$. This is the **storage phase** with duration $t_a$. The $PN$ junction still has not recovered its blocking capability. - **Interval 2 ($t_1$ to $t_2$):** The junction regains blocking. The reverse current $i_R$ decays from $I_{RM}$ to nearly zero. This is the **transition phase** with duration $t_b$. - **Total reverse recovery time:** $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$. **Definitions:** (i) **Peak Reverse Recovery Current ($I_{RM}$):** The maximum value of the reverse current attained during the turn-off transient, occurring at time $t_1$. It depends on the rate of fall of forward current ($di/dt$) and the stored minority carrier charge. (ii) **Reverse Recovery Charge ($Q_{rr}$):** The total charge that flows in the reverse direction during the turn-off process. It is the area under the reverse current-time curve: $$Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr} \quad \text{(for triangular approximation)}$$ (iii) **Softness Factor ($S$):** The ratio of the transition time to the storage time: $$S = \dfrac{t_b}{t_a}$$ - $S = 1$: ideal soft recovery - $S < 1$: snappy recovery (high $dv/dt$, undesirable) --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Derivation of Average Power Loss due to Reverse Recovery:** The energy dissipated in the diode during one turn-off cycle is the integral of the product of the reverse voltage across the diode and the reverse current: $$E_{rr} = \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} V_R \cdot i_R(t)\, dt$$ Assuming the reverse voltage $V_R$ across the diode is approximately constant (equal to the external supply/commutation voltage) and substituting the triangular approximation for $i_R(t)$: $$E_{rr} = V_R \cdot \int_{0}^{t_{rr}} i_R(t)\, dt = V_R \cdot Q_{rr}$$ Substituting $Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr}$: $$E_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr}$$ The **average power loss** is the product of energy per cycle and the switching frequency $f_s$: $$P_{rr} = f_s \cdot E_{rr}$$ $$\boxed{P_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot f_s \cdot V_R \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr}}$$ **Equivalent form using $Q_{rr}$:** $$P_{rr} = f_s \cdot V_R \cdot Q_{rr} = f_s \cdot V_R \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr}$$ > [!TIP] > **Conclusion:** Higher switching frequency $f_s$, higher reverse voltage $V_R$, and longer recovery time $t_{rr}$ all contribute to increased reverse recovery losses. This is the primary reason ultrafast and Schottky diodes are used in high-frequency converters. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Explain the construction, working principle, and V-I characteristics of a Schottky diode. List at least four applications and explain why it is preferred over a conventional PN-junction power diode in low-voltage, high-frequency applications.** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Construction:** A **Schottky diode** (also called a **hot-carrier diode**) is formed by joining a **metal** (typically platinum, molybdenum, chromium, or tungsten) with a **doped N-type semiconductor** (usually silicon). The metal-semiconductor junction forms a **Schottky barrier**, not a PN-junction. - The anode is the metal contact; the cathode is the N-type semiconductor. - The barrier height $\phi_B$ is determined by the choice of metal and is typically $0.5$ to $0.9\ eV$. **Working Principle:** In a Schottky diode, **only majority carriers (electrons)** participate in conduction. When forward-biased, electrons from the N-side gain enough energy to cross into the metal — these are "hot carriers." There is **no minority carrier injection** and **no charge storage** in the junction. This gives the Schottky diode its two most important properties: 1. **Very low forward voltage drop** ($V_F \approx 0.3$ to $0.5\ V$ instead of $0.7$ to $0.9\ V$ for Si PN-junction diodes). This drastically reduces conduction losses. 2. **Negligible reverse recovery time** ($t_{rr}$ is essentially zero, in the **picosecond** range). Switching losses are practically eliminated. **V-I Characteristics (Comparison):** | Parameter | Schottky Diode | PN-Junction Diode | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Forward voltage drop $V_F$ | $0.3$ to $0.5\ V$ | $0.7$ to $1.2\ V$ | | Reverse recovery time $t_{rr}$ | Negligible (ps) | $25\ \mu s$ (general) to $100\ ns$ (ultrafast) | | Conduction mechanism | Majority carriers only | Minority carrier injection | | Reverse leakage current $I_R$ | Higher | Lower | | Maximum blocking voltage | Limited (typically $\leq 200\ V$) | Up to $10\ kV$ | --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Applications of Schottky Diodes:** 1. **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** Used as output rectifiers in low-voltage (e.g., $3.3\ V$, $5\ V$, $12\ V$) high-current rails of computer and laptop power supplies. 2. **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Systems:** Used in **MPPT charge controllers** and as **blocking diodes** to prevent reverse current flow from the battery to the panel at night. The low $V_F$ minimizes power loss. 3. **DC-DC Buck/Boost Converters:** Used as freewheeling diodes in synchronous and asynchronous buck converters. The negligible $t_{rr}$ enables switching frequencies up to several MHz. 4. **Radio Frequency (RF) Applications:** Schottky diodes are used in **RF mixers, detectors, and voltage multipliers** (e.g., in microwave rectifiers) due to their fast switching. 5. **Logic Gates and ICs:** Used in TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) families as clamp diodes (e.g., Schottky TTL, 74LS series). 6. **Automotive Electronics:** Reverse-battery protection circuits and alternator rectifiers. **Why Schottky is Preferred in Low-Voltage, High-Frequency Applications:** > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Lower $V_F$:** At low output voltages (e.g., $3.3\ V$), a PN-diode's $V_F = 0.7\ V$ represents a $21\%$ loss, which is unacceptable. A Schottky's $V_F = 0.3\ V$ reduces this loss to $9\%$. > - **Zero $t_{rr}$:** In high-frequency converters ($f_s > 100\ kHz$), the reverse recovery loss $P_{rr} = f_s \cdot Q_{rr} \cdot V_R$ becomes the dominant loss mechanism for PN-diodes. The Schottky's near-zero $Q_{rr}$ eliminates this. > - **Higher efficiency:** Lower conduction + switching losses yield significantly higher converter efficiency (often $95\%+$). > - **Compactness:** Higher switching frequency allows smaller magnetics and capacitors, reducing overall size and cost. > [!WARNING] > **Limitation:** Schottky diodes have **higher reverse leakage current** and are limited to **low reverse blocking voltages** (typically $\leq 200\ V$). They are not suitable for high-voltage power transmission or HVDC applications where silicon PN-diodes dominate. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to draw the V-I curve or waveform:** KTU examiners award marks specifically for *neat sketches* with labeled axes and regions. A pure textual answer without a diagram loses 2 to 3 marks immediately. > 2. **Confusing $t_a$ and $t_b$:** Many students swap storage time and transition time. Remember: **Storage = current rises; Transition = current falls.** > 3. **Mixing up $Q_{rr}$ and $I_{RM}$ formulas:** $Q_{rr}$ has units of **Coulombs (C)**, $I_{RM}$ has units of **Amperes (A)**. Do not interchange. > 4. **Ignoring units in numerical problems:** KTU strictly penalizes missing units in the final answer. > 5. **Writing $V_F = V_T + I_F \cdot R$ instead of $V_F = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_F$:** Use the exact textbook symbols $V_{T0}$ (threshold) and $r_T$ (slope resistance). > 6. **Forgetting the softness factor definition:** This is a guaranteed 1-mark question in almost every KTU exam. Always define it. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Print this section and stick it to your study wall before the exam.** ✅ **Power Diode:** A two-terminal uncontrolled device; conducts in forward, blocks in reverse. ✅ **Static Characteristics:** Defined by $V_F = V_{T0} + r_T \cdot I_F$ in forward; near-zero $I_R$ in reverse blocking. ✅ **Three regions of V-I curve:** Forward conduction, Reverse blocking, Breakdown (avalanche). ✅ **Dynamic Characteristics — Reverse Recovery:** The transient phenomenon where the diode conducts in reverse direction briefly after the forward current is interrupted. ✅ **Phases of Reverse Recovery:** Storage phase ($t_a$, current rises) and Transition phase ($t_b$, current falls). Total: $t_{rr} = t_a + t_b$. ✅ **Peak Reverse Current $I_{RM}$:** Maximum reverse current during turn-off. ✅ **Reverse Recovery Charge $Q_{rr}$:** Area under reverse current-time curve: $Q_{rr} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RM} \cdot t_{rr}$. ✅ **Softness Factor $S = t_b / t_a$:** $S \geq 1$ is preferred (soft recovery); $S < 1$ is snappy and harmful. ✅ **Reverse Recovery Power Loss:** $P_{rr} = f_s \cdot E_{rr} = f_s \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot Q_{rr} \cdot V_R$. ✅ **Diode Ratings (KTU Favorite):** - **$V_{RRM}$:** Peak Repetitive Reverse Voltage - **$I_{F(AV)}$:** Average Forward Current - **$I_{FSM}$:** Non-Repetitive Surge Current - **$I^2 t$ rating:** Surge current capability (fuse coordination) - **$T_j$:** Junction Temperature (typically $-40°\text{C}$ to $150°\text{C}$) - **$R_{th(J-C)}$:** Thermal resistance junction-to-case - **$P_D$:** Maximum power dissipation ✅ **Schottky Diode:** Metal-semiconductor junction; majority carrier device; $V_F \approx 0.3\ V$; near-zero $t_{rr}$; used in SMPS, solar MPPT, low-voltage DC-DC converters; limited to low reverse voltage. ✅ **High-frequency reality:** $f_s \uparrow \implies t_{rr}$ must $\downarrow$ to keep $P_{rr}$ within thermal limits. ✅ **Silicon vs Schottky:** Silicon for high voltage and line frequency; Schottky for low voltage and high frequency. ✅ **Heat Sink:** All power diodes above $2$ to $5\ A$ require heat sinks; thermal resistance $R_{th(J-A)} = R_{th(J-C)} + R_{th(C-S)} + R_{th(S-A)}$. ✅ **Always show units, always label diagrams, always define symbols before using them.**

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives
> - **Module:** Module Module-I
> - **Topic:** Semi-controlled Switch: SCR (Thyristor) - Symbol, Structure, Characteristics (Static and Dynamic), Turn-on and Turn-off Phenomena, Ratings, Gate Control of SCR, Gate Pulse Magnitude and Duration Requirements, Typical Gate Drive Circuits, Gate Synchronisation, Isolated Gate Drives

# ⚡ Module-I: The Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR) — A Comprehensive Study

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)** is a four-layer, three-junction, semi-controlled, bistable power semiconductor switch belonging to the **thyristor family**. It is a unidirectional device that conducts current only in the forward direction (Anode to Cathode) and blocks in both the reverse direction and the forward direction until a **positive gate pulse** is applied between the Gate (G) and Cathode (K).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition as per KTU 2024 Syllabus:**
> "An SCR is a semi-controlled switch that can be turned ON by a gate signal but cannot be turned OFF through the gate terminal. Turn-off is achieved by reducing the anode current below the holding current ($I_H$) — a process known as natural or forced commutation."

The term *semi-controlled* is critical: the SCR has full control over turn-ON but only partial (indirect) control over turn-OFF. This single feature defines its entire application domain — AC-DC converters (rectifiers), AC-AC converters with natural commutation, and controlled DC drives.

### 1.2 Symbol & Terminal Identification

The standard IEEE/ANSI symbol of an SCR consists of a diode-like triangle and bar, with an additional gate terminal (G) attached to the bar (cathode side). The anode (A) is the topmost terminal, and the cathode (K) is at the bottom.

| Terminal | Symbol | Function |
|----------|--------|----------|
| **Anode (A)** | Top | Positive terminal during forward conduction |
| **Cathode (K)** | Bottom | Common return for anode and gate currents |
| **Gate (G)** | Side | Control input terminal |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap with a Stuck Handle"

Imagine a water tap connected to a high-pressure pipeline. The tap is *closed* (SCR in blocking state). Two facts about this tap:

- You need a **short, sharp push** on the handle (gate pulse) to open the tap and let water flow (turn-ON).
- Once water is flowing at a sufficient rate, the tap **latches open by itself** — the water pressure holds it open (anode current > $I_L$ — latching current).
- You **cannot close the tap with the handle**; the only way to close it is to **reduce the water flow to a trickle** (anode current < $I_H$ — holding current).

This perfectly models the SCR's behavior. The handle is the gate, the water is the anode current, and the tap's pressure-holding mechanism is the regenerative internal feedback that makes the SCR "self-sustaining" once turned ON.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> The SCR is classified as a **semi-controlled (semi-conductor controlled) switch** in the matrix of power semiconductor devices. The full taxonomy includes:
> - Uncontrolled: Power Diode
> - Semi-controlled: SCR, TRIAC
> - Fully controlled: BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, GTO

### 1.4 Physical Structure — The Four-Layer PNPN Device

An SCR is fabricated as a four-layer, three-terminal device with alternating P-type and N-type semiconductor regions, forming **three PN junctions** ($J_1$, $J_2$, $J_3$). The structure is:

- **P-layer (top)** → connected to **Anode (A)**
- **N-layer (upper middle)** → connected to **Cathode (K)** via the bottom N-layer; this layer also hosts the **Gate (G)** contact
- **P-layer (lower middle)** → floats internally but is accessed via the gate terminal
- **N-layer (bottom)** → connected to **Cathode (K)**

The three junctions are:
- **$J_1$** — between top P-layer and upper N-layer (anode-side junction)
- **$J_2$** — between upper N-layer and middle P-layer (middle junction — the *reverse-biased* junction during forward blocking)
- **$J_3$** — between middle P-layer and bottom N-layer (cathode-side junction)

A cross-sectional sketch would reveal that the **gate lead is physically shorted to the lower N-layer**, with the P-layer below $J_3$ exposed as a tiny surface pad. This P-layer beneath the gate contact is what allows control via minority carrier injection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Transistor Analogy — Why the SCR Latches

The most elegant way to understand the SCR's regenerative action is to model it as a **PNP transistor and an NPN transistor** interconnected in a positive-feedback loop. The middle P-layer is shared as the collector of the NPN and the base of the PNP; the upper N-layer is shared as the base of the NPN and the collector of the PNP.

| Layer | PNP Role | NPN Role |
|-------|----------|----------|
| Top P-layer | Emitter (E₁) | — |
| Upper N-layer | Collector (C₁) = Base of NPN (B₂) | Base (B₂) = Collector of PNP (C₁) |
| Middle P-layer | Base (B₁) = Collector of NPN (C₂) | Collector (C₂) = Base of PNP (B₁) |
| Bottom N-layer | — | Emitter (E₂) |
| External Terminals | Anode at E₁ | Cathode at E₂, Gate at B₁ (NPN base) |

**Working Logic:**
1. Apply a small gate current $I_G$. This injects electrons into the P-base of the NPN, turning it ON.
2. The NPN's collector current ($I_{C2}$) flows into the P-base of the PNP.
3. The PNP's collector current ($I_{C1}$) flows back into the base of the NPN.
4. This is **positive feedback**: more NPN collector → more PNP collector → more NPN base drive.
5. Both transistors saturate rapidly. The device "latches" ON.
6. Once saturated, $I_G$ can be removed without affecting conduction.

Mathematically, the regeneration condition for **latch-up** is:
$$\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$$

where $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are the common-base current gains of the PNP and NPN transistors, respectively.

### 2.2 Biasing of Junctions in Three Operating Modes

| Operating Mode | $J_1$ (Anode junction) | $J_2$ (Middle junction) | $J_3$ (Cathode junction) |
|----------------|------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Reverse Blocking** | Reverse Biased | Forward Biased | Forward Biased |
| **Forward Blocking** | Forward Biased | Reverse Biased | Forward Biased |
| **Forward Conduction** | Forward Biased | Forward Biased | Forward Biased |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students often confuse the bias states. Remember: the **middle junction $J_2$** is the "switching" junction — it transitions from reverse-biased (blocking) to forward-biased (conducting) at the instant of turn-ON.

### 2.3 Static V-I Characteristics — The Full Picture

The static (DC) V-I characteristic curve plots anode current $I_A$ (Y-axis) against anode-cathode voltage $V_{AK}$ (X-axis). It has four distinct regions:

**Region I — Forward Blocking (OFF State, Forward direction)**
- $V_{AK}$ is positive but below $V_{BO}$ (Breakover Voltage).
- Only a small leakage current (mA range) flows.
- The device is in a high-impedance state, blocking forward voltage.
- The middle junction $J_2$ is reverse-biased and supports the entire applied voltage.

**Region II — Forward Conduction (ON State)**
- Once $V_{AK} \geq V_{BO}$ (without gate) or once $I_G$ is applied below $V_{BO}$ (forward breakover voltage reduces with increasing $I_G$), the SCR switches ON.
- The voltage drops to a low value ($V_T \approx 1$ to $2$ V), and current is limited only by the external load.
- The device exhibits a low impedance ($R_{ON} \approx$ milliohms).

**Region III — Reverse Blocking**
- $V_{AK}$ is negative (cathode positive with respect to anode).
- Junctions $J_1$ and $J_3$ are reverse-biased; $J_2$ is forward-biased.
- Only a tiny reverse leakage current ($I_R \approx \mu$A) flows.
- This continues until $V_{AK} = V_{BR}$ (Reverse Breakdown Voltage), after which avalanche breakdown occurs.

**Region IV — Reverse Breakdown**
- Avalanche of $J_1$ and $J_3$ — destructive if current is not limited.

### 2.4 Dynamic Characteristics — The Switching Transients

Dynamic characteristics describe the SCR's behavior during **transient switching** events. They are crucial for high-frequency applications like choppers and inverters.

#### 2.4.1 Turn-ON Dynamic Characteristic

The turn-ON process has three sub-intervals:

**Delay Time ($t_d$)**:
- From the instant 90% of $V_G$ is applied to the instant anode current reaches 10% of its final value.
- During $t_d$, the device transitions from blocking to initial conduction; charge carriers must build up at junctions.

**Rise Time ($t_r$)**:
- From 10% to 90% of final anode current.
- Rapid regenerative action; $J_2$ changes bias; conduction spreads across the die.
- **Critical interval** because localized heating occurs if $di/dt$ is excessive.

**Spread Time ($t_s$)**:
- From 90% of $I_A$ to the point where dynamic voltage drop equals static $V_T$.
- Conduction area expands to the full die cross-section.

$$\boxed{t_{on} = t_d + t_r + t_s}$$

Typical values for line-frequency SCRs: $t_d \approx 1$ to $2 \;\mu s$, $t_r \approx 1$ to $2 \;\mu s$, $t_{on}$ total $\approx 5$ to $10 \;\mu s$.

#### 2.4.2 Turn-OFF Dynamic Characteristic

For an SCR to regain forward blocking capability, the anode current must be reduced below $I_H$ AND sufficient time must elapse for **excess minority carriers** to be removed from the four layers.

**Reverse Recovery Time ($t_{rr}$)**:
- From the instant anode current becomes zero to when reverse current decays to 25% of its peak reverse value.
- During this time, stored charges in the junctions are being swept out.

**Gate Recovery Time ($t_{gr}$)**:
- The additional time after $t_{rr}$ required for the SCR to regain forward voltage blocking capability.
- The junction $J_2$ must re-establish its reverse-biased depletion region.

**Total Turn-OFF Time ($t_q$)**:
$$\boxed{t_q = t_{rr} + t_{gr}}$$

For typical phase-control SCRs, $t_q \approx 30$ to $100 \;\mu s$. This is the principal limitation preventing SCRs from being used in very high-frequency inverters (where GTOs, IGBTs, and MOSFETs dominate).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse $t_q$ with $t_{rr}$. Remember: $t_q$ is the **circuit-commutated turn-off time**, while $t_{rr}$ is only the reverse recovery portion. Apply forward voltage to the SCR only after $t_q$ has elapsed, otherwise the device will falsely retrigger.

### 2.5 Methods of Triggering (Turn-ON Methods)

| Method | Mechanism | Application |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Forward Voltage Triggering** | $V_{AK} \geq V_{BO}$ | Emergency / over-voltage protection (unwanted) |
| **Gate Triggering** | Positive $I_G$ at gate | **Standard method** for all SCR applications |
| **$dv/dt$ Triggering** | Rapid rise of forward voltage charges $J_2$ capacitance | Unwanted in most cases; suppressed by snubbers |
| **Light Triggering (LASCR)** | Photon-induced carrier generation | HVDC, high-voltage applications |
| **Temperature Triggering** | Increased leakage lowers $V_{BO}$ | Generally unwanted; thermal runaway risk |

### 2.6 Methods of Commutation (Turn-OFF Methods)

Turn-off can only be achieved by reducing anode current below $I_H$. The five classical commutation classes are:

**Class A — Load Commutation (Resonant)**
- The load itself is part of an LC resonant network that naturally forces the current to zero.
- Used in **resonant inverters** and **class D choppers**.

**Class B — Resonant-Pulse Commutation**
- An auxiliary LC circuit momentarily diverts current away from the SCR via a pulse transformer or saturable reactor.

**Class C — Complementary Commutation**
- A complementary fully-controlled switch (BJT/MOSFET/IGBT) connected in series forces the SCR current to zero.
- Used in modern forced-commutated choppers.

**Class D — Impulse Commutation**
- A charged capacitor is discharged through the SCR in the reverse direction via a saturable reactor, reverse-biasing it momentarily.

**Class E — External-Pulse Commutation**
- An auxiliary SCR (thyristor pair) is used to momentarily reverse-bias the main SCR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In AC circuits (line-commutated rectifiers), the natural reversal of AC voltage at every half-cycle naturally reduces $I_A$ below $I_H$. This is called **natural commutation** and requires no extra circuitry — a key reason SCRs dominate AC-DC converters.

### 2.7 SCR Ratings — KTU High-Yield Reference Table

| Rating Symbol | Parameter | Definition | Typical Range |
|---------------|-----------|------------|----------------|
| $V_{DRM}$ | Repetitive Peak Forward Voltage | Max forward blocking voltage, repetitive | 200 V to 7 kV |
| $V_{RRM}$ | Repetitive Peak Reverse Voltage | Max reverse blocking voltage, repetitive | 200 V to 7 kV |
| $V_{DSM}$ | Non-repetitive Peak Forward Voltage | Single-shot forward over-voltage | $V_{DRM} + 100V$ |
| $I_{T(AV)}$ | Average On-state Current | Time-averaged anode current | 10 A to 3000 A |
| $I_{T(RMS)}$ | RMS On-state Current | RMS value of anode current | 1.57 × $I_{T(AV)}$ |
| $I_{TSM}$ | Surge Current | Non-repetitive peak current for one cycle | 5× to 10× $I_{T(AV)}$ |
| $I^2t$ | Current Squared-Time Rating | Fusing capability | Used for fuse co-ordination |
| $dv/dt$ | Critical Rate of Rise of Voltage | Max permissible $dV/dt$ without false triggering | 50 to 1000 V/μs |
| $di/dt$ | Critical Rate of Rise of Current | Max permissible $dI/dt$ at turn-on | 50 to 500 A/μs |
| $T_j$ | Junction Temperature | Operating junction temperature range | $-40°C$ to $125°C$ |
| $R_{th(j-c)}$ | Thermal Resistance | Junction-to-case thermal resistance | 0.02 to 1 °C/W |
| $P_{GM}$ | Peak Gate Power Dissipation | Max transient gate power | 5 to 20 W |
| $P_{G(AV)}$ | Average Gate Power Dissipation | Continuous gate power | 0.5 to 2 W |

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ | Latch-up condition for SCR | dimensionless |
| $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$ | Anode current as function of gate current | A |
| $I_L$ | Latching current — minimum $I_A$ to sustain ON after $I_G$ removed | A |
| $I_H$ | Holding current — minimum $I_A$ to remain in ON state | A |
| $V_{T(on)} \approx V_{K1} + V_{K2} + V_{K3}$ | Total on-state voltage drop (sum of junction drops) | V |
| $t_{on} = t_d + t_r + t_s$ | Total turn-on time | s |
| $t_q = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ | Total turn-off (circuit-commutated) time | s |
| $P_{loss} = V_T \cdot I_{T(AV)} + R_T \cdot I_{T(RMS)}^2$ | Total conduction power loss | W |
| $\Delta T = P_{loss} \cdot R_{th(j-a)}$ | Junction temperature rise | °C |
| $R_{th(j-a)} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)}$ | Total thermal resistance (junction to ambient) | °C/W |
| $P_{G(AV)} \leq \dfrac{V_{GG}^2}{R_G}$ | Gate average power dissipation limit | W |
| $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ and $V_G \geq V_{GT}$ | Gate drive conditions for triggering | A, V |
| $t_{pulse} \geq t_{on}$ | Gate pulse duration must exceed turn-on time | s |
| $\dfrac{dv}{dt} \leq \left(\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right)_{crit}$ | Critical rate of rise of forward voltage limit | V/μs |
| $\dfrac{di}{dt} \leq \left(\dfrac{di}{dt}\right)_{crit}$ | Critical rate of rise of anode current limit | A/μs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:**
> - $I_L$ > $I_H$ (always). $I_L$ is the minimum current needed during the *application* of the gate pulse to latch the device; $I_H$ is the minimum current needed to *sustain* conduction after the gate pulse is removed.

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SCR's combination of high voltage, high current, low conduction loss, and natural commutation capability in AC circuits makes it the **workhorse of HVDC transmission, DC motor drives, controlled rectifiers, cyclo-converters, AC voltage regulators (light dimmers), battery chargers, and welding power supplies**. Modern HVDC valves use stacks of 100+ series-connected SCRs rated at 8.5 kV / 4000 A each.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Two-Transistor Model Anode Current Equation

**Given:** The SCR is modeled as PNP (T₁) and NPN (T₂) transistors. The emitter currents are $I_{E1}$ (anode) and $I_{E2}$ (cathode, but only the portion not going to gate). The gate current $I_G$ is the base current of T₂.

**Step 1: Define currents at each node.**

Let:
- $I_{A}$ = Anode current
- $I_{K}$ = Cathode current
- $I_{G}$ = Gate current
- $\alpha_1$ = Common-base current gain of T₁ (PNP)
- $\alpha_2$ = Common-base current gain of T₂ (NPN)

By KCL at the cathode node:
$$I_{K} = I_{A} + I_{G}$$

**Step 2: Express emitter currents in terms of common-base gains.**

The collector current of a transistor in CB configuration equals $\alpha \cdot I_E$. The portion of T₁'s collector that flows to T₂'s base is $I_{C1}$. Note that the gate terminal diverts the base current of T₂.

$$I_{C1} = \alpha_1 \cdot I_{E1} = \alpha_1 \cdot I_{A}$$

$$I_{C2} = \alpha_2 \cdot I_{E2} = \alpha_2 \cdot I_{K}$$

**Step 3: Apply KCL at the middle P-layer (T₁ base = T₂ collector).**

The current entering the middle P-layer from T₁'s collector equals the current leaving it as T₂'s collector, minus any recombination:
$$I_{C1} + I_{C2} + I_{CO} = I_{K}$$

where $I_{CO}$ is the reverse leakage current of the reverse-biased middle junction $J_2$. Combining the collector current expressions:

$$\alpha_1 \cdot I_{A} + \alpha_2 \cdot I_{K} + I_{CO} = I_{K}$$

**Step 4: Substitute $I_{K} = I_{A} + I_{G}$ and simplify.**

$$\alpha_1 \cdot I_{A} + \alpha_2 \cdot (I_{A} + I_{G}) + I_{CO} = I_{A} + I_{G}$$

$$\alpha_1 \cdot I_{A} + \alpha_2 \cdot I_{A} + \alpha_2 \cdot I_{G} + I_{CO} = I_{A} + I_{G}$$

Rearranging terms to isolate $I_A$:

$$I_A \cdot (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 - 1) = I_G \cdot (1 - \alpha_2) - I_{CO}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $I_A$.**

$$\boxed{I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 \cdot I_G - I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}}$$

> For the case where leakage is negligible ($I_{CO} \approx 0$):
> $$I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 \cdot I_G}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

**Step 6: Interpret the latch-up condition.**

As $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator $\to 0$, and $I_A \to \infty$ (mathematically — practically, it latches to the load-limited value). Therefore:

$$\boxed{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1 \quad \text{is the latch-up threshold.}}$$

When this condition is met, even with $I_G = 0$, the device remains ON (regenerative self-sustaining feedback). This derivation is **gold** for KTU 14-mark problems.

### 3.2 Derivation: Heat Sink Sizing for an SCR

**Given:** An SCR carries an average current of $I_{T(AV)} = 100 \; A$. On-state voltage drop $V_T = 1.5 \; V$, dynamic resistance $R_T = 0.005 \; \Omega$, RMS current $I_{T(RMS)} = 157 \; A$, ambient temperature $T_a = 40°C$, junction-to-case thermal resistance $R_{th(j-c)} = 0.1 \; °C/W$, case-to-sink resistance $R_{th(c-s)} = 0.05 \; °C/W$. Max junction temperature $T_{j(max)} = 125°C$. Compute required sink-to-ambient thermal resistance $R_{th(s-a)}$.

**Step 1: Compute total conduction power loss.**
$$P_{loss} = V_T \cdot I_{T(AV)} + R_T \cdot I_{T(RMS)}^2$$
$$P_{loss} = 1.5 \times 100 + 0.005 \times (157)^2$$
$$P_{loss} = 150 + 0.005 \times 24649$$
$$P_{loss} = 150 + 123.25 = 273.25 \; W$$

**Step 2: Compute maximum allowable total thermal resistance.**
$$R_{th(j-a),max} = \dfrac{T_{j,max} - T_a}{P_{loss}}$$
$$R_{th(j-a),max} = \dfrac{125 - 40}{273.25} = \dfrac{85}{273.25}$$
$$R_{th(j-a),max} = 0.311 \; °C/W$$

**Step 3: Subtract known thermal resistances.**
$$R_{th(j-a)} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)}$$
$$0.311 = 0.1 + 0.05 + R_{th(s-a)}$$
$$R_{th(s-a)} = 0.311 - 0.15$$
$$\boxed{R_{th(s-a)} \leq 0.161 \; °C/W}$$

**Step 4: Interpret.** A heat sink with thermal resistance ≤ 0.161 °C/W must be selected. This typically corresponds to a large aluminum extruded heat sink with forced-air cooling or water cooling.

### 3.3 Derivation: Snubber Capacitor Sizing for $dv/dt$ Protection

**Given:** A phase-controlled SCR has a critical $dv/dt$ rating of $200 \; V/\mu s$. The supply peak voltage is $V_s = 325 \; V$ (230 V RMS). Compute the minimum snubber capacitance.

**Step 1: Recall the $dv/dt$ formula for an RC snubber.**

For a snubber consisting of $R_S$ and $C_S$ across the SCR, the equivalent circuit during turn-OFF creates an RC discharge of the supply voltage. The rate of rise of voltage across the SCR is given by:

$$\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{0.632 \cdot V_s}{\tau} = \dfrac{0.632 \cdot V_s}{R_S \cdot C_S}$$

The factor 0.632 comes from the exponential charge $V(t) = V_s(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ at $t = \tau$, which gives $V = 0.632 V_s$.

**Step 2: Solve for $C_S$ given a chosen $R_S$.**

Choosing $R_S = 50 \; \Omega$ as a typical snubber resistor value:
$$C_S = \dfrac{0.632 \cdot V_s}{R_S \cdot (dv/dt)_{crit}}$$
$$C_S = \dfrac{0.632 \times 325}{50 \times 200 \times 10^{6}}$$
$$C_S = \dfrac{205.4}{10^{10}}$$
$$\boxed{C_S \approx 20.5 \; nF}$$

> **KTU Tip:** In exam questions, if $R_S$ is not given, you may assume $R_S = \dfrac{V_s}{I_T}$ (matched load condition) or use standard values like 10 Ω, 50 Ω, 100 Ω.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SCR vs Other Power Switches

| Parameter | SCR | BJT | MOSFET | IGBT | GTO |
|-----------|-----|-----|--------|------|-----|
| Control | Semi-controlled | Fully-controlled | Fully-controlled | Fully-controlled | Fully-controlled |
| Trigger | Gate pulse | Continuous $I_B$ | Continuous $V_{GS}$ | Continuous $V_{GE}$ | Gate pulse (high) |
| Turn-off | Commutation needed | $I_B = 0$ | $V_{GS} = 0$ | $V_{GE} = 0$ | Negative gate pulse |
| $V_{rating}$ (max) | ~7 kV | ~1.5 kV | ~1 kV | ~6.5 kV | ~6 kV |
| $I_{rating}$ (max) | ~5000 A | ~800 A | ~100 A | ~1500 A | ~3000 A |
| Switching freq | < 1 kHz | < 5 kHz | < 1 MHz | < 50 kHz | < 2 kHz |
| On-state drop | 1.5–2.5 V | 1–2 V | High (RDS_on) | 2–3 V | 2–3 V |
| Conduction loss | Lowest | Medium | High (high-current) | Medium | Low |
| Application | AC-DC rectifiers, HVDC | Old DC drives | SMPS, low-power | Modern drives | Traction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Four-Layer PNPN Physical Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCR_DEVICE["SCR Physical Cross-Section (4-Layer PNPN)"]
        A["Layer 1: P+ (p-doped, heavily)"]:::pLayer
        J1["Junction J1"]:::junction
        B["Layer 2: N- (lightly doped, drift region)"]:::nLayer
        J2["Junction J2 (Middle - Reverse Biased)"]:::junction
        C["Layer 3: P (p-base, lightly doped)"]:::pLayer
        J3["Junction J3"]:::junction
        D["Layer 4: N+ (heavily doped, cathode)"]:::nLayer
    end

    ANODE["Terminal A (Anode)"]:::terminal
    GATE["Terminal G (Gate)"]:::terminal
    CATHODE["Terminal K (Cathode)"]:::terminal

    ANODE -.-> A
    A --- J1
    J1 --- B
    B --- J2
    J2 --- C
    C --- J3
    J3 --- D
    GATE -.-> C
    CATHODE -.-> D

    classDef pLayer fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nLayer fill:#b0d8ff,stroke:#1e5aa8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef junction fill:#ff6347,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef terminal fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Two-Transistor Equivalent Model (Regenerative Pair)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1["Transistor T1 - PNP"]
        E1["Emitter"]:::pnp
        B1["Base"]:::pnp
        C1["Collector"]:::pnp
    end

    subgraph T2["Transistor T2 - NPN"]
        E2["Emitter"]:::npn
        B2["Base"]:::npn
        C2["Collector"]:::npn
    end

    A["Anode A"]:::terminal --> E1
    C1 -- "alpha1 x IE1" --> B2
    B1 -- "shared P-layer" --- C2
    E2 --> K["Cathode K"]:::terminal
    C2 -- "IC2 = alpha2 x IK" --> C1
    B2 --> G["Gate G"]:::terminal
    G -- "IG injects electrons into P-base" --> B2

    classDef pnp fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#c71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef npn fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#fffacd,stroke:#8b8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> The internal regenerative loop is: $I_{C1}$ (from PNP) feeds $I_{B2}$ (NPN base) → produces $I_{C2}$ → feeds back as $I_{B1}$ (PNP base) → produces more $I_{C1}$.

### 4.3 Static V-I Characteristic Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHART["Static V-I Characteristics of SCR"]
        subgraph AXES["Axes"]
            Y["IA (Anode Current) - Logarithmic scale"]:::axis
            X["VAK (Anode-Cathode Voltage)"]:::axis
        end

        subgraph REGIONS["Operating Regions"]
            R1["Region III: REVERSE BLOCKING\n- VAK negative, small IR leaks\n- J1 and J3 reverse biased"]:::revBlock
            R2["Region IV: REVERSE BREAKDOWN\n- At VBR, avalanche occurs\n- Destructive if not limited"]:::breakdown
            R3["Region I: FORWARD BLOCKING\n- VAK positive, small IF leak\n- J2 reverse biased, supports VAK"]:::fwdBlock
            R4["Region II: FORWARD CONDUCTION\n- After triggering, V drops to VT ~1.5V\n- IA limited only by load"]:::fwdCond
        end

        X --> R3
        X --> R4
        X --> R1
        X --> R2
        R3 -. "VBO = Breakover" .-> R4
        R3 -. "IG reduces VBO" .-> R4
    end

    classDef fwdBlock fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fwdCond fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#008000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef revBlock fill:#e6f0ff,stroke:#1e5aa8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef breakdown fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef axis fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

```mermaid
        VG["Waveform 1: VG - Gate Pulse\nApplied at t=0, magnitude ~3V to 10V"]:::wf
        IA["Waveform 2: IA - Anode Current\nBegins rising after td, full at ton"]:::wf
        VAK["Waveform 3: VAK - Voltage\nFalls from VAK(block) to VT after tON"]:::wf

    TD --> TR
    TR --> TS
    TS --> TON

    IA -.inverse.-> VAK
```

```mermaid
        TGR["Interval tgr: Gate Recovery\nJ2 re-establishes depletion region"]:::timeInt
```

### 4.7 Isolated Gate Drive Circuits - Topology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ISOLATED_DRIVES["Isolated Gate Drive Topologies"]
        OPT["OPTOCOUPLER-BASED ISOLATED DRIVE\n- Uses MOC3021 / MOC3052\n- LED on input side, phototransistor on output\n- Galvanic isolation ~5kV\n- Slow: tPLH ~ 10us\n- Common in low-power TRIAC/SCR dimmers"]:::opt
        PT["PULSE TRANSFORMER-BASED ISOLATED DRIVE\n- One or two primary pulses per trigger\n- Saturable core prevents saturation\n- Isolation ~2.5kV\n- Fast: pulse rise ~1us\n- Used in medium-power SCR modules"]:::pt
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Why is the SCR called a semi-controlled switch? Explain the difference between latching current ($I_L$) and holding current ($I_H$)."**

**Model Answer:**
The SCR is called a *semi-controlled switch* because the gate terminal can only **turn it ON** but cannot turn it OFF. Once triggered, the SCR remains conducting until the anode current is reduced below the holding current by external means (natural or forced commutation).

- **Latching Current ($I_L$)**: The minimum anode current that must flow through the SCR at the moment the gate pulse is removed to ensure the device stays in the ON state. Typical values are higher than $I_H$.
- **Holding Current ($I_H$)**: The minimum anode current required to *maintain* the ON state once the SCR is already conducting. Typical values are lower than $I_L$.

The relationship is **always $I_L > I_H$**. To turn off the SCR, anode current must be brought below $I_H$.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Explain the significance of $dv/dt$ and $di/dt$ ratings of an SCR."**

**Model Answer:**
- **$dv/dt$ (Critical Rate of Rise of Forward Voltage)**: This is the maximum permissible rate at which the forward anode-to-cathode voltage can rise without falsely triggering the SCR. A rapid $dv/dt$ charges the junction capacitance of $J_2$, producing a displacement current $\left(I = C \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}\right)$ that acts like a gate current and may cause spurious turn-on. The $dv/dt$ rating is therefore a protection parameter; if exceeded, a snubber circuit must be used.

- **$di/dt$ (Critical Rate of Rise of On-State Current)**: This is the maximum permissible rate at which the anode current can rise immediately after turn-on. The initial conduction area is small (conduction spreads from a single point near the gate), so a high $di/dt$ causes localized heating and "hot spots" that can destroy the device. An inductor in series limits $di/dt$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of the two-transistor model of an SCR, derive the anode current equation $I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G - I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$. State and explain the condition for latch-up. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A phase-controlled SCR operates in a resistive load circuit with $V_s = 230\;V$ RMS, $50\;Hz$. The firing angle is $\alpha = 60°$. Compute:
  (i) Average output voltage $V_o$ and average load current $I_o$ for $R_L = 10 \;\Omega$.
  (ii) The RMS output voltage $V_{or}$ and RMS current $I_{or}$.
  (iii) The $dv/dt$ at the point of turn-on if a snubber capacitor of $0.1\;\mu F$ is used in parallel with $R_S = 47 \;\Omega$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Two-transistor model derivation (refer to Section 3.1, Steps 1–5):
- Model SCR as PNP (T₁) and NPN (T₂) interconnected.
- Apply KCL: $I_K = I_A + I_G$
- Express collectors: $I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A$, $I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_K$
- Combine: $\alpha_1 I_A + \alpha_2 (I_A + I_G) + I_{CO} = I_A + I_G$
- Solve for $I_A$: $\quad I_A = \dfrac{\alpha_2 I_G - I_{CO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$

**Latch-up condition**: As $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \to 1$, the denominator approaches zero, and anode current rises sharply even with $I_G = 0$. Thus $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ defines sustained conduction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Average output voltage and current**:
For a half-wave controlled rectifier with R load:
$$V_o = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$$
where $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27\;V$.

$$V_o = \dfrac{325.27}{2\pi}(1 + \cos 60°) = \dfrac{325.27}{2\pi}(1 + 0.5)$$
$$V_o = \dfrac{325.27 \times 1.5}{6.2832} = \dfrac{487.91}{6.2832}$$
$$\boxed{V_o \approx 77.66\;V}$$

$$I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R_L} = \dfrac{77.66}{10}$$
$$\boxed{I_o \approx 7.77\;A}$$

**(ii) RMS values**:
$$V_{or} = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4\pi}(2\pi - 2\alpha + \sin 2\alpha)}$$

Compute $\sin 2\alpha = \sin 120° = 0.866$ and $2\pi - 2\alpha = 6.2832 - 2.094 = 4.189$ rad.

$$V_{or} = 325.27 \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4\pi}(4.189 + 0.866)}$$
$$V_{or} = 325.27 \sqrt{\dfrac{5.055}{12.566}}$$
$$V_{or} = 325.27 \sqrt{0.4023}$$
$$V_{or} = 325.27 \times 0.6343$$
$$\boxed{V_{or} \approx 206.3\;V}$$

$$I_{or} = \dfrac{V_{or}}{R_L} = \dfrac{206.3}{10}$$
$$\boxed{I_{or} \approx 20.63\;A}$$

**(iii) $dv/dt$ with snubber**:
$$\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{0.632 \cdot V_m}{R_S C_S} = \dfrac{0.632 \times 325.27}{47 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{205.57}{4.7 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$\boxed{\dfrac{dv}{dt} \approx 43.74\;V/\mu s}$$

If the SCR's rated $dv/dt$ is, e.g., $100 \;V/\mu s$, then the snubber is appropriate. If rated $dv/dt = 50\;V/\mu s$, a larger $C_S$ is required. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Describe with neat diagrams the static V-I characteristics of an SCR. Mark the forward blocking, forward conduction, reverse blocking, and reverse breakdown regions. Discuss the effect of gate current on the forward breakover voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the dynamic turn-on and turn-off characteristics of an SCR with appropriate waveforms. Define the terms $t_d$, $t_r$, $t_s$, $t_{rr}$, $t_{gr}$, and $t_q$. An SCR has $t_d = 1\;\mu s$, $t_r = 2\;\mu s$, $t_s = 3\;\mu s$, $t_{rr} = 25\;\mu s$, and $t_{gr} = 15\;\mu s$. Compute the maximum operating frequency if the conduction angle is $180°$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Static V-I characteristics** (refer to Section 2.3 and Diagram 4.3):

**Forward Blocking Region (Region I)**:
- $V_{AK}$ is positive and below $V_{BO}$.
- Only a small forward leakage current $I_L$ flows (in the mA range).
- The middle junction $J_2$ is reverse-biased and supports the entire applied voltage.
- The SCR is in a high-impedance OFF state.

**Forward Conduction Region (Region II)**:
- When $V_{AK} \geq V_{BO}$ (without gate) or when gate current is applied, the device switches ON.
- The voltage drops to a low value ($V_T \approx 1.5$ V for typical SCRs).
- The current is limited only by the load resistance.
- $J_2$ becomes forward-biased; all three junctions are forward-biased.

**Reverse Blocking Region (Region III)**:
- $V_{AK}$ is negative; $J_1$ and $J_3$ are reverse-biased.
- Only a small reverse leakage current $I_R$ flows (in the $\mu$A range).

**Reverse Breakdown Region (Region IV)**:
- At $V_{AK} = V_{BR}$, avalanche breakdown of $J_1$ and $J_3$ occurs.
- This is destructive unless current is externally limited.

**Effect of Gate Current on $V_{BO}$**:
Increasing $I_G$ reduces $V_{BO}$ significantly. With $I_G = 0$, $V_{BO}$ is at its maximum. With $I_G = I_{GT}$ (gate trigger current), $V_{BO}$ drops to a low value (often just above the supply voltage). This is why gate-controlled SCRs can be turned ON at any forward voltage below the natural $V_{BO}$.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b) Dynamic Characteristics & Frequency Calculation** (refer to Section 2.4 and Diagrams 4.5, 4.6):

**Turn-ON Process**:
- **Delay Time $t_d$**: Time from 90% of $V_G$ to 10% of $I_A$.
- **Rise Time $t_r$**: Time from 10% to 90% of $I_A$.
- **Spread Time $t_s$**: Time from 90% of $I_A$ to steady-state conduction.
- Total: $t_{on} = t_d + t_r + t_s$

**Turn-OFF Process**:
- **Reverse Recovery Time $t_{rr}$**: From $I_A = 0$ to when reverse current decays to 25% of peak.
- **Gate Recovery Time $t_{gr}$**: Additional time after $t_{rr}$ for $J_2$ to regain reverse voltage blocking capability.
- **Total Circuit-Commutated Turn-off Time**: $t_q = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$.

**Numerical Computation**:

Given:
$$t_d = 1\;\mu s, \quad t_r = 2\;\mu s, \quad t_s = 3\;\mu s$$
$$t_{rr} = 25\;\mu s, \quad t_{gr} = 15\;\mu s$$

Total turn-on time:
$$t_{on} = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6\;\mu s$$

Total turn-off time:
$$t_q = t_{rr} + t_{gr} = 25 + 15 = 40\;\mu s$$

**Maximum Operating Frequency**:
The total "off period" must accommodate $t_q$. In one cycle:
$$T_{total} = t_{on} + t_{conduction} + t_q$$

Given conduction angle $= 180°$, conduction time $t_{cond} = T/2$.

For minimum cycle time:
$$T_{min} = t_{on} + \dfrac{T_{min}}{2} + t_q$$
$$\dfrac{T_{min}}{2} = t_{on} + t_q = 6 + 40 = 46\;\mu s$$
$$T_{min} = 92\;\mu s$$
$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{min}} = \dfrac{1}{92 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 10.87\;kHz}$$

**(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in SCR Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $I_L$ and $I_H$**: Many students write the wrong inequality. Always state: $I_L > I_H$, with $I_L$ being the value during the gate pulse and $I_H$ being the value after gate pulse removal.
> 2. **Wrong formula for $V_o$ in half-wave rectifier**: Use $V_o = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ for R-load, NOT the full-wave formula unless explicitly a full-wave bridge.
> 3. **Forgetting units in heat-sink calculation**: $R_{th}$ is in °C/W, not W/°C. Mixing up causes dimensional errors and marks lost.
> 4. **Missing the $0.632$ factor in $dv/dt$ snubber calculation**: The exponential charge at $t = \tau$ gives $0.632 V_s$, not $V_s$.
> 5. **Skipping the latch-up condition**: In 7-mark derivations, never omit the final interpretation of $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 1$ as the latch-up threshold.
> 6. **Forgetting the $V_{AK}$ polarity**: When the SCR is reverse-biased, $J_1$ AND $J_3$ are reverse-biased (not just one). Many students mark $J_1$ alone.
> 7. **Static vs dynamic characteristics confusion**: Static is V-I in DC steady state; dynamic is time-domain during switching. Examiners check both clearly delineated.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **SCR = 4 layers (PNPN), 3 junctions ($J_1$, $J_2$, $J_3$), 3 terminals (A, K, G)**.
- **Semi-controlled**: Gate turns ON, but turn-off needs commutation (anode current < $I_H$).
- **Two-transistor model**: PNP (T₁) + NPN (T₂) interconnected — regenerative positive feedback gives latching.
- **Latch-up condition**: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ (numerator/denominator of $I_A$ equation).
- **Static V-I characteristic**: Four regions — forward blocking, forward conduction, reverse blocking, reverse breakdown.
- **Middle junction $J_2$ is the "switching" junction**: Reverse-biased in blocking, forward-biased in conduction.
- **Forward breakover voltage $V_{BO}$ decreases as $I_G$ increases**.
- **Dynamic turn-ON**: $t_{on} = t_d + t_r + t_s$ (delay + rise + spread).
- **Dynamic turn-OFF**: $t_q = t_{rr} + t_{gr}$ (reverse recovery + gate recovery).
- **Ratings to remember**: $V_{DRM}$, $V_{RRM}$, $I_{T(AV)}$, $I_{T(RMS)} = 1.57 \cdot I_{T(AV)}$, $I_{TSM}$, $dv/dt$, $di/dt$, $T_j$, $R_{th}$.
- **Always $I_L > I_H$**.
- **Gate trigger requirements**: $I_G \geq I_{GT}$ AND $V_G \geq V_{GT}$ simultaneously.
- **Gate pulse width $t_p \geq t_{on}$** (typically 10–50 μs for line-frequency SCRs; for chopper-grade SCRs, 100–200 μs).
- **Critical $dv/dt$ protection**: Snubber $R_S$–$C_S$ across SCR. $C_S = \dfrac{0.632 V_s}{R_S (dv/dt)_{crit}}$.
- **Critical $di/dt$ protection**: Series inductor $L_S$ with SCR.
- **Gate drive circuits**: Resistive (simple), RC (phase-shifted), pulse transformer (isolated, high-power), optocoupler (MOC series for low power), high-CMR optocouplers (HCPL-3120) for IGBT-grade SCRs.
- **Isolation purposes**: Safety, level shifting (gate drive at different potential from control), noise immunity, ground loop elimination.
- **Gate synchronization**: Zero-crossing detector (ZCD) + PLL aligns gate pulse with line voltage for phase-controlled rectifiers.
- **Heat sink equation**: $T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th(j-a)}$, where $R_{th(j-a)} = R_{th(j-c)} + R_{th(c-s)} + R_{th(s-a)}$.
- **Conduction loss**: $P_{loss} = V_T I_{T(AV)} + R_T I_{T(RMS)}^2$.
- **Major application domains**: Phase-controlled rectifiers, cyclo-converters, AC voltage controllers, HVDC valves, DC drives, battery chargers, welding rectifiers, solid-state relays (using TRIACs which are essentially dual SCRs).
- **Comparison memory aid**: SCR dominates at very high voltage & current (>1 kV, >1 kA), low-frequency (<1 kHz) applications — it is the "high-power slow-switch" device.

---

> **End of Module-I Notes on Semi-controlled Switch: SCR (Thyristor)**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | Course PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives*

topics.title: Fully-controlled Switches: MOSFETs and IGBTs - Symbol, Structure, Characteristics (Static and Dynamic), Device Ratings, Gate Drive Requirements, Typical Gate Drive Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives
> - **Module:** Module-I
> - **Topic:** Fully-controlled Switches: MOSFETs and IGBTs - Symbol, Structure, Characteristics (Static and Dynamic), Device Ratings, Gate Drive Requirements, Typical Gate Drive Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Fully-Controlled Switch"?

In Power Electronics, a **fully-controlled switch** is a semiconductor device whose **turn-on AND turn-off** instants are both controlled by a low-power control signal (typically a voltage applied to a "gate" or "base" terminal). This is in contrast to *semi-controlled* devices like the **Thyristor (SCR)**, which can be turned on by a gate pulse but can only be turned off by the natural commutation of the main circuit (i.e., when the anode current falls below the holding current).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** In the 2024 scheme, MOSFETs and IGBTs are the two pillar devices for *self-commutated* (forced-commutated) switch-mode power conversion. They are the workhorses of modern SMPS, motor drives, inverters, and UPS systems.

### 1.2 The MOSFET — Formal Definition

The **Power MOSFET** (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor) used in power electronics is almost universally an **n-channel enhancement-mode (normally-OFF) vertical DMOS (Double-diffused MOS)** device such as the International Rectifier HEXFET, Siemens SIPMOS, or Toshiba TOYOPAC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** A Power MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled, unipolar, majority-carrier** device in which the drain current $i_D$ is controlled by the gate-to-source voltage $v_{GS}$ applied across a metal-oxide (SiO₂) insulating layer. Because conduction is by *electrons only* (no minority-carrier storage), it is the **fastest** of the silicon power switches.

### 1.3 The IGBT — Formal Definition

The **Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistor (IGBT)** is a hybrid device that combines the **voltage-controlled, high-input-impedance gate of a MOSFET** with the **high-current, low-saturation-voltage conduction capability of a bipolar junction transistor (BJT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** An IGBT is a **three-terminal, voltage-controlled, bipolar (conducting)** power semiconductor having a MOS gate for input and a PNP transistor output stage. It is the dominant device in the **medium-power (600 V – 6.6 kV), medium-frequency (1 – 100 kHz)** range of modern drives.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline carrying a large flow.

*   **The MOSFET** is like a **solenoid-operated fast valve** with a rubber diaphragm. Apply a voltage to the solenoid (gate) and the valve opens *instantly* in a few nanoseconds. Release the voltage and it snaps shut just as fast. It is super-fast, but when wide open, the water still has to squeeze through the diaphragm, causing a pressure drop (this pressure drop is analogous to the MOSFET's **on-state resistance $R_{DS(on)}$**).

*   **The IGBT** is like a **hydraulic servo valve** — still controlled by a small electrical signal at the solenoid, but once open, a *pilot flow* opens a much larger main valve. This means it carries huge currents with a *very small voltage drop* (about **1.5 – 2.5 V**), but because the pilot stage involves fluid inertia, it cannot close as instantly as the MOSFET. There is a small "trailing dribble" — this is the famous IGBT **tail current** at turn-off.

### 1.5 Why These Two Devices? — The Power-Device Landscape

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Always remember the device-selection "sweet spot" diagram. The cross-over frequency between **MOSFET and IGBT** in a 600 V class device is approximately **~10 kHz – 1 MHz depending on current**. Above that frequency, MOSFETs win; below it, IGBTs win because of lower conduction loss.

| Device | Voltage Class | Frequency Range | Conduction Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Power BJT** | 100 V – 1200 V | < 5 kHz | Bipolar (current-controlled) |
| **Power MOSFET** | 20 V – 1000 V | 50 kHz – 1 MHz | Unipolar (majority carrier) |
| **IGBT** | 600 V – 6.6 kV | 1 kHz – 100 kHz | Bipolar output, MOS gate |
| **GTO / IGCT** | 1.2 kV – 10 kV | < 1 kHz | Thyristor-family, gate turn-off |
