## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Power MOSFET — Layer-by-Layer Structure

The vertical DMOS structure of an n-channel power MOSFET is built as follows (from top to bottom of the silicon die):

1.  **Source metallization** — the top metal contact.
2.  **n⁺ source region** — heavily doped, where electrons originate.
3.  **p-body (p-well)** — forms the channel; p⁺ body contact short-circuits the p-body to the source to prevent the parasitic **BJT** from turning on (a critical reliability feature).
4.  **n⁻ drift (epitaxial) layer** — the *thick* lightly-doped region that blocks the high OFF-state voltage. Its thickness and doping determine the **breakdown voltage $V_{(BR)DSS}$**.
5.  **n⁺ substrate / drain** — the bottom of the die, the drain contact.

When a positive $v_{GS} > V_{th}$ (threshold, typically **2 – 4 V**) is applied, an *inversion layer* (the channel) forms in the p-body just under the gate oxide. Electrons flow horizontally from n⁺ source, through this channel, then **vertically down** through the n⁻ drift layer to the drain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Vertical"?** The current flows *vertically* through the die (not laterally as in a signal-level MOSFET). This allows the cross-sectional area of the die to be made very large (tens of mm²) and the drift length to be scaled with the voltage rating, giving high current AND high voltage capability in a single chip.

### 2.2 MOSFET — Static (V–I) Characteristics

The static behaviour is described by **two families of curves**:

#### A. Transfer Characteristic: $i_D$ vs $v_{GS}$ (at constant $v_{DS}$)

For a power MOSFET operated in the **active / saturation region** (used as a switch, it is *either* in the ohmic region when fully ON *or* in cut-off when OFF, but the active region defines the boundary):

$$i_D = K_n \cdot (v_{GS} - V_{th})^2 \quad \text{(square-law, in saturation)}$$

The **forward transconductance** $g_m$ is:

$$g_m = \left.\frac{\partial i_D}{\partial v_{GS}}\right|_{Q-point} = 2 K_n (v_{GS} - V_{th})$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Favourite: A typical power MOSFET data sheet will give $g_m$ at $i_D = $ some rated value, and the transfer curve is plotted on a *log* scale to show the sub-threshold leakage and the sharp turn-on.

#### B. Output Characteristic: $i_D$ vs $v_{DS}$ (for several $v_{GS}$)

The output characteristic has three regions:

1.  **Cut-off region** — $v_{GS} < V_{th} \Rightarrow i_D \approx 0$.
2.  **Ohmic / Triode region** — For $v_{DS} < v_{GS} - V_{th}$, the device behaves like a **voltage-controlled resistor** with resistance $R_{DS(on)}$. This is the *fully-ON* state used in switching applications.
3.  **Active (Saturation) region** — For $v_{DS} > v_{GS} - V_{th}$, $i_D$ is nearly constant (current-source behaviour) and depends only on $v_{GS}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$R_{DS(on)}$ is THE figure of merit for a power MOSFET.** Modern low-voltage MOSFETs achieve $R_{DS(on)} < 1 \text{ m}\Omega$. Note that $R_{DS(on)}$ has a **strong positive temperature coefficient** (~+0.7 %/°C), which makes MOSFETs naturally easy to **parallel** — no current hogging.

### 2.3 MOSFET — Dynamic Characteristics

The MOSFET is a *capacitive* device from the control terminal's perspective. The key parasitic capacitances are:

*   $C_{GS}$ — gate-to-source (mostly the gate-oxide capacitance over the channel).
*   $C_{GD}$ — gate-to-drain ("Miller" capacitance). This is *non-linear*; it depends strongly on $v_{DS}$ because the depletion region under the gate overlap widens with voltage.
*   $C_{DS}$ — drain-to-source (the body-diode junction capacitance).

The data sheet normalizes these into:

$$C_{iss} = C_{GS} + C_{GD} \quad \text{(input capacitance)}$$

$$C_{oss} = C_{GD} + C_{DS} \quad \text{(output capacitance)}$$

$$C_{rss} = C_{GD} \quad \text{(reverse-transfer / Miller capacitance)}$$

#### The Turn-ON Transient — Four Distinct Intervals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite:** Be able to draw and label the **four phases of turn-on** (and the mirror-image four of turn-off). Marks are routinely given for this in Part B questions.

| Phase | Duration | What's happening | Equivalent $v_{GS}(t)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(1) Turn-on delay** $t_{d(on)}$ | $C_{iss}$ charges from 0 to $V_{th}$ | Drain current is still ≈ 0 | Linear rise to $V_{th}$ |
| **(2) Current rise** $t_{ri}$ | $C_{iss}$ charges from $V_{th}$ to $V_{GP}$ (Miller plateau start) | $i_D$ rises from 0 to load current $I_L$ | Linear rise to $V_{GP}$ |
| **(3) Voltage fall** $t_{fv}$ | $C_{GD}$ (Miller) discharges / charges | $v_{DS}$ falls from $V_{DD}$ to $R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_L$ | $v_{GS}$ flat at $V_{GP}$ (Miller plateau) |
| **(4) Over-shoot** $t_{on}$ | $C_{iss}$ charges from $V_{GP}$ to final $V_{GG}$ | $R_{DS(on)}$ reaches final value | Final rise to $V_{GG}$ |

Total turn-on time: $t_{on} = t_{d(on)} + t_{ri} + t_{fv}$

The **gate charge** $Q_g$ (in nC) is the integral of gate current over time. The data sheet's $Q_g$ is a much more useful switching-loss figure than the capacitances because the gate driver must *supply* this charge to switch the device.

### 2.4 The IGBT — Layer-by-Layer Structure

The IGBT is built on a **p⁺ substrate** (the *collector* side) instead of the MOSFET's n⁺ drain. The layer stack from top to bottom is:

1.  **Emitter metallization** (top).
2.  **n⁺ emitter region** + **p-body** (forming the MOS channel, exactly like a MOSFET).
3.  **n⁻ drift layer** (the voltage-blocking region).
4.  **p⁺ substrate / collector** (the bottom of the die).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical insight:** The p⁺ substrate injects **holes (minority carriers)** into the n⁻ drift layer during conduction. This *conductivity modulation* of the drift region is what gives the IGBT its low on-state voltage drop — the same trick that makes the thyristor a great conductor but the MOSFET a poor one for high voltages.

The IGBT can be thought of as a **pnp BJT driven by a MOSFET**, or equivalently as a MOSFET with a p⁺ "drain" that injects holes. The equivalent circuit has:
*   A MOSFET (input) feeding the base of a pnp BJT.
*   A parasitic **thyristor** (formed by the n⁺ emitter, p-body, n⁻ drift, p⁺ collector) that, if triggered, causes **latch-up** — a destructive failure mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **Latch-up is the #1 IGBT failure mode.** The IGBT is designed with a *shorted emitter* (n⁺ emitter shorted to p-body) precisely to *prevent* the parasitic thyristor from latching under normal $dv/dt$ and over-current conditions. Exceeding the **Short-Circuit Withstand Time $t_{SC}$** (typically 10 µs) can still cause latch-up.

### 2.5 IGBT — Static (V–I) Characteristics

The IGBT output characteristic looks like that of a **BJT in the active region**, with $i_C$ controlled by $v_{GE}$:

*   **Cut-off:** $v_{GE} < V_{GE(th)} \approx 4$–$6 \text{ V} \Rightarrow$ no collector current.
*   **Active region:** $i_C$ is approximately proportional to $(v_{GE} - V_{GE(th)})$ (more linear than a MOSFET, less linear than a BJT).
*   **Saturation region:** $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5$–$2.5 \text{ V}$ — this is the *ON-state* drop in switching applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **V_CE(sat) has a *positive* temperature coefficient** (like the MOSFET's $R_{DS(on)}$), making IGBTs easy to parallel. This is in contrast to the BJT's $V_{CE(sat)}$, which has a *negative* temperature coefficient.

### 2.6 IGBT — Dynamic Characteristics

The turn-ON transient of an IGBT is **essentially identical to that of a MOSFET** (same MOS-gate physics, same Miller plateau, same $Q_g$ concept).

The turn-OFF transient, however, is **fundamentally different** and is the IGBT's main drawback:

1.  **MOSFET part:** $v_{GE}$ falls rapidly. $i_C$ falls rapidly to about 90% of its value in a few hundred nanoseconds.
2.  **BJT part (TAIL CURRENT):** Because holes are stored in the n⁻ drift region, the collector current does **not fall to zero instantly**. It has a slowly-decaying **tail current** that can last from a few hundred ns to several µs, depending on the device.

$$i_C(t) \approx I_{tail,0} \cdot e^{-t / \tau_{tail}}$$

This tail current causes a "current–voltage overlap" loss at turn-off, and limits the maximum practical switching frequency of the IGBT.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol / Equation | Units | Physical Meaning |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | MOSFET drain current (sat.) | $i_D = K_n(v_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | A | Square-law transfer characteristic |
| 2 | MOSFET transconductance | $g_m = 2 K_n (v_{GS} - V_{th})$ | S (or A/V) | Control gain of the device |
| 3 | MOSFET ON-state drop | $V_{DS,on} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | V | Conduction loss voltage |
| 4 | Input / Miller capacitance | $C_{iss} = C_{GS}+C_{GD},\ C_{rss}=C_{GD}$ | F | Gate-drive design |
| 5 | Turn-on switching loss energy | $E_{on} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DD} I_L (t_{ri} + t_{fv})$ | J | Energy per switch event |
| 6 | Turn-off switching loss energy | $E_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DD} I_L (t_{rv} + t_{fi})$ | J | Energy per switch event |
| 7 | Total switching loss power | $P_{sw} = f_{sw}(E_{on} + E_{off})$ | W | Total switching loss per device |
| 8 | Conduction loss (MOSFET) | $P_{cond} = I_D^{2,\text{rms}} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | W | Conduction loss |
| 9 | IGBT saturation voltage | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5 - 2.5$ | V | ON-state drop |
| 10 | IGBT conduction loss | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C + r_C \cdot I_C^2$ | W | Linear + quadratic model |
| 11 | Gate charge | $Q_g = \int i_G \, dt$ | C (or nC) | Charge to fully turn on |
| 12 | Average gate-drive power | $P_G = Q_g \cdot V_{GG} \cdot f_{sw}$ | W | Driver dissipation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Valuation):** When asked "compare MOSFET and IGBT switching losses", state explicitly: (i) MOSFET turn-off loss is small because there is no minority-carrier storage; (ii) IGBT turn-off loss is *dominated* by the tail current, which can contribute 50–80% of the total $E_{off}$.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Switching-Loss Power in a Hard-Switched Chopper

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**. Consider an IGBT (or MOSFET) switching a clamped inductive load of current $I_L$ at DC bus voltage $V_s$, at switching frequency $f_{sw}$ with duty ratio $D$.

**Assumptions (state these in your KTU answer for full marks):**
1. The load current $I_L$ is **constant** (large $L$).
2. The switching trajectories are **piecewise linear** (ideal trapezoidal $v$ and $i$).
3. Turn-on and turn-off are mirror-symmetric for simplicity (we will correct for tail current if the device is an IGBT).

**Step 1 — Voltage and current waveforms during the turn-on interval $[0, t_{on}]$:**

During turn-on, the device current rises from 0 to $I_L$ over a time $t_{ri}$ and the device voltage falls from $V_s$ to $V_{on}$ (≈ 0 for a MOSFET, $V_{CE(sat)}$ for an IGBT) over a time $t_{fv}$. The cross-over (overlap) time is taken as $t_{c,on} = t_{ri} \approx t_{fv}$ for the simplified case.

The instantaneous device power dissipation is:

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = \left(V_s - \frac{V_s}{t_{c,on}} t\right) \cdot \left(\frac{I_L}{t_{c,on}} t\right)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over the overlap time to get the turn-on switching energy:**

$$E_{on} = \int_0^{t_{c,on}} p(t) \, dt = \int_0^{t_{c,on}} \frac{V_s I_L}{t_{c,on}^2} t \left(t_{c,on} - t\right) dt$$

$$E_{on} = \frac{V_s I_L}{t_{c,on}^2} \int_0^{t_{c,on}} \left(t \cdot t_{c,on} - t^2\right) dt$$

$$E_{on} = \frac{V_s I_L}{t_{c,on}^2} \left[\frac{t_{c,on} \cdot t^2}{2} - \frac{t^3}{3}\right]_0^{t_{c,on}}$$

$$E_{on} = \frac{V_s I_L}{t_{c,on}^2} \left[\frac{t_{c,on}^3}{2} - \frac{t_{c,on}^3}{3}\right] = \frac{V_s I_L}{t_{c,on}^2} \cdot \frac{t_{c,on}^3}{6}$$

$$\boxed{E_{on} = \frac{1}{6} V_s I_L \cdot t_{c,on}}$$

If the $V$–$I$ cross-over is taken as half the total transition (i.e. $t_{c,on} = t_{on}/2$), we obtain the **textbook result** $E_{on} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_s I_L t_{on}$, which is the form you should quote for KTU exams.

**Step 3 — Similarly for turn-off (ideal, no tail current):**

$$E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_s I_L \cdot t_{off}$$

**Step 4 — Total switching loss per switching cycle:**

$$E_{sw,total} = E_{on} + E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_s I_L (t_{on} + t_{off})$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by switching frequency to get average power dissipation:**

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot E_{sw,total} = \frac{1}{2} V_s I_L (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **load-independent** approximate formula — it depends only on $V_s$, $I_L$, the transition times, and $f_{sw}$. The conduction loss must be added separately. This formula is the basis of all data-sheet "switching loss vs. $I_C$" curves.

### 3.2 Derivation: IGBT Turn-Off Loss Including Tail Current

For an IGBT, the turn-off is **not** symmetric. After the rapid fall of the MOSFET portion of $i_C$ (over a time $t_f$), a slowly-decaying tail current of initial magnitude $I_{tail}$ persists for several time-constants $\tau_{tail}$.

**Step 1 — Decompose the turn-off energy:**

$$E_{off} = E_{off,fast} + E_{off,tail}$$

**Step 2 — Fast component (similar to MOSFET):**

$$E_{off,fast} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_s I_L \cdot t_f$$

**Step 3 — Tail component.** During the tail, the device voltage has already risen to $V_s$, so the entire $V_s$ is across the device. The tail current decays as:

$$i_{tail}(t) = I_{tail} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{tail}}$$

Integrating the power $V_s \cdot i_{tail}(t)$ from $t = 0$ to $\infty$:

$$E_{off,tail} = \int_0^\infty V_s \cdot I_{tail} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{tail}} \, dt = V_s I_{tail} \tau_{tail}$$

**Step 4 — Total turn-off energy:**

$$\boxed{E_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_s I_L t_f + V_s I_{tail} \tau_{tail}}$$

**Step 5 — Total switching loss power:**

$$P_{sw,IGBT} = f_{sw} \left[ \tfrac{1}{2} V_s I_L (t_{on} + t_f) + V_s I_{tail} \tau_{tail} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **tail term $V_s I_{tail} \tau_{tail}$** is unique to bipolar-output devices (IGBT, GTO). It is what forces the use of **soft-switching** (ZVS / ZCS) or **snubber circuits** at high frequencies, and is the *single most important* reason IGBTs are limited to < 100 kHz.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**
> A step-down chopper uses an IGBT with the following data:
> $V_s = 400$ V, $I_L = 50$ A, $f_{sw} = 5$ kHz, $D = 0.4$, $V_{CE(sat)} = 2$ V, $t_{on} = 0.4$ µs, $t_{off} = 0.6$ µs, $I_{tail} = 10$ A, $\tau_{tail} = 0.5$ µs.
> Calculate the total power dissipation in the IGBT.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Switching loss power:**

$$E_{on} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_s I_L t_{on} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 400 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.4 \times 10^{-6} = 4.0 \text{ mJ}$$

$$E_{off,fast} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 400 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.6 \times 10^{-6} = 6.0 \text{ mJ}$$

$$E_{off,tail} = V_s I_{tail} \tau_{tail} = 400 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 2.0 \text{ mJ}$$

$$E_{sw,total} = 4.0 + 6.0 + 2.0 = 12.0 \text{ mJ per cycle}$$

$$P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot E_{sw,total} = 5000 \cdot 12 \times 10^{-3} = 60 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Conduction loss power:**

The average collector current is $I_{C,avg} = D \cdot I_L = 0.4 \cdot 50 = 20$ A.

$$P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{C,avg} = 2 \cdot 20 = 40 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Total IGBT dissipation:**

$$P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{cond} = 60 + 40 = \mathbf{100 \text{ W}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation for the KTU answer:** Of the 60 W switching loss, the **tail current alone contributes 10 W** (≈17 %). If this IGBT were used at 20 kHz instead of 5 kHz, the switching loss would *quadruple* to 240 W and the tail term would become 40 W, illustrating the IGBT's frequency limitation.

### 3.4 Quick-Reference Loss-Formula Summary

For KTU's short-answer (Part-A 3-mark) questions, memorize this triangle:

$$P_{total} = \underbrace{V_{on} \cdot I_{avg}}_{\text{conduction}} + \underbrace{f_{sw} \cdot E_{sw,total}}_{\text{switching}} + \underbrace{P_{gate}}_{\text{driver (usually < 1 W)}}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Equivalent Circuit and Functional Model

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional/equivalent-circuit model** of the IGBT (which can be contrasted with a MOSFET by removing the pnp BJT). It also serves as the block architecture for the gate-drive interface.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gate_sig[Gate Drive Signal\nPWM from controller] --> gate_res[Gate Resistor Rg]
    gate_res --> gate_node[Gate Node]
    gate_node --> cge[CGE Capacitor]
    gate_node --> cgc[CGC Miller Capacitor]
    cgc --> collector_node[Collector Node]
    collector_node --> bjt_base[BJT Base Drive]
    bjt_base --> pnp_bjt[PNP BJT Output Stage]
    pnp_bjt --> collector_terminal[Collector Terminal VC]
    gate_node --> mosfet_input[MOSFET Input Channel]
    mosfet_input --> bjt_base
    pnp_bjt -.-> tail_current[Tail Current Source]
    collector_node --> cgc
    emitter_terminal[Emitter Terminal VE] --> cge
    emitter_terminal --> cgc
    style gate_sig fill:#FFD700
    style gate_node fill:#FFA500
    style collector_node fill:#87CEEB
    style emitter_terminal fill:#90EE90
    style pnp_bjt fill:#FF6B6B
    style tail_current fill:#FF6B6B
```

**Reading the diagram:**

*   The **MOSFET input** at the top converts the gate voltage into a base current for the pnp BJT.
*   The **Miller capacitor $C_{GC}$** couples the fast-changing collector voltage back to the gate, creating the Miller plateau and the $dv/dt$ vulnerability.
*   The **tail-current source** (dashed, in red) is the lumped model of the minority-carrier storage in the n⁻ drift layer — unique to the IGBT and absent in a pure MOSFET.

### 4.2 Mermaid Timing Diagram — The Four-Phase Turn-On

```mermaid
gantt
    title MOSFET IGBT Turn ON Phases
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Gate Voltage vGS
    Phase1 Turn on delay td on           :a1, 0, 50
    Phase2 Current rise tri              :a2, 50, 100
    Phase3 Miller plateau vDS falling    :a3, 100, 150
    Phase4 Final rise to VGG            :a4, 150, 200
    section Drain Current iD
    Zero during phase1                   :b1, 0, 50
    Rising to IL during phase2           :b2, 50, 100
    Flat at IL during phase3             :b3, 100, 150
    Flat at IL during phase4             :b4, 150, 200
    section Drain Voltage vDS
    Flat at VDD during phase1            :c1, 0, 50
    Flat at VDD during phase2            :c2, 50, 100
    Falling to VON during phase3         :c3, 100, 150
    Flat at VON during phase4            :c4, 150, 200
```

**Reading the diagram:**

*   The gate voltage is *flat* (Miller plateau) during the *entire* $v_{DS}$ fall — this is the visual signature of the Miller effect.
*   All three waveforms (vGS, iD, vDS) are in the **same time domain**, illustrating the energy-overlap that produces switching loss.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Typical Isolated IGBT Gate-Drive Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ctrl[Microcontroller or DSP\nPWM Output 3.3 V or 5 V] --> level_shifter[Level Shifter to 5 V CMOS]
    level_shifter --> opto[TLP250 or HCPL 316J\nOptocoupler Isolator 15 kV us]
    opto --> isolated_pwr[Isolated DC DC Converter\n15 V to minus 8 V rails]
    isolated_pwr --> gate_driver_uvlo[Gate Driver IC with UVLO\nM57962L or similar]
    gate_driver_uvlo --> desat[Desaturation Protection\nVce desat detector]
    gate_driver_uvlo --> rg_series[Series Gate Resistor Rg]
    desat --> fault_out[Fault Output to Controller]
    rg_series --> gate_igbt[IGBT Gate Emitter]
    gate_igbt --> collector_feedback[Collector Voltage Feedback]
    collector_feedback --> desat
    style ctrl fill:#FFD700
    style opto fill:#FFA500
    style isolated_pwr fill:#87CEEB
    style gate_driver_uvlo fill:#FF6B6B
    style gate_igbt fill:#90EE90
```

**Reading the diagram:**

*   **Three isolation barriers** are normally present: (1) optocoupler, (2) isolated DC–DC for gate power, (3) digital signal isolator for the fault feedback. All three are needed for **functional isolation** of a 600 V+ bus.
*   The **desaturation detector** monitors $V_{CE}$ during the ON state. If $V_{CE}$ rises above ≈ 7 V (meaning the IGBT is *not* fully saturated, i.e. it is in **short circuit**), the driver turns the IGBT off within ≈ 5 µs and latches a fault.
*   The series gate resistor $R_g$ (typically 5 – 50 Ω) damps $L C$ ringing, limits the $di_G/dt$ from the driver, and trades switching speed against EMI.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by a "fully-controlled" switch? Give two examples of fully-controlled switches used in power electronic converters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A fully-controlled switch is a semiconductor device in which both the turn-on and turn-off instants are controlled by a low-power control signal applied to a third terminal (gate/base). Unlike the thyristor, which can only be turned off by natural commutation, a fully-controlled device can interrupt the current at any instant.

*Two examples:* (i) **Power MOSFET**, (ii) **IGBT**. (Other valid examples: GTO, IGCT, power BJT.)

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, KTU expects the *defining property* (commutation by control signal) AND two *named* examples. Writing only the examples without the definition is worth at most 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2022]**
*Why is the IGBT not used at very high switching frequencies (> 100 kHz), even though its gate is a MOSFET-like input?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The IGBT is a **bipolar-output** device — the p⁺ collector injects **holes (minority carriers)** into the n⁻ drift layer to conductivity-modulate it and achieve a low on-state drop. At turn-off, these stored holes must be removed by recombination, producing a slowly-decaying **tail current** $i_C(t)$ that typically lasts 0.2 – 2 µs. This tail current causes an unavoidable *current–voltage overlap* loss:

$$E_{off,tail} = V_{CE} \cdot I_{tail} \cdot \tau_{tail}$$

As switching frequency $f_{sw}$ increases, the switching loss $P_{sw} = f_{sw} \cdot E_{sw}$ grows linearly, and the tail-current contribution becomes the dominant loss term. Above ≈ 100 kHz, the IGBT's efficiency falls below that of a comparable MOSFET, so the MOSFET is preferred.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **vertical DMOS structure** of an n-channel enhancement-mode power MOSFET. Explain how the *short-circuit between the n⁺ source and p-body* prevents the parasitic BJT from causing device failure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw and explain the **static output and transfer characteristics** of the power MOSFET. Define the threshold voltage $V_{th}$ and the ON-state resistance $R_{DS(on)}$, and state which one is the *key figure of merit* in switching applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Structure (sketch — describe in words for KTU written exam):**

The vertical n-channel power MOSFET has five layers stacked vertically:

| Layer (top to bottom) | Doping | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Source metal | — | Top electrical contact |
| n⁺ source | Heavily doped n | Source of electrons |
| p-body (channel) | p (light) | Inversion layer forms here |
| n⁻ drift (epitaxy) | Lightly doped n | Voltage-blocking region |
| n⁺ substrate / drain | Heavily doped n | Drain contact (bottom) |

The **gate** is a polysilicon electrode separated from the p-body by a thin SiO₂ layer. When $v_{GS} > V_{th}$, an n-type inversion layer (channel) is induced in the p-body just under the oxide. Electrons flow from n⁺ source → channel → n⁻ drift → n⁺ drain. The current is *vertical* through the die, allowing large cross-sectional area and high current.

**The shorted source–body contact (3 marks dedicated):**

The MOSFET inherently contains a **parasitic n-p-n BJT**: emitter = n⁺ source, base = p-body, collector = n⁻ drift. The body-drift junction is normally reverse-biased in the OFF state, so the BJT is OFF. However, if the p-body resistance is too high, a *lateral* hole current (from avalanche-generated carriers in the drift region during turn-off) can forward-bias the **base–emitter** junction of this BJT, switching it on. Once on, the BJT *cannot* be turned off by the gate — it is a latch-up failure.

**Solution:** The p-body is *shorted to the n⁺ source* by the source metallization. This shorts the BJT's base to its emitter, guaranteeing $V_{BE,BJT} = 0$ and *preventing turn-on of the parasitic BJT* under all $dv/dt$ conditions.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Transfer characteristic** is $i_D$ vs $v_{GS}$ (drawn for several $v_{DS}$ values, but the curves nearly coincide in saturation):

*   For $v_{GS} < V_{th}$: $i_D \approx 0$ (cut-off).
*   For $v_{GS} > V_{th}$: $i_D = K_n (v_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ (square-law rise).
*   The slope $\partial i_D / \partial v_{GS}$ is the **transconductance $g_m$**, also called the *forward transfer admittance* $y_{fs}$ in data sheets.

**Output characteristic** is $i_D$ vs $v_{DS}$ for several $v_{GS}$ values, with three regions:

1.  **Cut-off:** $v_{GS} < V_{th} \Rightarrow i_D = 0$.
2.  **Ohmic (triode) region:** $v_{DS} < v_{GS} - V_{th}$. The MOSFET behaves like a **voltage-controlled resistor** $R_{DS}$. At high $v_{GS}$, the curves merge into a single line of slope $1/R_{DS(on)}$.
3.  **Active (saturation) region:** $v_{DS} > v_{GS} - V_{th}$. $i_D$ is constant and controlled by $v_{GS}$.

**Definitions:**

*   **Threshold voltage $V_{th}$:** The minimum $v_{GS}$ at which a conducting channel forms and $i_D$ begins to flow. Typical value: **2 – 4 V** for power MOSFETs.
*   **ON-state resistance $R_{DS(on)}$:** The slope resistance of the $i_D$–$v_{DS}$ curve in the ohmic region at the maximum rated $v_{GS}$ (typically 10 V). Typical value: **1 mΩ – 1 Ω** depending on voltage class.

**Key figure of merit:** $R_{DS(on)}$ is the *key figure of merit* for switching applications because the conduction loss in the ON state is

$$P_{cond} = I_D^{2,\text{rms}} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$$

A low $R_{DS(on)}$ directly reduces the conduction loss and the散热器 (heatsink) requirement. Note that $R_{DS(on)}$ is usually specified at $T_J = 25\,°C$ and increases to roughly 1.5×–1.7× its value at $T_J = 125\,°C$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a clear **layer diagram and equivalent circuit**, describe the construction of the **IGBT**. Explain the role of the **p⁺ substrate** in conductivity modulation and the function of the **shorted emitter** in preventing latch-up. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **static and switching characteristics** of the power MOSFET and the IGBT. In a 600 V, 100 A motor-drive inverter switching at 5 kHz, which device is preferred, and why? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Layer diagram (top to bottom of the IGBT die):**

| Layer | Doping | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Emitter metal | — | Top contact |
| n⁺ emitter | n⁺ | Electron source (MOS channel) |
| p-body | p | Channel + BJT base |
| n⁻ drift (epitaxy) | n⁻ | Voltage blocking, also stores holes |
| **p⁺ substrate / collector** | p⁺ | **Hole injector, BJT collector** |

**Equivalent circuit:** A pnp BJT whose base is driven by a MOSFET. The MOSFET's drain current = BJT's base current. A parasitic n-p-n-p thyristor (n⁺ emitter / p-body / n⁻ drift / p⁺ collector) is also present, modelled in parallel.

**Role of the p⁺ substrate (4 marks):**

When the IGBT is ON ($v_{GE} > V_{GE(th)}$), the MOSFET channel conducts electrons from the n⁺ emitter into the n⁻ drift layer. The p⁺ collector is forward-biased with respect to the n⁻ drift, so it **injects holes** into the n⁻ drift region. These holes (minority carriers in the n-type drift layer) **accumulate and recombine slowly**, raising the *free-carrier density* in the drift region far above the original doping. This is called **conductivity modulation**.

*Effect:* The effective resistance of the n⁻ drift region is dramatically reduced — by a factor of 10 – 100× compared to a MOSFET of the same voltage rating. This is why an IGBT has $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5$–$2.5$ V at high currents, whereas an equivalent MOSFET would have $V_{DS,on} = I_D \cdot R_{DS(on)} \approx 5$–$10$ V or more.

*Drawback:* These stored holes cannot be removed instantly at turn-off. They must either be swept out by the electric field or recombine. This produces the **tail current** at turn-off.

**Shorted emitter (3 marks):**

The p-body is shorted to the n⁺ emitter by the top metal. This:

*   Prevents the **parasitic n-p-n BJT** (formed by n⁺ emitter, p-body, n⁻ drift) from turning on.
*   Diverts any hole current flowing laterally in the p-body to the emitter metal, ensuring the **base–emitter junction of the parasitic BJT** stays at $V_{BE} = 0$.
*   Prevents the **parasitic thyristor** (BJT + pnp) from latching during high $dv/dt$ or short-circuit transients.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Power MOSFET | IGBT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Conduction mechanism | Unipolar (electrons only) | Bipolar (electrons + holes) |
| Output stage | n⁻ drift (no modulation) | n⁻ drift with hole injection |
| ON-state drop at high V | High (resistive) | Low (modulated, $V_{CE(sat)}$) |
| Input | Voltage (MOS gate) | Voltage (MOS gate) |
| Turn-off time | Very fast (10 – 100 ns) | Slower, **tail current** (0.2 – 2 µs) |
| Max frequency | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | 1 kHz – 100 kHz |
| Voltage rating | up to 1000 V | 600 V – 6.6 kV |
| Current rating | up to ~ 500 A | up to ~ 3600 A |
| Temperature coeff. of $R_{DS(on)}$ / $V_{CE(sat)}$ | Positive (easy parallel) | Positive (easy parallel) |
| Cost at high V/I | Higher | Lower |

**Selection for 600 V, 100 A, 5 kHz inverter (2 marks):**

At 600 V and 100 A, this is squarely in the **IGBT's sweet spot**. Reasoning:

1.  At 600 V, a MOSFET would have $R_{DS(on)} \geq 50$ mΩ, giving $V_{DS,on} = 5$ V and conduction loss $I^2 R = 100^2 \times 0.05 = 500$ W per device.
2.  An IGBT at the same rating has $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 2$ V, giving conduction loss $V \cdot I = 2 \times 100 = 200$ W.
3.  At 5 kHz, the IGBT's switching loss (≈ 60 W from the worked example above) is acceptable.
4.  A MOSFET at 5 kHz would *also* work, but it would dissipate much more total power, requiring a larger heatsink and reducing efficiency.

**Conclusion:** **IGBT is preferred** for a 600 V, 100 A, 5 kHz motor drive because the lower conduction loss outweighs the slightly higher switching loss, and the 5 kHz frequency is well within the IGBT's capability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "MOSFET is faster, so it is better" — this is **wrong** for 600 V / 100 A. Speed alone is not the criterion. The criterion is the **total power loss** = conduction + switching. Always justify your device choice with a *quantitative loss comparison*, not a qualitative adjective. Marks lost in this question are almost always for *not computing numbers*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

*   **MOSFET is unipolar, IGBT is bipolar-output.** This is THE single most important distinction in this module.
*   MOSFET: square-law transfer, $R_{DS(on)}$ is the figure of merit, $R_{DS(on)}$ has a **positive** temperature coefficient (good for paralleling).
*   IGBT: linear-ish transfer (more like a BJT), $V_{CE(sat)}$ is the figure of merit, also has **positive** temperature coefficient.
*   **Gate charge $Q_g$**, not capacitance, is the figure of merit for *gate-drive design*.
*   MOSFET dynamic behaviour: $C_{iss}$, $C_{oss}$, $C_{rss}$, four-phase turn-on, Miller plateau.
*   IGBT dynamic behaviour: same as MOSFET turn-on, but **tail current** at turn-off dominates $E_{off}$.
*   IGBT failure modes: **(1) latch-up** of the parasitic thyristor, **(2) short-circuit withstand** $t_{SC} \approx 10$ µs, **(3) thermal runaway** if the heatsink fails.
*   MOSFET failure modes: **(1) $V_{DS}$ over-voltage** from unclamped inductive switching, **(2) $dv/dt$ turn-on** (the Miller effect can re-trigger the MOSFET in a half-bridge), **(3) thermal** via $R_{DS(on)}$.
*   **Gate-drive requirements:**
    *   MOSFET: $+10$ to $+15$ V ON, 0 V (or negative a few volts for noise margin) OFF. Need a low-impedance driver to source/sink the Miller current.
    *   IGBT: $+15$ V ON, **$-5$ to $-15$ V OFF** (the negative off-state is a *must* to prevent $dv/dt$-induced turn-on in half-bridge configurations). Use a *desaturation* (Vce-desat) short-circuit protection circuit.
*   **Typical gate drive** = isolated DC–DC converter + optocoupler/digital isolator + gate driver IC (e.g. **TLP250**, **HCPL-316J**, **M57962L**, **UCC21750**).
*   **Switching loss formula:** $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_s I_L (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ (no tail).
*   **Conduction loss formula:** $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg}$ (IGBT) or $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ (MOSFET).
*   **Device cross-over frequency** (for 600 V class): approximately **10 – 50 kHz**. Above → MOSFET, below → IGBT.
*   **Snubber circuits** (RCD) are required for IGBTs at high $di/dt$ and for MOSFETs at high $dv/dt$ to limit voltage spikes during turn-off.
*   **Body diode** of a MOSFET (and IGBT) is a *real* parasitic diode; it has slow reverse recovery and is often paralleled with a faster external diode in bridge circuits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Power Devices: Introduction to Wide Bandgap Devices - SiC MOSFET and GaN HEMT - Features and Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives
> - **Module:** Module-I
> - **Topic:** Modern Power Devices: Introduction to Wide Bandgap Devices - SiC MOSFET and GaN HEMT - Features and Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wide Bandgap (WBG) Semiconductors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wide Bandgap (WBG) Semiconductors** are a class of electronic semiconductor materials whose **bandgap energy ($E_g$)** is significantly larger than that of conventional **Silicon (Si)**, which has $E_g \approx \mathbf{1.12\ eV}$. For power-device applications, "wide" typically refers to $E_g > \mathbf{2\ eV}$, placing **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** and **Gallium Nitride (GaN)** in this category.

The **bandgap** is the minimum energy required to liberate an electron from the valence band into the conduction band, allowing it to participate in electrical conduction. A wider bandgap means the intrinsic carrier concentration is exponentially suppressed, which is the root cause of WBG devices' superior high-temperature, high-voltage, and high-frequency performance.

| Material | Bandgap Energy $E_g$ | Category |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Silicon (Si) | **1.12 eV** | Narrow / Conventional |
| Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) | 1.42 eV | Narrow |
| Silicon Carbide (SiC) — 4H polytype | **3.26 eV** | Wide Bandgap |
| Gallium Nitride (GaN) | **3.39 eV** | Wide Bandgap |
| Diamond (C) | 5.47 eV | Ultra-Wide Bandgap |

## 1.2 The Two Hero Devices of This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **SiC MOSFET** = a **majority-carrier, normally-off, vertical-channel** power transistor built on a Silicon Carbide wafer, used for high-voltage (> 600 V) applications such as EV traction inverters, solar inverters, and industrial drives.
>
> **GaN HEMT** = a **High Electron Mobility Transistor** built on a Gallium Nitride heterostructure (AlGaN/GaN), exploiting a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** for ultra-high switching speeds in medium-voltage (≤ 650 V) applications such as chargers, adapters, and telecom power supplies.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Why Bandgap Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Model:**
> Imagine a bucket filled with electrons. In **Silicon**, the bucket has a low wall (1.12 eV) — even a small shake (heat or electric field) knocks electrons over the wall, causing **leakage current** and **thermal runaway** at high temperatures. In **SiC and GaN**, the bucket wall is nearly **3× taller**. You would need a much harder shake for electrons to escape, so the device stays "off" much more stubbornly, can withstand higher temperatures, and supports much higher electric fields before breaking down. This is the *single root reason* why WBG devices outperform Si.

### 1.3.1 The Three Master Advantages of WBG Materials

1. **Higher Breakdown Electric Field ($E_{crit}$):** SiC $\approx$ **3 MV/cm**, GaN $\approx$ **3.3 MV/cm**, Si $\approx$ **0.3 MV/cm** — a 10× advantage.
2. **Higher Thermal Conductivity:** SiC $\approx$ **4.9 W/cm·K**, Si $\approx$ **1.5 W/cm·K** — easier heat removal.
3. **Higher Electron Saturation Velocity ($v_{sat}$):** GaN $\approx$ **2.5 × 10⁷ cm/s**, Si $\approx$ **1.0 × 10⁷ cm/s** — faster switching.

These three physical advantages cascade into *system-level* wins: smaller magnetics, higher power density, higher efficiency, and lower cooling cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Wider Bandgap → Higher Blocking Voltage

The **avalanche breakdown field** of a semiconductor scales as:

$$E_{crit} \propto E_g^{2.5}$$

Therefore, increasing $E_g$ from 1.12 eV (Si) to 3.26 eV (SiC) — a factor of 2.91 — yields a theoretical $E_{crit}$ advantage of roughly **10×**. This is the foundational physics behind the entire WBG revolution.

## 2.2 Specific On-Resistance — The Figure-of-Merit Battle

The **specific on-resistance** ($R_{on,sp}$) of a unipolar power device is the most quoted performance metric. For a given breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$, it is given by:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 V_{BR}^{2}}{\varepsilon_s \mu_n E_{crit}^{3}}$$

where:
- $V_{BR}$ = breakdown voltage (V)
- $\varepsilon_s$ = permittivity of semiconductor (F/cm)
- $\mu_n$ = electron mobility (cm²/V·s)
- $E_{crit}$ = critical breakdown field (V/cm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the formula:** Since $E_{crit}$ appears in the **denominator raised to the third power**, even a modest 10× improvement in $E_{crit}$ (SiC vs Si) reduces $R_{on,sp}$ by a factor of **1000×** for the same blocking voltage. This is why SiC MOSFETs can be made dramatically smaller than equivalent Si IGBTs.

## 2.3 Baliga's Figure of Merit (BFOM)

The most cited **engineering figure of merit** for power switching devices:

$$BFOM = \varepsilon_s \mu_n E_{crit}^{3}$$

| Material | BFOM (relative to Si = 1) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Silicon | 1 |
| SiC (4H) | **~290** |
| GaN | **~ 1700** (with 2DEG mobility) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning:** A higher BFOM means a *smaller die* can achieve the *same on-resistance and blocking voltage*. Smaller die → lower capacitance → faster switching → smaller passive components (inductors, capacitors) → higher power density.

## 2.4 Johnson's Figure of Merit (JFoM) — For High Frequency

$$JFoM = \frac{E_{crit} \cdot v_{sat}}{2\pi}$$

This combines breakdown strength with switching speed — the relevant FoM for RF and high-frequency power conversion. GaN excels here because of its high $v_{sat}$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is **the** condensed formula reference a KTU student should memorize. Every equation here is testable.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Intrinsic carrier concentration | $n_i \propto T^{3/2} \exp(-E_g / 2kT)$ | WBG → exponentially lower leakage at high T |
| 2 | Specific on-resistance | $R_{on,sp} = \dfrac{4 V_{BR}^{2}}{\varepsilon_s \mu_n E_{crit}^{3}}$ | Smaller die for same voltage |
| 3 | Baliga's FoM | $BFOM = \varepsilon_s \mu_n E_{crit}^{3}$ | Material ranking for power switches |
| 4 | Johnson's FoM | $JFoM = \dfrac{E_{crit} \cdot v_{sat}}{2\pi}$ | High-frequency / RF capability |
| 5 | Switching loss energy | $E_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | Energy lost per switching cycle |
| 6 | Conduction loss | $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^{2} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Steady-state loss |
| 7 | Maximum junction temperature | $T_{j,max}$: SiC = **175 °C**, GaN = **150–200 °C**, Si = 150 °C | Higher ambient capability |
| 8 | Power density scaling | $P_{density} \propto BFOM$ | Compact converters |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Units!** $E_{crit}$ must be in **V/cm** (not V/m or MV/cm) when used in the cgs-unit form of the BFOM/Ron formula. Always state units. Examiners deduct for missing units.

## 2.6 SiC MOSFET — Features and Advantages

### 2.6.1 Construction
- Vertical **DMOSFET** (Double-diffused MOSFET) structure fabricated on a **4H-SiC** substrate.
- The drift region is **10× thinner** than a Si equivalent because of the higher $E_{crit}$, dramatically lowering $R_{DS(on)}$.
- **SiO₂** is used as the gate oxide (a key SiC advantage over GaN — it enables a true MOSFET gate, similar to Si).

### 2.6.2 Key Features
- **Normally-off** device (like a Si MOSFET) — failsafe.
- **Voltage ratings:** 650 V, 1200 V, 1700 V, 3300 V (commercially available).
- **Zero reverse-recovery charge** in the body diode (no bipolar minority-carrier storage).
- **High $T_{j,max}$** = 175 °C (some automotive-grade up to 200 °C).
- Gate drive: 0 V (off) / +15 to +20 V (on) — similar to Si, simplifying migration.
- **Third quadrant:** Body diode can conduct — but $V_f$ is higher than Si; a **SiC Schottky** is often anti-paralleled for lowest loss.

### 2.6.3 Advantages Over Si IGBT
1. **10× higher switching frequency** (50–100 kHz vs 5–20 kHz) → smaller magnetics.
2. **Eliminates tail current** → much lower switching losses.
3. **Higher efficiency** (97–99% converter efficiency achievable).
4. **Smaller heatsink** — less heat to remove.
5. **Rugged at high temperature** — derating curve is gentle.

## 2.7 GaN HEMT — Features and Advantages

### 2.7.1 Construction — The 2DEG Magic
- A **heterostructure** of **AlGaN / GaN** is grown on a Si, SiC, or sapphire substrate.
- Spontaneous and piezoelectric polarization creates a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** at the AlGaN/GaN interface.
- The 2DEG has an electron mobility of $\approx$ **1500–2000 cm²/V·s** — far higher than bulk GaN or Si — and very high sheet carrier density $\approx$ **1 × 10¹³ cm⁻²**.
- The 2DEG is **normally-on** (depletion-mode) by default. Commercial devices use a **p-GaN gate** or **cascode** configuration to achieve **normally-off** operation.

### 2.7.2 Two Packaging Flavors of GaN
1. **Cascode (D-mode GaN + low-voltage Si MOSFET in one package):** Standard Si-style gate drive, very rugged.
2. **E-mode with p-GaN gate:** Direct gate drive, but $\pm$10 V gate limits — sensitive to over-voltage.

### 2.7.3 Key Features
- **Voltage ratings:** 100 V, 200 V, 400 V, 650 V (most common).
- **Reverse conduction** via the 2DEG channel itself (no body diode) — very low $Q_{rr}$.
- **Ultra-low gate charge ($Q_g$)** and **output capacitance ($C_{oss}$)** → femto-to-nano-coulomb switching.
- **Switching frequency:** 100 kHz to 10 MHz (multi-MHz in soft-switched topologies).
- **No reverse-recovery loss** — major advantage in hard-switched half-bridges.

### 2.7.4 Advantages Over Si MOSFET
1. **10–100× faster switching** → enables MHz power supplies.
2. **Smaller dead-time** → higher efficiency.
3. **Dramatically reduced driver losses** (low $Q_g$).
4. **Lighter, smaller, cooler** chargers and adapters.
5. **Zero $Q_{rr}$** → ideal for totem-pole PFC (bridgeless rectifier).

## 2.8 SiC vs GaN — Quick Engineering Selection Guide

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb used in industry:**
> - **> 1200 V or high power (> 50 kW):** Choose **SiC MOSFET**.
> - **≤ 650 V, high-frequency, low-to-medium power (< 5 kW):** Choose **GaN HEMT**.
> - **100–200 V, very high frequency (e.g., LiDAR, RF):** GaN HEMT is unrivaled.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $E_{crit}$ Controls $R_{on,sp}$ Cubically

We will derive the **specific on-resistance of a vertical drift region** for a punch-through (PT) power device, since this is the geometry used by both SiC MOSFETs and the underlying physics that defines BFOM.

### Step 1 — Define the geometry
Let the drift region have length $L_d$ and cross-sectional area $A$. The breakdown condition requires the integral of the electric field across the drift region to reach the critical value:

$$V_{BR} = \int_0^{L_d} E(x) \, dx$$

For an abrupt one-sided junction, the field is maximum at the junction ($E_{max} = E_{crit}$) and falls linearly to zero at $L_d$. The average field is $E_{crit}/2$:

$$V_{BR} = \frac{1}{2} E_{crit} L_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_d = \frac{2 V_{BR}}{E_{crit}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the resistance
The drift-region resistance is:

$$R_{drift} = \rho \frac{L_d}{A} = \frac{L_d}{q \mu_n N_D A}$$

For an abrupt junction, the doping $N_D$ required to support the field is given by Poisson's equation:

$$N_D = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}}{q L_d}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $L_d$ into $N_D$

$$N_D = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit} \cdot E_{crit}}{q \cdot 2 V_{BR}} = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}^{2}}{2 q V_{BR}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the specific on-resistance
Specific on-resistance is $R_{drift} \cdot A$:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{L_d}{q \mu_n N_D}$$

Substitute the expressions for $L_d$ and $N_D$:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{2 V_{BR} / E_{crit}}{q \mu_n \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}^{2}}{2 q V_{BR}}}$$

Simplify the numerator and denominator (the $q$ cancels):

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{2 V_{BR}}{E_{crit}} \cdot \frac{2 V_{BR}}{\mu_n \varepsilon_s E_{crit}^{2}}$$

Multiply:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 V_{BR}^{2}}{\varepsilon_s \mu_n E_{crit}^{3}}$$

**This is the canonical BFOM equation.** It is the single most important equation in WBG device physics.

### Step 5 — Interpret the cubic dependence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cubic dependence on $E_{crit}$ is the entire reason WBG devices exist.** When $E_{crit}$ increases 10× (from Si to SiC), $R_{on,sp}$ decreases by **1000×** for the same blocking voltage. This is *not* a 10× improvement; it is a **1000×** improvement — orders of magnitude.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Compare the specific on-resistance of a **Silicon** and a **4H-SiC** device both rated at **$V_{BR}$ = 1200 V**. Given: $\varepsilon_{Si} = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm, $\mu_{n,Si} = 1350$ cm²/V·s, $E_{crit,Si} = 3 \times 10^{5}$ V/cm. For SiC: $\varepsilon_{SiC} = 9.7 \times 10^{-13}$ F/cm, $\mu_{n,SiC} = 720$ cm²/V·s, $E_{crit,SiC} = 2.5 \times 10^{6}$ V/cm.

### Step A — Compute $R_{on,sp}$ for Silicon

$$R_{on,sp,Si} = \frac{4 \cdot (1200)^{2}}{(1.04 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 1350 \cdot (3 \times 10^{5})^{3}}$$

Numerator:

$$4 \cdot 1.44 \times 10^{6} = 5.76 \times 10^{6}$$

Denominator:

$$(1.04 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 1350 \cdot (2.7 \times 10^{16}) = 1.04 \cdot 1350 \cdot 2.7 \times 10^{4}$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$1.04 \cdot 1350 = 1404$$

$$1404 \cdot 2.7 = 3790.8$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 3790.8 \times 10^{4} = 3.79 \times 10^{7}$$

Therefore:

$$R_{on,sp,Si} = \frac{5.76 \times 10^{6}}{3.79 \times 10^{7}} = 0.152\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^{2}$$

### Step B — Compute $R_{on,sp}$ for SiC

$$R_{on,sp,SiC} = \frac{4 \cdot (1200)^{2}}{(9.7 \times 10^{-13}) \cdot 720 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{6})^{3}}$$

Numerator: $5.76 \times 10^{6}$ (same as above).

Denominator:

$$(9.7 \times 10^{-13}) \cdot 720 \cdot (1.5625 \times 10^{19})$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$9.7 \cdot 720 = 6984$$

$$6984 \cdot 1.5625 = 10912.5$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 10912.5 \times 10^{6} = 1.091 \times 10^{10}$$

Therefore:

$$R_{on,sp,SiC} = \frac{5.76 \times 10^{6}}{1.091 \times 10^{10}} = 5.28 \times 10^{-4}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^{2}$$

### Step C — Ratio (the punchline)

$$\frac{R_{on,sp,Si}}{R_{on,sp,SiC}} = \frac{0.152}{5.28 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 288$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For the same 1200 V blocking voltage, a 4H-SiC device has roughly **288× lower specific on-resistance** than Si. In practical terms, this means the SiC die can be ~17× smaller in linear dimension (since area scales as the ratio), or the same die size with vastly lower conduction loss.

## 3.3 Derivation: Switching Loss Reduction from Lower $C_{oss}$

The energy stored in the output capacitance at turn-off is:

$$E_{oss} = \frac{1}{2} C_{oss} V_{DS}^{2}$$

For a half-bridge hard-switched transition, the total switching loss per cycle is:

$$E_{sw,tot} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_r + t_f) + E_{oss,HS} + E_{oss,LS}$$

where HS and LS are high-side and low-side devices. Because GaN's $C_{oss}$ is typically **5–10× smaller** than an equivalent Si MOSFET, and $t_r$/$t_f$ are **5–10× shorter**, the switching loss is reduced by **25–100×** — which is exactly why GaN enables MHz operation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is the most-asked KTU sub-question pattern.** Memorize every cell.

| Parameter | Si MOSFET | Si IGBT | **SiC MOSFET** | **GaN HEMT** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Bandgap $E_g$ (eV) | 1.12 | 1.12 | **3.26** | **3.39** |
| $E_{crit}$ (MV/cm) | 0.3 | 0.3 | **2.5–3.0** | **3.0–3.3** |
| $v_{sat}$ ($\times 10^{7}$ cm/s) | 1.0 | 1.0 | 2.0 | **2.5** |
| Max $T_j$ (°C) | 150 | 150 | **175–200** | **150–200** |
| Voltage rating (V) | ≤ 600 | 600–6500 | 650–3300 | 100–650 |
| Switching freq. (typical) | ≤ 100 kHz | 5–20 kHz | **50–200 kHz** | **100 kHz – 10 MHz** |
| $Q_{rr}$ | Moderate | High | **~0** | **~0** |
| Conduction type | Unipolar | Bipolar (tail) | **Unipolar (no tail)** | **Unipolar (no tail)** |
| BFOM (rel. to Si) | 1 | 1 | **~290** | **~1700** |
| Typical application | SMPS, low-V | Traction, drives | **EV, solar, drives** | **Adapters, PFC, LiDAR** |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Wide Bandgap (WBG) semiconductors. List any two WBG materials and their bandgap energies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Wide Bandgap semiconductors are materials whose bandgap energy ($E_g$) is significantly greater than that of silicon ($E_g = 1.12$ eV), typically above 2 eV. **(1 mark)**
>
> Two WBG materials:
> 1. **Silicon Carbide (4H-SiC)** with $E_g = \mathbf{3.26\ eV}$ **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Gallium Nitride (GaN)** with $E_g = \mathbf{3.39\ eV}$ **(1 mark)**
>
> *Total: 3 marks*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a 2DEG, and how does it arise in a GaN HEMT?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **2DEG (Two-Dimensional Electron Gas)** is a thin sheet of high-mobility electrons confined at the heterointerface between **AlGaN and GaN** in a GaN HEMT. **(1 mark)**
>
> It arises from **spontaneous and piezoelectric polarization** at the AlGaN/GaN interface, which produces a fixed positive sheet charge on the AlGaN side, inducing a high-density ($\sim 1 \times 10^{13}$ cm⁻²) layer of mobile electrons on the GaN side. **(2 marks)**
>
> *Total: 3 marks*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and operating principle of a **SiC MOSFET**. Compare its performance with a **conventional Si IGBT** for high-voltage applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **specific on-resistance** of a vertical power-MOSFET drift region and show that $R_{on,sp} \propto E_{crit}^{-3}$. Hence, calculate the ratio of $R_{on,sp}$ for Si and 4H-SiC at $V_{BR} = 1200$ V using standard material parameters. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A 4H-SiC MOSFET is a **vertical-channel, DMOSFET-like** device fabricated on a 4H-SiC wafer.
- It has a **SiO₂ gate oxide**, an n-type **drift region** (much thinner than a Si equivalent), a p-well that forms the body and the body diode, and an n⁺ source contact on top.
- The drain is at the bottom (n⁺ SiC substrate).
- A typical 1200 V SiC MOSFET has a drift region of only **~10 µm** — about **10× thinner** than an equivalent Si IGBT.

**Operating Principle (2 marks):**
- When $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ (~2–4 V), an inversion layer (n-channel) forms under the gate, connecting source to drift region. Electrons flow vertically to the drain → **on-state** with low $R_{DS(on)}$.
- When $V_{GS} = 0$ V, the channel is pinched off → **off-state**; the thin drift region blocks the full 1200 V because the high $E_{crit}$ of SiC prevents avalanche breakdown.

**Comparison with Si IGBT (2 marks):**

| Feature | Si IGBT | SiC MOSFET |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Switching frequency | 5–20 kHz | 50–200 kHz |
| Tail current | Yes (bipolar) | **None** (unipolar) |
| Efficiency | 96–98% | **98–99%** |
| Conduction loss | $V_{CE(sat)}$ drop (~1.5 V) | Resistive ($I^2 R$), no offset |

*Total: 7 marks*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (5 marks):** *(Reproduce the full derivation from Section 3.1 here)*

From the derivation:

$$R_{on,sp} = \frac{4 V_{BR}^{2}}{\varepsilon_s \mu_n E_{crit}^{3}}$$

Since $E_{crit}$ appears in the **denominator raised to the third power**, we have $R_{on,sp} \propto E_{crit}^{-3}$. **(1 mark)**

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** *(Reproduce the worked example from Section 3.2)*

$$R_{on,sp,Si} = 0.152\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^{2}$$

$$R_{on,sp,SiC} = 5.28 \times 10^{-4}\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}^{2}$$

$$\frac{R_{on,sp,Si}}{R_{on,sp,SiC}} \approx 288$$

> **The SiC device has ~288× lower specific on-resistance.** *Total: 7 marks*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the construction of a **GaN HEMT**. Explain the role of the **2DEG** in device operation. What is meant by a **cascode** GaN HEMT? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain **five key advantages** of WBG devices (SiC and GaN) over conventional Si devices. Comment on the **typical application range** (voltage and power) where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A GaN HEMT is built as a **heterostructure**: an AlGaN barrier layer grown epitaxially on a GaN layer, which itself sits on a Si, SiC, or sapphire substrate.
- **Source and drain** are ohmic contacts to the 2DEG channel at the AlGaN/GaN interface.
- The **gate** is a Schottky metal or a p-GaN layer that depletes the 2DEG beneath it for normally-off (E-mode) operation.

**Role of 2DEG (2 marks):**
- The 2DEG is a **high-mobility (~1500–2000 cm²/V·s)**, **high-density (~10¹³ cm⁻²)** electron channel formed by polarization.
- It provides an **ultra-low on-resistance** path from source to drain, and because the carriers are *confined to a 2D plane*, they experience very little impurity scattering → high speed.
- The high sheet density means the device can carry high current in a small area.

**Cascode Configuration (2 marks):**
- A **cascode** GaN HEMT packages a **normally-on (D-mode) GaN HEMT** in series with a **low-voltage Si MOSFET**.
- The Si MOSFET is in the *gate path* of the GaN HEMT. When the Si MOSFET is off, the GaN gate is pulled to 0 V → GaN is off. When the Si MOSFET is on, the GaN gate is pulled below its threshold → GaN is on.
- The user only sees the **Si MOSFET gate** → standard **0/+10 V** drive, no special driver needed.

*Total: 7 marks*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Five Advantages of WBG Devices (5 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Higher breakdown voltage per unit drift thickness:** $E_{crit}$ of SiC and GaN is ~10× that of Si → thinner drift region, lower $R_{DS(on)}$, smaller die. **(1 mark)**
2. **Higher operating temperature:** $T_{j,max}$ up to **200 °C** (vs 150 °C for Si) → less cooling, smaller heatsink, better reliability. **(1 mark)**
3. **Higher switching frequency:** 10–100× faster than Si → smaller inductors and capacitors, higher power density (kW/L and kW/kg). **(1 mark)**
4. **Zero reverse-recovery charge ($Q_{rr}$):** WBG devices are unipolar; no minority-carrier storage → near-zero reverse-recovery loss, ideal for hard-switched and totem-pole topologies. **(1 mark)**
5. **Higher efficiency:** 98–99% achievable, leading to lower operating cost and longer battery life in EVs. **(1 mark)**

**Application Range (2 marks):**
- **SiC MOSFET** — preferred for **≥ 1200 V** and **high power (> 50 kW)**: EV traction inverters, solar PV inverters (1500 V DC), industrial motor drives, railway traction, UPS.
- **GaN HEMT** — preferred for **≤ 650 V** and **medium-to-high frequency**: 65 W USB-PD adapters, telecom 48 V converters, server power, LiDAR, wireless charging, audio amplifiers, RF envelope tracking.

*Total: 7 marks*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Why" of cubic dependence.** Writing $R_{on,sp} \propto 1/E_{crit}^{3}$ is not enough — you must state *why* this matters (drift length, doping concentration trade-off, avalanche integral). Examiners will deduct 2 marks for a one-line answer here.
> 2. **Confusing the GaN cascode with E-mode GaN.** A cascode uses a Si MOSFET in series; E-mode uses a p-GaN gate. Examiners will mark you down for mixing the two.
> 3. **Using "voltage" and "current" ratings interchangeably** for SiC vs GaN. SiC is **voltage-leading** (>1200 V); GaN is **frequency-leading** (multi-MHz). Always state the *operating regime*, not just the rating.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the 2DEG mobility** value (1500–2000 cm²/V·s). A generic "very high" loses a mark.
> 5. **No units in numerical answers.** Always write $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$ for $R_{on,sp}$, e/cm³ for $N_D$, °C for $T_j$. Examiners deduct up to 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 6. **Missing the JFoM vs BFOM distinction.** BFOM is for power switching; JFoM is for RF/high-frequency. Mixing them up costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision summary — read this the morning of the exam.**

- [x] **WBG = bandgap > 2 eV**; SiC = **3.26 eV**, GaN = **3.39 eV**, Si = 1.12 eV.
- [x] **Three master material advantages:** higher $E_{crit}$, higher thermal conductivity, higher $v_{sat}$.
- [x] **Master formula (BFOM foundation):** $R_{on,sp} = \dfrac{4 V_{BR}^{2}}{\varepsilon_s \mu_n E_{crit}^{3}}$.
- [x] **Cubic dependence on $E_{crit}$** — 10× $E_{crit}$ → 1000× lower $R_{on,sp}$.
- [x] **BFOM** = $\varepsilon_s \mu_n E_{crit}^{3}$; SiC ≈ 290×, GaN ≈ 1700× of Si.
- [x] **JFoM** = $E_{crit} v_{sat} / 2\pi$ — GaN wins RF.
- [x] **SiC MOSFET:** vertical, normally-off, SiO₂ gate, 650–3300 V, 50–200 kHz, automotive-grade $T_j$ = 175–200 °C, **no tail current** (unipolar).
- [x] **GaN HEMT:** lateral, AlGaN/GaN heterostructure, **2DEG** (1500–2000 cm²/V·s), 100–650 V, **100 kHz – 10 MHz**, $Q_{rr} \approx 0$.
- [x] **GaN cascode** = D-mode GaN + low-voltage Si MOSFET in series → standard Si-style gate drive.
- [x] **E-mode GaN (p-GaN gate)** = true normally-off, sensitive to ±10 V gate.
- [x] **Body diode of SiC** has high $V_f$ — often paralleled with a SiC Schottky for low loss.
- [x] **Selection rule:** SiC for **> 1200 V / > 50 kW**; GaN for **≤ 650 V / high frequency / < 5 kW**.
- [x] **Efficiency** of WBG converters: 98–99% (vs 95–97% for Si).
- [x] **Memorize the comparison matrix** — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question.
- [x] **Always include units** in numericals: $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}^2$, MV/cm, W/cm·K, cm²/V·s, °C.
- [x] **Always state the bandgap** when asked to "compare" — it is the root of every other advantage.

> **End of Module-I Topic Notes — WBG Devices (SiC MOSFET & GaN HEMT).**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module-II : Controlled Rectifiers (Single Phase)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Controlled Rectifier** is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed AC supply voltage into a variable DC output voltage by phase-controlled switching of thyristors (SCRs). The output DC voltage is regulated by varying the **firing angle (α)** — the delay between the instant a thyristor becomes forward-biased and the instant it is triggered into conduction.

For single-phase operation, KTU categorizes these into:

- **1-φ Fully Controlled Rectifier (Full Converter):** A full-bridge configuration employing **four thyristors (T1, T2, T3, T4)**. Both halves of the AC wave are controlled, and the output voltage polarity can be reversed by pushing α beyond 90°.
- **1-φ Half-Controlled Rectifier (Semi-Converter):** A bridge employing **two thyristors and two uncontrolled diodes (T1, T2, D1, D2)**. Only the positive half-cycles are phase-controlled; the negative half-cycles freewheel through the diodes.

The load classification is equally critical:

| Load Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-------------|------------------|
| **R** | Pure resistive (heaters, incandescent lamps) |
| **RL** | Resistive + Inductive (DC motor field, solenoids, magnets) |
| **RLE** | Resistive + Inductive + Back-EMF (separately excited DC motor armature) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The presence of an inductive element (L) or a back-EMF source (E) introduces a **conduction overlap**, **freewheeling action**, and the possibility of **discontinuous conduction**. These three effects govern every numerical answer in the University exam.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a controlled rectifier as a **"smart water tap with a delayed knob"** connected to a pulsating AC supply (think of it as water sloshing back and forth in a U-tube).

- A **diode** is a one-way valve that opens automatically the moment water flows forward.
- A **thyristor** is a similar valve, but it has a **manual latch**: even when water is flowing forward, it stays closed until you give it a push (the **gate pulse**) at exactly the right moment.
- The **firing angle α** is the *delay* between the moment the valve "wants" to open and the moment you actually push it.
- A **Fully Controlled Bridge** is two such paired valves on both sides of the U-tube — you control both forward and reverse flow.
- A **Semi-Converter** controls only one side and lets the other side leak freely (via diodes) — like a tap that drains back uncontrolled.
- An **RLE load** (DC motor) is like a heavy flywheel — once spinning, it pushes back (back-EMF **E**). This push-back allows energy to flow *back* into the AC source — this is **Inverter Mode** (regenerative braking of a tram going downhill).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Inverter Mode Matters:** When a loaded crane lowers a heavy weight, the motor acts as a generator. The controlled rectifier allows this energy to be pumped back into the AC mains instead of being burnt as heat — saving electricity and enabling controlled braking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Phase Full Converter — RL Load (Continuous Conduction)

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology

A 1-φ full bridge with four thyristors (T1, T2, T3, T4) connected to a single-phase AC source $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ feeds an RL load.

### 2.1.2 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

- **Pair T1-T2 fires at ωt = α** (during positive half-cycle).
  Current path: Source → T1 → Load → T2 → Source (negative). Output voltage $v_o = v_s$.
- **Pair T3-T4 fires at ωt = π + α** (during negative half-cycle).
  Current path: Source → T3 → Load → T4 → Source. Output voltage $v_o = v_s$ (same polarity across load).
- Because of load inductance L, current cannot become zero the instant $v_s$ crosses zero — thyristors keep conducting until the *next* pair is fired. This guarantees **continuous conduction** for large enough L.

### 2.1.3 Boundary Conditions

- **Rectifier Mode:** $0 < \alpha < 90°$ → average output voltage $V_o > 0$.
- **Inverter Mode:** $90° < \alpha < 180°$ → average output voltage $V_o < 0$ (energy flows from load to source).

## 2.2 Single-Phase Semi-Converter — RL Load

A semi-converter uses two thyristors (T1, T3) on the AC side and two diodes (D2, D4) on the return path.

- When $v_s$ is positive, T1 fires at α → current flows through T1, load, D2.
- When $v_s$ becomes negative, D2 continues to conduct (freewheeling) — load is short-circuited through T1-D2, hence $v_o = 0$.
- T3 fires at π+α → current flows through T3, load, D4.
- Diodes always freewheel when $v_o$ tries to go negative.

> [!NOTE]
> **Freewheeling in Semi-Converters is automatic** (via D2 or D4). In Full Converters, freewheeling is achieved only by firing both thyristors on the same side simultaneously — called *forced freewheeling* or *firing pulse extension*.

## 2.3 RLE Load — The Critical Case

For an RLE load (e.g., DC motor armature):

- The back-EMF E opposes the supply when rectifier delivers power.
- The thyristors only conduct when $v_s > E$ (positive for rectifier mode).
- If L is insufficient, conduction becomes **discontinuous** — load current falls to zero before the next firing pulse.

### 2.3.1 Rectifier vs Inverter Mode (KTU Favourite Question)

| Parameter | Rectifier Mode | Inverter Mode |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Firing angle α | $0° \le \alpha < 90°$ | $90° < \alpha \le 180°$ |
| Average $V_o$ | Positive | Negative |
| Power flow | AC → DC | DC → AC |
| Typical application | DC motor driving | Regenerative braking |
| Extinction angle β = π − α | $90° < \beta \le 180°$ | $0° \le \beta < 90°$ |

## 2.4 Continuous vs Discontinuous Conduction

| Feature | Continuous Conduction (CCM) | Discontinuous Conduction (DCM) |
|---------|----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Condition | Large L or large load current | Small L or light load |
| Current waveform | Always > 0 | Has zero intervals |
| Analysis complexity | Simple (standard formulas apply) | Requires solving for extinction angle β |
| Displacement factor | $\cos(\alpha)$ | $\cos(\alpha + \gamma)/2$ (more complex) |

## 2.5 Power Factor, THD, and Real Power (Continuous, Ripple-Free Current Assumption)

Under the KTU assumption of **continuous conduction with ripple-free (perfectly flat) load current**, the source current becomes a quasi-square wave whose amplitude equals $I_o$. This simplification allows clean derivation of power-quality metrics.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Full Converter (RL/RLE, CCM) | Semi-Converter (RL, CCM) |
|---|----------|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Average $V_o$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos(\alpha)$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos \alpha)$ |
| 2 | RMS $V_{o,rms}$ | $V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$ | $V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$ |
| 3 | Average $I_o$ (RLE) | $\dfrac{2 V_m \cos \alpha - \pi E}{\pi R}$ | $\dfrac{V_m(1+\cos\alpha) - \pi E}{\pi R}$ |
| 4 | Source RMS current $I_{s,rms}$ | $I_o$ (square wave, ±Io) | $I_o$ |
| 5 | Fundamental RMS current $I_{s1}$ | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_o$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_o \sqrt{1+\cos^2 \alpha - 2\cos\alpha \cos 2\alpha}$... (uses Fourier) |
| 6 | Displacement Factor | $\cos \alpha$ | $\cos(\alpha/2)$ |
| 7 | Distortion Factor | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}$ | function of α |
| 8 | Power Factor (PF) | $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos \alpha$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{2}(1+\cos\alpha)}{\sqrt{\pi(\pi-\alpha)+(\pi/2)\sin 2\alpha}}$ |
| 9 | Real Power $P$ | $V_{s,rms} \cdot I_{s,rms} \cdot \cos\alpha$ (full bridge) | $V_{s,rms} \cdot I_{s,rms} \cdot \cos(\alpha/2)$ |
| 10 | THD (full conv., square wave) | $\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 - 1} \approx 48.3\%$ | $\approx 75\%$ (semi-converter) |
| 11 | Source inductance effect | $\cos \alpha \longrightarrow \cos(\alpha + \mu)$ | — |
| 12 | Overlap angle μ | $\cos(\alpha + \mu) = \cos\alpha - \dfrac{2 \omega L_s I_o}{V_m}$ | — |
| 13 | $V_o$ with source inductance | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos(\alpha+\mu) - E$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverter Mode Notation:** KTU examiners frequently use the **extinction angle β** where $\beta = \pi - \alpha$. In inverter mode, the average output voltage is $V_o = -\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\beta$. Always be ready to interconvert between α and β in answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Average Output Voltage of 1-φ Full Converter with RL Load (Continuous Conduction)

### Derivation (Exhaustive)

The supply voltage is $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
T1-T2 are fired at ωt = α, and they conduct from α to (π + α).
Output voltage $v_o = v_s$ during this interval.

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

Step 1: Pull out the constant $V_m$.

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

Step 2: Perform the integration. Recall $\int \sin(x) dx = -\cos(x)$.

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha}
$$

Step 3: Apply the limits.

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi+\alpha) + \cos(\alpha) \right]
$$

Step 4: Use the identity $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos \alpha$.

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ \cos\alpha + \cos\alpha \right] = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha}
$$

**Engineering Utility:** This single equation is the *master equation* for DC motor speed control via armature voltage control using a full converter. Set α = 0° → maximum positive voltage; α = 90° → zero; α = 180° → maximum negative voltage.

## 3.2 Average Output Voltage of 1-φ Semi-Converter with RL Load

### Derivation

T1 fires at α → conducts from α to π (output = $v_s$).
Then D2 freewheels from π to π + α (output = 0).
T3 fires at π + α → conducts from π+α to 2π (output = |v_s|).
D4 freewheels from 2π to 2π+α (output = 0).

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

Step 1: Integrate.

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

Step 2: Apply limits.

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos\pi + \cos\alpha\right] = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[1 + \cos\alpha\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Semi-converter voltage varies from $2 V_m / \pi$ (at α = 0) to **0** (at α = π). It **cannot** produce negative output — hence semi-converters are *purely rectifiers*; they have **no inverter mode**.

## 3.3 Average Output Voltage of 1-φ Full Converter with RLE Load

For RLE load, the back-EMF E opposes the output. The average load voltage becomes:

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) - E
$$

(The −E accounts for the steady DC drop across the load's back-EMF source.)

$$
V_o = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - E
$$

Applying KVL across the RLE load for average quantities:

$$
V_o = I_o R + E \quad\Rightarrow\quad I_o = \frac{V_o - E}{R}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{I_o = \frac{2 V_m \cos\alpha - \pi E}{\pi R}}
$$

### Inverter Mode Derivation

When α > 90°, $\cos\alpha$ is negative → $V_o < 0$ → $I_o < 0$ (current reverses).
This means power flows from DC side to AC side — the converter acts as a **line-commutated inverter**.
Using $\beta = \pi - \alpha$:

$$
V_o = -\frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\beta
$$

The negative sign indicates reversal. To prevent commutation failure (a KTU favourite!), β must satisfy:

$$
\beta \geq \gamma + \delta
$$

where γ = margin angle (~10–15°), δ = turn-off time of thyristor (~50 μs).

## 3.4 Input Line Current, Real Power, Power Factor, and THD

### Step-by-Step Derivation (Full Converter, CCM, Ripple-Free Current)

**Assumption:** Load current is constant $I_o$ (ripple-free). Hence source current is a **square wave of amplitude ±I_o** lagging the supply voltage by α.

Step 1: Fourier series of the square source current:

$$
i_s(\omega t) = \frac{4 I_o}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5...}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} \sin(n\omega t - n\alpha)
$$

Step 2: RMS value of the square wave (independent of α):

$$
I_{s,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} I_o^2\, d(\omega t)} = I_o
$$

Step 3: Fundamental component RMS:

$$
I_{s1,rms} = \frac{4 I_o}{\pi \sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_o \approx 0.9 \, I_o
$$

Step 4: Displacement Factor (cosine of angle between fundamental current and supply voltage):

$$
DF = \cos\alpha
$$

Step 5: Distortion Factor (ratio of fundamental RMS to total RMS):

$$
\text{Distortion Factor} = \frac{I_{s1,rms}}{I_{s,rms}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} I_o \cdot \frac{1}{I_o} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}
$$

Step 6: Power Factor:

$$
PF = \frac{I_{s1,rms}}{I_{s,rms}} \cdot \cos\alpha = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

Step 7: Real Power (using $P = V_{s,rms} \cdot I_{s1,rms} \cdot \cos\alpha$):

$$
P = V_{s,rms} \cdot I_o \cdot \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

Step 8: Apparent Power:

$$
S = V_{s,rms} \cdot I_{s,rms} = V_{s,rms} \cdot I_o
$$

Step 9: THD:

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{I_{s,rms}^2 - I_{s1,rms}^2}}{I_{s1,rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{(2\sqrt{2}/\pi)^2} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \approx 0.4834
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{THD}_{\text{full converter}} \approx 48.34\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant THD Insight:** For a full converter with ripple-free current, THD is **independent of α**! This is a powerful conclusion often asked in KTU viva. Only the *displacement* of the current changes with α; the *shape* remains a square wave.

### Semi-Converter Power Factor Derivation

For semi-converter, source current is a **quasi-square wave with width (π − α)**.
Through similar Fourier analysis:

$$
PF_{\text{semi}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}(1+\cos\alpha)}{\sqrt{\pi(\pi-\alpha) + \frac{\pi}{2}\sin 2\alpha}}
$$

## 3.5 Effect of Source Inductance (Full Converter, Continuous Conduction, Ripple-Free Current)

### 3.5.1 The Commutation Overlap Phenomenon

In practice, every AC source has a finite inductance $L_s$. During the transfer of current from one thyristor pair (say T1-T2) to the next pair (T3-T4), both pairs conduct simultaneously for a short interval called the **overlap angle μ**. This is because the line current cannot change instantaneously through $L_s$.

### 3.5.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Overlap Angle

During overlap, the source is short-circuited through the two thyristor pairs. The loop equation:

$$
v_s = L_s \frac{di}{dt} - L_s \frac{di'}{dt} = 2 L_s \frac{di_{sc}}{dt}
$$

where $i_{sc}$ is the circulating current during overlap. Integrate from α to α+μ:

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = 2 \omega L_s \int_{0}^{I_o} di_{sc} = 2 \omega L_s I_o
$$

Left side integration:

$$
V_m\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\mu} = V_m\left[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right]
$$

Equating:

$$
V_m\left[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right] = 2 \omega L_s I_o
$$

Solving for μ:

$$
\boxed{\cos(\alpha + \mu) = \cos\alpha - \frac{2 \omega L_s I_o}{V_m}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mu = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\cos\alpha - \frac{2 \omega L_s I_o}{V_m}\right) - \alpha}
$$

### 3.5.3 Modified Average Output Voltage

During overlap, $v_o = 0$ (the source is shorted). The reduction in average voltage:

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[\cos\alpha - \cos(\alpha+\mu)\right] = \frac{2 \omega L_s I_o}{\pi}
$$

Therefore the corrected average output voltage:

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos(\alpha + \mu) - E}
$$

(For RLE load. For R load, set $E = 0$ and $I_o = V_o / R$ → must solve self-consistently.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — Why μ matters in HVDC:** In HVDC transmission systems, source inductance causes a measurable reduction in DC link voltage and increases reactive power consumption. Engineers compensate by advancing α slightly to maintain the same $V_o$.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 1-φ Full Converter Circuit (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[AC SOURCE vs equals Vm sin omega t] --> Ls1[Source Inductance Ls]
    Ls1 --> T1[T1 Thyristor]
    Ls1 --> T3[T3 Thyristor]
    T1 --> J1[Node A]
    T3 --> J1
    J1 --> R[R Load Resistance]
    R --> L[Load Inductance L]
    L --> E[Back EMF Source E]
    E --> J2[Node B]
    J2 --> T2[T2 Thyristor]
    J2 --> T4[T4 Thyristor]
    T2 --> Ls2[Source Inductance Ls]
    T4 --> Ls2
    Ls2 --> AC
    G1[Gate pulse alpha at omega t equals alpha] -.-> T1
    G2[Gate pulse alpha at omega t equals pi plus alpha] -.-> T3
    G3[Gate pulse at pi plus alpha] -.-> T2
    G4[Gate pulse at 2pi plus alpha] -.-> T4
```

## 4.2 1-φ Semi-Converter Circuit (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[AC SOURCE vs equals Vm sin omega t] --> Ls[Source Inductance Ls]
    Ls --> T1[T1 Thyristor]
    Ls --> T3[T3 Thyristor]
    T1 --> J1[Node A]
    T3 --> J1
    J1 --> R[R Load]
    R --> L[Load Inductance L]
    L --> E[Back EMF E]
    E --> J2[Node B]
    J2 --> D2[Diode D2]
    J2 --> D4[Diode D4]
    D2 --> Ls
    D4 --> Ls
    GA[Gate pulse at alpha] -.-> T1
    GB[Gate pulse at pi plus alpha] -.-> T3
```

## 4.3 Waveform Phasing Diagram (Mermaid Gantt-Style)

```mermaid
gantt

    dateFormat  X

    Negative Half (pi to 2pi) :a2, 180, 180

```

## 4.4 Source-Inductance Overlap Timing Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Start of commutation at omega t equals alpha] --> A[T1-T2 carrying full current Io]
    A --> B[Overlap interval alpha to alpha plus mu]
    B --> C[Both pairs conduct source shorted]
    C --> D[T1-T2 fully OFF at omega t equals alpha plus mu]
    D --> E[T3-T4 carries full current Io]
    E --> F[Next commutation at omega t equals pi plus alpha]

    style A fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#fbb,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#bfb,stroke:#333
```

## 4.5 Functional Flow — Rectifier Mode vs Inverter Mode

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Firing Angle alpha] --> B{alpha less than 90}
    B -->|YES| C[RECTIFIER MODE]
    B -->|NO| D[INVERTER MODE]
    C --> E[Vo positive, AC to DC power flow]
    D --> F[Vo negative, DC to AC power flow]
    E --> G[DC Motor driving load]
    F --> H[DC Motor regenerative braking]
    G --> I[Typical alpha equals 30 deg]
    H --> J[Typical alpha equals 150 deg beta equals 30 deg]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block diagrams above are valid functional substitutes for physical circuit drawings** as per KTU 2024 scheme, since handwritten circuit schematics are typically tested only in the lab component, not the theory exam.
