## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**"What is a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)? How is it different from a Current Source Inverter (CSI)?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** is a power electronic converter that converts a **fixed DC input voltage** into a **variable-magnitude, variable-frequency AC output voltage** using fully controllable switches. The DC input is shunted by a **large filter capacitor**, which makes the DC link behave as a **stiff voltage source** (constant $V_s$ regardless of load current direction).

**Differences:**
- **VSI:** DC-link energy storage = **capacitor**; input quantity = **stiff voltage**; switches must have **anti-parallel diodes** (to carry reverse reactive current); output is a **voltage waveform**.
- **CSI:** DC-link energy storage = **inductor**; input quantity = **stiff current**; switches must have **reverse voltage blocking** (series diode with each switch); output is a **current waveform**.

**Key distinguishing feature:** the DC-link energy storage element (capacitor → VSI; inductor → CSI).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**"Define Modulation Index ($m_a$) and Frequency Modulation Ratio ($m_f$) in SPWM. What is their typical range?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$
m_a = \frac{\hat{V}_r}{\hat{V}_c} = \frac{\text{Peak amplitude of sinusoidal reference wave}}{\text{Peak amplitude of triangular carrier wave}}
$$
$$
m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_1} = \frac{\text{Carrier (switching) frequency}}{\text{Fundamental (reference) frequency}}
$$

**Typical Ranges:**
- $m_a$: **0 to 1** in **linear region**; $1 < m_a < 4/\pi$ in **over-modulation**; effectively **infinite** in **square-wave mode**.
- $m_f$: **integer ≥ 21** (must be **odd** for bipolar and even multiple for unipolar to avoid DC and even harmonics); practical industrial range is **21 to 105** (e.g., 2 kHz carrier at 50 Hz reference gives $m_f = 40$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. (A) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **operation of a single-phase full-bridge Voltage Source Inverter** in **bipolar PWM** mode with a neat circuit diagram and switching waveforms. Derive the expression for the **peak fundamental output voltage** in the linear modulation region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat circuit, explain the **unipolar PWM** scheme for the same full-bridge VSI. Compare its **harmonic spectrum** with bipolar PWM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Full-Bridge Bipolar PWM Operation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description:** A single-phase full-bridge VSI consists of **4 IGBT switches** $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ arranged in two legs. **$T_1$–$T_2$** form the **left leg**; **$T_3$–$T_4$** form the **right leg**. The load is connected between the midpoints **A** (between $T_1, T_2$) and **B** (between $T_3, T_4$). Anti-parallel diodes $D_1$–$D_4$ provide the path for **inductive load currents**. DC bus voltage = $V_s$.

**Switching Logic:** A sine reference $v_r = \hat{V}_r \sin(\omega_1 t)$ is compared with a triangular carrier $v_c$ of amplitude $\hat{V}_c$ and frequency $f_c$.
- When $v_r > v_c$: turn **ON $T_1$ and $T_4$**, turn **OFF $T_2$ and $T_3$** → $v_o = +V_s$.
- When $v_r < v_c$: turn **ON $T_2$ and $T_3$**, turn **OFF $T_1$ and $T_4$** → $v_o = -V_s$.

Thus, the output is a **2-level waveform** (only $+V_s$ and $-V_s$, no zero state). The switches in each leg operate **complementarily** with a small **dead time** $t_d$.

**Derivation of Peak Fundamental Voltage:**

In linear modulation, the **average output voltage over a carrier cycle** follows the reference. Using **Bennett's double Fourier integral** for 2-level PWM, the output is:

$$
v_o(t) = m_a V_s \sin(\omega_1 t) + \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{j} \cos(j \pi) \left[ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} J_k(j m_a \pi) \sin\big((j m_f + k)\omega_1 t\big) \right]
$$

The **fundamental component** ($j=0, k=1$ or directly the DC term in the double series) gives:
$$
\boxed{\hat{V}_{o,1} = m_a V_s}
$$

This shows **linear control** of the fundamental amplitude by the modulation index in the range $0 \le m_a \le 1$.

**Harmonic Locations:** $n = j m_f \pm k$ for $j = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and $k = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$. The **first dominant cluster** is at $n = m_f$ (i.e., at carrier frequency).

**(b) Unipolar PWM and Harmonic Comparison (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description:** Same full-bridge VSI hardware, but **two reference signals** are used: $v_{r1} = \hat{V}_r \sin(\omega_1 t)$ modulates the **left leg**; $v_{r2} = -\hat{V}_r \sin(\omega_1 t)$ modulates the **right leg**.

**Switching Logic for Left Leg:**
- When $v_{r1} > v_c$: $T_1$ ON, $T_2$ OFF → $v_{AN} = +V_s$
- When $v_{r1} < v_c$: $T_2$ ON, $T_1$ OFF → $v_{AN} = 0$

**Switching Logic for Right Leg:**
- When $v_{r2} > v_c$: $T_3$ ON, $T_4$ OFF → $v_{BN} = +V_s$
- When $v_{r2} < v_c$: $T_4$ ON, $T_3$ OFF → $v_{BN} = 0$

**Output Voltage:** $v_o = v_{AN} - v_{BN}$, producing **3 levels** ($+V_s, 0, -V_s$) per fundamental cycle.

**Harmonic Locations:** $n = 2 j m_f \pm k$ for $j = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ and $k = 1, 3, 5, \ldots$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Bipolar | Unipolar |
|---|---|---|
| Output levels | 2 | 3 |
| First harmonic cluster | $m_f$ | $2 m_f$ |
| THD (same $m_f, m_a$) | Higher | Lower |
| Filter size | Larger | Smaller |
| Switching losses per leg | Same | Same |

**Key advantage of unipolar:** Harmonics shift to **double the carrier frequency**, making output filtering easier and EMI lower.

---

**(B) Alternative Choice for Q3 — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **operation of a single-phase half-bridge VSI** with a circuit diagram. Derive the **peak fundamental output voltage** for square-wave operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **need for dead time (blanking time)** in a VSI phase leg. Derive the **expression for the dead-time voltage error** per half-cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Half-Bridge VSI Operation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description:** The half-bridge VSI uses **2 IGBTs** ($T_1$ top, $T_2$ bottom) and **2 equal DC-link capacitors** ($C_1 = C_2 = C$) with the load connected between the **switch midpoint (A)** and the **capacitor midpoint (O)**. The total DC bus is $V_s$; each capacitor holds $V_s/2$.

**Switching Logic (square-wave mode):**
- $T_1$ ON, $T_2$ OFF for half cycle: $v_o = +V_s/2$
- $T_2$ ON, $T_1$ OFF for the other half cycle: $v_o = -V_s/2$
- Switching period = $T_1 = 1/f_1$ (no high-frequency PWM).

**Fourier Series Derivation:**

A periodic square wave of amplitude $\pm V_s/2$ with period $T_1$ is an **odd function** (assuming symmetry), so the Fourier series contains only sine terms:

$$
v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} b_n \sin(n \omega_1 t)
$$

where
$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T_1} \int_0^{T_1} v_o(t) \sin(n \omega_1 t) \, dt
$$

For the half-bridge square wave, with $v_o = +V_s/2$ for $0 < t < T_1/2$ and $v_o = -V_s/2$ for $T_1/2 < t < T_1$:

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T_1} \left[ \int_0^{T_1/2} \frac{V_s}{2} \sin(n \omega_1 t) \, dt + \int_{T_1/2}^{T_1} \left(-\frac{V_s}{2}\right) \sin(n \omega_1 t) \, dt \right]
$$

By symmetry of the square wave, $b_n = \frac{4}{T_1} \int_0^{T_1/2} \frac{V_s}{2} \sin(n \omega_1 t) \, dt$.

Computing the integral:
$$
b_n = \frac{2 V_s}{T_1} \left[ -\frac{\cos(n \omega_1 t)}{n \omega_1} \right]_0^{T_1/2} = \frac{2 V_s}{T_1 n \omega_1} \big[ 1 - \cos(n \pi) \big]
$$

Since $\omega_1 T_1 = 2\pi$:
$$
b_n = \frac{V_s}{n \pi} \big[ 1 - \cos(n \pi) \big]
$$

For $n$ **odd**: $\cos(n\pi) = -1$, so $b_n = \dfrac{2 V_s}{n \pi}$.
For $n$ **even**: $\cos(n\pi) = +1$, so $b_n = 0$.

The fundamental ($n=1$) amplitude is:
$$
\boxed{\hat{V}_{o,1} = \frac{2 V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \, V_s}
$$

which is **half** of the full-bridge value $4V_s/\pi$.

**(b) Dead Time — Need and Voltage Error (7 marks):**

**Need for Dead Time:** In a phase leg of the VSI (e.g., $T_1$ top, $T_2$ bottom), the two switches are driven **complementarily**. Due to the **finite turn-off time** of semiconductor devices (IGBTs take ~0.5–2 μs to turn off), there is a finite window where one switch has not yet turned off while the complementary switch is being turned on. If both are ON simultaneously, the DC bus is **short-circuited** (called **shoot-through**), causing a massive current spike that destroys the switches.

To prevent this, a **blanking time** $t_d$ (typically 1–5 μs) is inserted between the turn-off command of one switch and the turn-on command of the other.

**Effect on Output Voltage:** During the dead time, the phase leg output is **not controlled by the gate signals** but is determined by the **load current direction** through the anti-parallel diodes:
- If $i_o > 0$ (current leaving the leg): the **lower diode ($D_2$)** conducts → output = $0$ (or $-V_s/2$ depending on configuration).
- If $i_o < 0$ (current entering the leg): the **upper diode ($D_1$)** conducts → output = $V_s$ (or $+V_s/2$).

**Derivation of Voltage Error:**

For a bipolar PWM full-bridge, in each carrier cycle, there are **2 dead-time intervals** (one for each leg transition). In each dead-time interval, the output deviates from the ideal by $\pm V_s$ for a duration $t_d$.

The **average voltage error per fundamental cycle** is:
$$
\Delta V_o = 4 V_s \cdot t_d \cdot f_1
$$

**Derivation:** The number of carrier cycles per fundamental cycle = $m_f$. In each carrier cycle, there are 2 dead-time events. Each event introduces a voltage deviation of $V_s$ for time $t_d$. Total dead time per fundamental cycle = $2 m_f t_d$. Average voltage loss = $V_s \times 2 m_f t_d \times f_1 = 2 V_s t_d f_c$. Accounting for both legs and both polarities of current over a full cycle (factor of 2): $\Delta V_o = 4 V_s t_d f_1$.

**Practical Impact:**
- **Low-order harmonics** (3rd, 5th, 7th) appear in the output — these are **not present** in ideal PWM and are **hard to filter** (close to fundamental).
- **Dead-time compensation** is required in high-performance drives: it adds/subtracts a pulse of duration $t_d$ to the gating signal, based on the polarity of the measured load current.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $V_s/2$ vs $V_s$:** Half-bridge fundamental uses $V_s/2$; full-bridge uses $V_s$. Many students write $4V_s/\pi$ for half-bridge — **wrong by a factor of 2** (correct is $2V_s/\pi$).
> 2. **Confusing $m_f$ and $m_a$:** $m_f$ controls **frequency ratio** (harmonic locations); $m_a$ controls **amplitude** of fundamental. Do not interchange in the answer.
> 3. **Forgetting Bessel functions:** When asked to "derive harmonic spectrum," you must mention that the **sideband amplitudes** are given by Bessel functions $J_k(j m_a \pi)$. A 1-mark bonus is often given for mentioning this.
> 4. **Dead time adds harmonics, doesn't remove:** Do not write "dead time reduces distortion."
> 5. **Square wave mode is NOT PWM:** When $m_a \to \infty$, the carrier becomes irrelevant; output is a **50% duty square wave**. Students often incorrectly apply the formula $\hat{V}_{o,1} = m_a V_s$ to square-wave mode.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **VSI = stiff DC voltage** (large $C_{DC}$); **CSI = stiff DC current** (large $L_{DC}$).
- [x] **Half-Bridge VSI** = 2 switches, 2 capacitors, output $\pm V_s/2$; **Full-Bridge** = 4 switches, output $\pm V_s$ (or 3-level with unipolar).
- [x] **$m_a$ (Modulation Index)** = $\hat{V}_r / \hat{V}_c$, controls **fundamental amplitude** linearly in $0 \le m_a \le 1$.
- [x] **$m_f$ (Frequency Ratio)** = $f_c / f_1$, controls **harmonic cluster locations**.
- [x] **Linear region** ($m_a \le 1$): $\hat{V}_{o,1} = m_a V_s$ (full-bridge) or $m_a V_s/2$ (half-bridge).
- [x] **Over-modulation** ($1 < m_a < 4/\pi$): nonlinear; fundamental grows slowly.
- [x] **Square wave mode** ($m_a \to \infty$): $\hat{V}_{o,1} = 4V_s/\pi$ (full-bridge); cannot vary amplitude.
- [x] **Bipolar PWM**: 2 levels ($+V_s, -V_s$); first cluster at $m_f$.
- [x] **Unipolar PWM**: 3 levels ($+V_s, 0, -V_s$); first cluster at $2 m_f$ (cleaner spectrum).
- [x] **Harmonic formula (bipolar)**: $n = j m_f \pm k$ (odd $k$).
- [x] **Harmonic formula (unipolar)**: $n = 2 j m_f \pm k$ (odd $k$).
- [x] **Sideband amplitudes**: given by Bessel function $J_k(j m_a \pi)$.
- [x] **Dead time** $t_d$: prevents shoot-through, **adds** low-order harmonics.
- [x] **Dead-time error per cycle**: $\Delta V_o = 4 V_s t_d f_1$.
- [x] **Over-modulation gain**: max extra 27.32% in fundamental ($4/\pi \approx 1.273$).
- [x] **Linear PWM merits**: low THD, linear control, easy filtering; **demerit**: high switching losses.
- [x] **Square wave merits**: highest fundamental utilization, lowest losses; **demerit**: no amplitude control, high THD (~48%).
- [x] **Half-bridge merits**: simple, fewer switches; **demerits**: low output voltage, capacitor imbalance.
- [x] **Full-bridge merits**: double the voltage, 3-level unipolar option; **demerits**: more switches, gate complexity.
- [x] **Anti-parallel diodes** are essential in VSI for **reactive power flow**.
- [x] **Anti-shoot-through interlock** is a hardware protection; **dead time** is its software/logic implementation.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:**
> When a 14-mark question asks "Explain X with waveforms and derive Y," always:
> 1. **Draw the circuit** (1–2 marks).
> 2. **List switching states** in a table (2 marks).
> 3. **Sketch 2–3 waveforms** (modulating, carrier, output) (2 marks).
> 4. **State the formula** for fundamental (1 mark).
> 5. **Derive step-by-step** using Fourier analysis (4–5 marks).
> 6. **Discuss merits/demerits or harmonic spectrum** (2–3 marks).
> Missing any one of these costs you ~2 marks. Structure your answer using these sub-headings explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three-Phase Pulse Width Modulated VSI - Fundamental Output Voltage, Linear, Over Modulation and Square Wave Modes, Third Harmonic Injection PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives
> - **Module:** Module- III
> - **Topic:** Three-Phase Pulse Width Modulated VSI - Fundamental Output Voltage, Linear, Over Modulation and Square Wave Modes, Third Harmonic Injection PWM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Three-Phase Pulse Width Modulated Voltage Source Inverter (PWM VSI)** is a power electronic converter that synthesizes a controllable three-phase AC output voltage from a fixed DC link voltage $V_{dc}$ by rapidly switching six power semiconductor devices (typically IGBTs) in a complementary fashion. The switching instants are determined by comparing three sinusoidal **reference signals** (one per phase, displaced by 120°) with a high-frequency **triangular carrier wave**.

The **modulation index** $m_a$ is the controlling parameter defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the control (reference) signal to the peak amplitude of the triangular carrier:

$$m_a = \frac{\hat{V}_{control}}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$$

The **frequency modulation ratio** is defined as:

$$m_f = \frac{f_{tri}}{f_{control}}$$

For sinusoidal PWM, the **fundamental RMS line-to-line output voltage** in the *linear region* ($m_a \leq 1$) is:

$$V_{LL,1(rms)} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \, m_a \, V_{dc} = 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The three operating regimes of a three-phase VSI — **Linear Modulation ($0 \leq m_a \leq 1$)**, **Overmodulation ($m_a > 1$)**, and **Six-Step / Square-Wave Mode ($m_a \rightarrow \infty$)** — are routinely asked as 7-mark derivations. The technique of **Third Harmonic Injection PWM (THIPWM)** is a *direct-syllabus* subtopic that allows extension of the linear range by **15.47 %**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three-Mixer Smoothie Shop"

Imagine a juice bar where you have **three blenders** (Phases A, B, C) and you want each to produce a smooth sinusoidal speed profile (the desired AC output) for a viscous fluid.

- The **triangular carrier** is a strict shop manager with a stopwatch, telling the blender when to switch ON and OFF in tiny intervals.
- The **sinusoidal reference** is the customer’s desired speed curve for each blender.
- The **comparator** is the head chef: *"If the recipe demands more than the current tick, run the blender; else, stop it."*
- The resulting **chopped waveform** when filtered by the motor’s inductance looks like a smooth sine wave — this is the fundamental component.

Now, three operating "modes" exist:

| Mode | Analogy | Effect |
|------|---------|--------|
| **Linear PWM** | Gentle stirring within limits | Output voltage scales linearly with $m_a$ |
| **Overmodulation** | Stirring so hard the recipe exceeds the cap | Output saturates; harmonics rise |
| **Square-wave** | Full power ON / OFF 50 % duty | Maximum fundamental, but maximum distortion |

**Third Harmonic Injection** is like adding a *secret* ingredient (a 3× frequency component) to each recipe. Individually, each blender gets this odd extra pulse, but when the three outputs are *subtracted from each other* (line voltage), the secret ingredient **vanishes** — but in the meantime, the chef can add 15 % more main ingredient (fundamental) without saturating!

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three-phase PWM VSIs form the backbone of:
> - **AC motor drives** (induction & PMSM) — variable speed, regenerative braking.
> - **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** — clean sinusoidal output.
> - **Renewable energy grid-tie inverters** — solar PV, wind.
> - **Active front-end rectifiers (bidirectional)**.
> 
> The DC-bus utilization and harmonic performance directly determine **motor torque ripple, switching losses, and acoustic noise**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Three-Phase VSI

A standard two-level, three-phase VSI consists of **six switches** ($S_1 \ldots S_6$) arranged in three legs. Each leg has an upper and lower switch operated **complementarily** to prevent DC-link short circuits.

### 2.1.1 Switching States and Pole Voltages

Each leg can produce two states, giving **8 possible switching states** ($2^3 = 8$):
- 6 **active states** — produce non-zero line voltages.
- 2 **zero states** — (111) and (000) — short the load through upper or lower switches.

The **pole voltage** of phase A (with respect to the negative DC rail) is:

$$v_{aN} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \quad \text{when } S_1 \text{ ON} \qquad v_{aN} = -\frac{V_{dc}}{2} \quad \text{when } S_4 \text{ ON}$$

Similarly for phases B and C, with reference signals 120° apart.

### 2.1.2 Logical Flow of PWM Generation

1. **Generate three balanced sinusoidal references:**
   $v_{a}^* = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t)$
   $v_{b}^* = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$
   $v_{c}^* = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$
2. **Generate a high-frequency triangular carrier** $v_{tri}$ of amplitude $\hat{V}_{tri}$ and frequency $f_{tri}$.
3. **Compare** $v_a^*$ with $v_{tri}$: if $v_a^* > v_{tri}$, fire upper switch of leg A; else fire lower.
4. Repeat independently for legs B and C.
5. Output **pole voltages** are $v_{aN}, v_{bN}, v_{cN}$. The line-to-line voltages are obtained as differences:
   $v_{ab} = v_{aN} - v_{bN}$ and so on.

## 2.2 The Three Operating Regimes

### 2.2.1 Linear Modulation Region ($0 \leq m_a \leq 1$)

- The reference signal **never exceeds** the triangular carrier.
- The **fundamental component** of the output varies **linearly** with $m_a$.
- The DC-bus utilization is limited to:
  - **Line-to-line peak:** $\hat{V}_{LL,1,\max} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} V_{dc} \approx 0.866 \, V_{dc}$
  - **Line-to-line RMS:** $V_{LL,1,\max(rms)} \approx 0.612 \, V_{dc}$
- The PWM waveform is "well-behaved" — the dominant harmonic clusters are at $m_f$ and its sidebands.

### 2.2.2 Overmodulation Region ($m_a > 1$)

- The reference signal **exceeds** the carrier for part of the cycle, causing the **inverter to dwell in the zero state** for those portions.
- The output fundamental **no longer rises linearly** with $m_a$.
- The relationship between $m_a$ and the fundamental is now **non-linear** and the **harmonic content increases significantly**.
- Typically divided into two sub-regions:
  - *Mode 1 Overmodulation* ($1 < m_a \leq 1.27$): pulsed output with some zero-state intervals.
  - *Mode 2 Overmodulation* ($1.27 \leq m_a < 3.24$): the active states are *chopped* at the boundaries — pulses shrink to a point.
- At $m_a = 1.27$ (numerically $4/\pi$): the output **degenerates to a six-step square wave**.

### 2.2.3 Six-Step / Square-Wave Mode ($m_a \rightarrow \infty$)

- Each switch is ON for **180°** per fundamental cycle; the line-to-line voltage becomes a **6-step waveform** (not a square wave — a pure square wave would be 2-step, hence the *six-step* nomenclature).
- **Maximum possible fundamental output** is reached:

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1,6\text{-step}} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 1.103 \, V_{dc}$$

- Harmonic spectrum contains only **non-triplen odd harmonics**: 5th, 7th, 11th, 13th … of amplitudes $\dfrac{1}{n}$ of the fundamental.
- Switching losses are minimum (each device switches once per cycle), but **THD is high** (~31 % for line current).

### 2.2.4 Comparison Summary

| Parameter | Linear ($m_a = 1$) | Overmodulation | Square-Wave |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|-------------|
| $V_{LL,1}$ peak | $0.866 \, V_{dc}$ | $0.866 \to 1.103 \, V_{dc}$ | $1.103 \, V_{dc}$ |
| Linearity | Yes | No (non-linear) | Saturates |
| Switching loss | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| Output THD | Lowest | High | Highest ($\sim 31\%$) |
| Application | High-quality drives | Field-weakening region | Cruise / constant power |

## 2.3 Third Harmonic Injection PWM (THIPWM)

### 2.3.1 The Core Idea

In a balanced three-phase system, **triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th, …)** in the phase voltages **cancel out** in the line voltages:

$$v_{ab,3\omega} = v_{aN,3\omega} - v_{bN,3\omega} = V_3 \sin 3\omega t - V_3 \sin(3\omega t - 360°) = 0$$

Hence we can *deliberately* inject a 3rd-harmonic component into each reference — it disappears in $v_{ab}$ but **flattens the peak of the reference waveform**, allowing a higher $m_a$ in the linear region.

### 2.3.2 Optimal Reference Signal

The optimal injection is **1/6 of the fundamental amplitude**:

$$v_a^*(t) = \hat{V}_1 \left[ \sin(\omega t) + \dfrac{1}{6} \sin(3\omega t) \right]$$

This produces a **flat-topped** reference with peak value reduced by a factor of $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ relative to a pure sinusoid of the same fundamental amplitude. Consequently, the **maximum linear modulation index** rises to:

$$m_{a,\max}^{\text{THIPWM}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$$

At this value, the **fundamental line-to-line peak reaches $V_{dc}$** (a 15.47 % improvement over SPWM).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-arsenal**. Memorize these and the derivations in Section 3.

| # | Quantity | Expression | Range / Condition |
|---|----------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Amplitude Modulation Index | $m_a = \hat{V}_{control} / \hat{V}_{tri}$ | $0 \leq m_a \leq 1$ (linear) |
| 2 | Frequency Modulation Ratio | $m_f = f_{tri} / f_{control}$ | $\geq 15$ (synchronous), $m_f = 3k$ preferred |
| 3 | SPWM phase voltage fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{ph,1} = m_a \, V_{dc} / 2$ | Linear, $m_a \leq 1$ |
| 4 | SPWM line voltage fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{LL,1} = \sqrt{3} \, m_a \, V_{dc} / 2$ | Linear, $m_a \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Max line voltage (linear SPWM) | $\hat{V}_{LL,1,\max} = \sqrt{3} \, V_{dc} / 2 \approx 0.866 \, V_{dc}$ | $m_a = 1$ |
| 6 | Max RMS line voltage (linear SPWM) | $V_{LL,1,\max}^{rms} = \sqrt{3} \, V_{dc} / (2\sqrt{2}) \approx 0.612 \, V_{dc}$ | $m_a = 1$ |
| 7 | Square-wave phase voltage fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{ph,1}^{sq} = 2 V_{dc} / \pi$ | $m_a \rightarrow \infty$ |
| 8 | Square-wave line voltage fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{sq} = 2\sqrt{3} \, V_{dc} / \pi \approx 1.103 \, V_{dc}$ | $m_a \rightarrow \infty$ |
| 9 | Square-wave line voltage RMS | $V_{LL,1}^{sq,rms} = \sqrt{6} \, V_{dc} / \pi \approx 0.780 \, V_{dc}$ | $m_a \rightarrow \infty$ |
| 10 | Maximum $m_a$ with THIPWM | $m_{a,\max} = 2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.155$ | Linear, flat-topped |
| 11 | THIPWM phase voltage fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{ph,1} = V_{dc} / \sqrt{3}$ | $m_a = 2/\sqrt{3}$ |
| 12 | THIPWM line voltage fundamental peak | $\hat{V}_{LL,1} = V_{dc}$ | $m_a = 2/\sqrt{3}$ |
| 13 | Harmonic order (line voltage, SPWM) | $n = m_f \pm 2, \, m_f \pm 4, \, 2m_f \pm 1, \ldots$ | Synchronous PWM |
| 14 | Dominant harmonic amplitude | $V_n = V_{dc} / (n\pi)$ (square wave) | $n = 5,7,11,13,\ldots$ |
| 15 | Switching losses (linear) | $P_{sw} \propto f_{sw}$ | $f_{sw} = m_f f_{fundamental}$ |

**Mnemonic — “Double-Root-Three” Rule:** A three-phase VSI delivers a line voltage that is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage — this single factor appears in **every** formula above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The 15.47 % boost from THIPWM is precisely why industrial drives (ABB, Siemens, Schneider) implement this in their firmware — it directly translates to higher motor torque in the constant-torque region, smaller DC bus capacitor, and lower IGBT current rating for the same power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fundamental Output Voltage of SPWM in Linear Region

We aim to derive $\hat{V}_{ph,1} = m_a V_{dc} / 2$.

**Step 1 — Define the pole voltage waveform.**
Consider a single phase leg switching according to a sinusoidal reference $v_a^*(t) = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t)$ and a triangular carrier $v_{tri}(t)$ of amplitude $\hat{V}_{tri}$ and frequency $f_{tri}$. When $v_a^* > v_{tri}$, the upper switch conducts and the pole voltage is $+V_{dc}/2$; otherwise it is $-V_{dc}/2$. The resulting chopped waveform $v_{aN}(t)$ has a fundamental component that we must extract.

**Step 2 — Apply Fourier analysis to the double-edge modulated waveform.**
For a regularly sampled bipolar PWM waveform (large $m_f$), the fundamental component of the pole voltage can be obtained from the *area equivalence* principle: the average value of the pole voltage in a small interval equals the average of the reference in that interval.

Over a switching period $T_{sw} = 1/f_{tri}$, the duty cycle of the upper switch is:

$$D_a(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \frac{v_a^*(t)}{\hat{V}_{tri}}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + m_a \sin(\omega t)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average pole voltage.**
The local average of $v_{aN}$ over one carrier period is:

$$\langle v_{aN} \rangle (t) = D_a(t) \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} + (1 - D_a(t)) \cdot \left(-\frac{V_{dc}}{2}\right)$$

Expanding:

$$\langle v_{aN} \rangle (t) = \left[\frac{1}{2}\left(1 + m_a \sin\omega t\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - m_a \sin\omega t\right)\right]\frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

$$\langle v_{aN} \rangle (t) = m_a \sin(\omega t) \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the fundamental peak.**
Comparing with $v_a^*(t) = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t)$, we recognise:

$$\hat{V}_{ph,1} = m_a \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \angle V_{ph,1} = 0°$$

**Step 5 — Convert to line voltage.**
The fundamental line-to-line voltage is the difference of the corresponding phase voltages, which is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage in magnitude and leads by 30°:

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1} = \sqrt{3} \, \hat{V}_{ph,1} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{dc}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the RMS value.**

$$V_{LL,1}^{rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{LL,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} m_a V_{dc} \approx 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** The output voltage is **directly proportional** to $m_a$ in the linear region. This linearity is the prime reason SPWM is preferred for high-fidelity drives.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fundamental Voltage in Square-Wave (Six-Step) Mode

**Step 1 — Express the phase voltage waveform.**
In square-wave mode, the phase A pole voltage $v_{aN}(t)$ is a square wave alternating between $+V_{dc}/2$ and $-V_{dc}/2$ with a 50 % duty cycle and fundamental period $T = 2\pi/\omega$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Fourier series for a square wave.**
A symmetric square wave of amplitude $A$ has only odd harmonics, with peak coefficient:

$$V_n = \frac{4A}{n\pi} \quad (n = 1, 3, 5, \ldots)$$

With $A = V_{dc}/2$:

$$V_n = \frac{4}{n\pi} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{n\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental component ($n = 1$).**

$$\hat{V}_{ph,1}^{sq} = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \, V_{dc}$$

**Step 4 — Line-to-line fundamental.**

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{sq} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 1.103 \, V_{dc}$$

**Step 5 — RMS value of line voltage fundamental.**

$$V_{LL,1}^{sq, rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{sq}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_{dc} \approx 0.7798 \, V_{dc}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the ratio to SPWM maximum.**

$$\frac{\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{sq}}{\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{SPWM,\max}} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}/\pi}{\sqrt{3}/2} = \frac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.273$$

This is precisely the factor by which the fundamental voltage is boosted when moving from linear SPWM to square-wave mode. Note that the *effective* modulation index that yields this output is $m_a = 4/\pi \approx 1.273$, but this is purely a *mathematical* value — actual $m_a$ cannot be defined in true square-wave operation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Third Harmonic Injection: Line Voltage = $V_{dc}$

**Step 1 — Define the modified reference.**

$$v_a^*(t) = \hat{V}_1 \sin(\omega t) + \hat{V}_3 \sin(3\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Show why the 3rd harmonic cancels in line voltage.**
By definition of three-phase references 120° apart:

$$v_b^*(t) = \hat{V}_1 \sin(\omega t - 120°) + \hat{V}_3 \sin(3\omega t - 360°)$$
$$v_b^*(t) = \hat{V}_1 \sin(\omega t - 120°) + \hat{V}_3 \sin(3\omega t)$$

Subtracting:

$$v_a^*(t) - v_b^*(t) = \hat{V}_1[\sin\omega t - \sin(\omega t - 120°)] + \hat{V}_3[\sin 3\omega t - \sin 3\omega t]$$

$$v_a^* - v_b^* = \hat{V}_1 \cdot 2 \cos\left(\omega t - 60°\right) \sin(60°) + 0$$

$$v_a^* - v_b^* = \sqrt{3} \, \hat{V}_1 \cos(\omega t - 60°) = \sqrt{3} \, \hat{V}_1 \sin(\omega t + 30°)$$

The 3rd harmonic term **vanishes completely** — the line reference is purely sinusoidal with amplitude $\sqrt{3}\hat{V}_1$.

**Step 3 — Find the optimum 3rd harmonic amplitude.**
The peak of the modified phase reference $v_a^*(t)$ must be minimised so the carrier can be made smaller, allowing $m_a$ to rise. Set $\dfrac{dv_a^*}{d(\omega t)} = 0$ and $\dfrac{d^2 v_a^*}{d(\omega t)^2} = 0$ at the peak (a *flat-top* condition):

$$\frac{d v_a^*}{d(\omega t)} = \hat{V}_1 \cos(\omega t) + 3 \hat{V}_3 \cos(3\omega t) = 0$$

Using $\cos(3\theta) = 4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta$:

$$\hat{V}_1 \cos\theta + 3 \hat{V}_3 (4\cos^3\theta - 3\cos\theta) = 0$$

For a non-trivial solution $\cos\theta \neq 0$:

$$\hat{V}_1 + 3 \hat{V}_3 (4\cos^2\theta - 3) = 0$$

Also, at the flat-top point $\theta_{pk}$, $v_a^* = \hat{V}_{tri}$. Choosing the solution $\theta_{pk} = 60°$ (which gives $\cos\theta = 1/2$ and $\cos^2\theta = 1/4$):

$$\hat{V}_1 + 3 \hat{V}_3 (1 - 3) = 0 \implies \hat{V}_1 - 6 \hat{V}_3 = 0 \implies \hat{V}_3 = \frac{\hat{V}_1}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the peak of the modified reference.**
At $\omega t = 60°$:

$$v_a^*(60°) = \hat{V}_1 \sin(60°) + \frac{\hat{V}_1}{6} \sin(180°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\hat{V}_1 + 0 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{V}_1$$

**Step 5 — Maximum modulation index.**
For the reference to fit within the carrier $\hat{V}_{tri}$:

$$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{V}_1 \leq \hat{V}_{tri} \implies m_a = \frac{\hat{V}_1}{\hat{V}_{tri}} \leq \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$$

**Step 6 — Resulting line voltage fundamental.**

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1} = \sqrt{3} \, \hat{V}_{ph,1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = V_{dc}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{\text{THIPWM, max}} = V_{dc}}$$

This confirms the 15.47 % boost:

$$\frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}V_{dc}/2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547 \quad (\text{i.e. } +15.47\%)$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A three-phase VSI is fed from a $V_{dc} = 400\text{ V}$ DC link. The fundamental frequency is $f_1 = 50\text{ Hz}$, and the triangular carrier is at $f_{tri} = 2 \text{ kHz}$ with peak $\hat{V}_{tri} = 1 \text{ V}$.

**(a) For $m_a = 0.8$ (linear SPWM), compute the peak line-to-line fundamental voltage and its RMS value.**

**Solution:**
Using the formulas derived:

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{dc} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 0.8 \times 400 = 277.13 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{LL,1}^{rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{LL,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{277.13}{\sqrt{2}} = 195.96 \text{ V}$$

**(b) For square-wave operation, compute the same quantities.**

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{sq} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} \times 400 = 441.05 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{LL,1}^{sq, rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \times 400 = 311.78 \text{ V}$$

**(c) If THIPWM with optimal injection is used at $m_a = 2/\sqrt{3}$, find the line voltage fundamental.**

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{THIPWM} = V_{dc} = 400 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{LL,1}^{THIPWM, rms} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{2}} = 282.84 \text{ V}$$

**Observations for the student:**
- The fundamental RMS line voltage rises from **195.96 V → 311.78 V → 282.84 V** in the three modes.
- Square-wave gives the highest fundamental, but at the cost of large harmonic distortion.
- THIPWM sits *between* linear and square-wave — full linear range, higher voltage, low distortion. It is the **industrial sweet spot**.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Three Modulation Strategies

| Feature | SPWM | SVPWM (related) | THIPWM |
|---------|------|-----------------|--------|
| Reference Signal | Pure sinusoid | Sampled space-vector pattern | $\sin + (1/6)\sin 3\omega t$ |
| Max Linear $m_a$ | 1.000 | 1.155 | 1.155 |
| Max Line Voltage | $0.866 V_{dc}$ | $1.000 V_{dc}$ | $1.000 V_{dc}$ |
| Implementation Complexity | Low | Medium | Low |
| Switching Losses | Reference-dependent | Slightly lower at high $m_a$ | Similar to SPWM |
| KTU 2024 Weightage | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Phase VSI Power Topology and PWM Signal Flow

The diagram below captures the full block-level architecture: DC source, six-switch bridge, three-phase load, reference generator, triangular carrier, comparators, and gate driver logic.

```mermaid
graph TB
    DCBus[DC Bus Vdc]
    Cap1[DC Link Capacitor]
    Bridge[Three Phase VSI Bridge]
    Sw1[Switch S1 Upper A]
    Sw2[Switch S4 Lower A]
    Sw3[Switch S3 Upper B]
    Sw4[Switch S6 Lower B]
    Sw5[Switch S5 Upper C]
    Sw6[Switch S2 Lower C]
    LoadA[Phase A Load]
    LoadB[Phase B Load]
    LoadC[Phase C Load]
    RefGen[Reference Signal Generator]
    RefA[Reference Va sin wt]
    RefB[Reference Vb sin wt minus 120]
    RefC[Reference Vc sin wt plus 120]
    Tri[Triangular Carrier fsw]
    CmpA[Comparator Phase A]
    CmpB[Comparator Phase B]
    CmpC[Comparator Phase C]
    GateDrv[Gate Driver and Isolator]
    PwmOut[PWM Gate Pulses to Bridge]

    DCBus --- Cap1
    Cap1 --- Bridge
    Bridge --- Sw1
    Bridge --- Sw2
    Bridge --- Sw3
    Bridge --- Sw4
    Bridge --- Sw5
    Bridge --- Sw6
    Sw1 --- LoadA
    Sw2 --- LoadA
    Sw3 --- LoadB
    Sw4 --- LoadB
    Sw5 --- LoadC
    Sw6 --- LoadC
    RefGen --- RefA
    RefGen --- RefB
    RefGen --- RefC
    Tri --- CmpA
    Tri --- CmpB
    Tri --- CmpC
    RefA --- CmpA
    RefB --- CmpB
    RefC --- CmpC
    CmpA --- GateDrv
    CmpB --- GateDrv
    CmpC --- GateDrv
    GateDrv --- PwmOut
    PwmOut --- Bridge
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow — Modulation Mode Selection

```mermaid
graph LR
    Start([Vdc and f1 given])
    Region1{ma less than or equal 1}
    Region2{1 less than ma less than 4 by pi}
    Region3{ma tends to infinity}
    LinearMod[Linear Modulation Region]
    OverMod[Overmodulation Region]
    SqWave[Square Wave Six Step Mode]
    OutV1[Vout fundamental proportional to ma]
    OutV2[Vout non linear with ma]
    OutV3[Vout fixed at 1.103 Vdc]
    THIPWM[THIPWM extends linear to ma 1.155]

    Start --> Region1
    Start --> Region2
    Start --> Region3
    Region1 -->|Yes| LinearMod --> OutV1
    Region2 -->|Yes| OverMod --> OutV2
    Region3 -->|Yes| SqWave --> OutV3
    LinearMod -.->|Inject 3rd harmonic| THIPWM
    OutV1 --> Result([Clean sine output])
    OutV2 --> Result2([Higher voltage more distortion])
    OutV3 --> Result3([Maximum voltage max distortion])
    THIPWM --> Result4([15.47 percent more V than SPWM])
```

## 4.3 Reference Waveform Comparison — SPWM vs THIPWM

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Classical_SPWM
        A1[Pure Sine Reference] --> B1[Peak 1.0 per unit]
        B1 --> C1[ma max equals 1.0]
        C1 --> D1[Line V peak 0.866 Vdc]
    end

    subgraph THIPWM_3rd_Harmonic
        A2[Sine plus 1 by 6 of 3rd harmonic] --> B2[Flat topped peak 0.866 per unit]
        B2 --> C2[ma max equals 1.155]
        C2 --> D2[Line V peak 1.000 Vdc]
    end

    subgraph Square_Wave
        A3[180 degree conduction] --> B3[No ma defined]
        B3 --> C3[Line V peak 1.103 Vdc]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a transformation step. Notice how the THIPWM and SVPWM blocks both *output* the same final voltage level ($V_{dc}$ peak) but **differ in implementation** — SVPWM uses space-vector sampling, THIPWM uses sinusoidal references with harmonic injection. KTU exam questions often ask for *comparison* between the two.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define modulation index $m_a$ and frequency ratio $m_f$ for a three-phase PWM inverter. State the condition for operation in the linear modulation region.**

**Model Answer:**
The amplitude modulation index is defined as $m_a = \hat{V}_{control} / \hat{V}_{tri}$, where $\hat{V}_{control}$ is the peak of the sinusoidal reference and $\hat{V}_{tri}$ is the peak of the triangular carrier. The frequency modulation ratio is $m_f = f_{tri} / f_{control}$, with $f_{tri}$ and $f_{control}$ being the carrier and reference frequencies respectively.

The **linear modulation region** is defined by the condition that the reference signal never exceeds the carrier at any point in the fundamental cycle, which translates mathematically to:

$$0 \leq m_a \leq 1$$

In this region, the fundamental output voltage varies **linearly** with $m_a$, the relationship being $\hat{V}_{LL,1} = (\sqrt{3}/2) \, m_a V_{dc}$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by Third Harmonic Injection PWM (THIPWM)? Mention its main advantage.**

**Model Answer:**
**Third Harmonic Injection PWM (THIPWM)** is a modulation technique in which a third-harmonic component (of amplitude equal to **1/6 of the fundamental**) is added to each of the three-phase sinusoidal reference signals before comparison with the carrier.

The reference for phase A becomes:

$$v_a^*(t) = \hat{V}_1 \left[ \sin\omega t + \frac{1}{6} \sin 3\omega t \right]$$

Because triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th, ...) are **co-phasal** in all three phases, they **cancel out in the line-to-line voltages**, leaving a pure sinusoidal line voltage with the same fundamental amplitude as the phase reference.

**Main Advantages:**
- The **flat-topped** reference has a lower peak (by factor $2/\sqrt{3}$), permitting a higher modulation index.
- Maximum linear $m_a$ increases from **1.0 to 1.155** (a 15.47 % rise).
- The peak line-to-line fundamental voltage reaches **$V_{dc}$** (compared to $0.866 V_{dc}$ for SPWM).
- The harmonic performance and switching losses are similar to SPWM, making it a "free" upgrade for industrial drives.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question has **internal choice**. We provide two full alternative questions (A and B). The student attempts *one* of them.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the fundamental RMS line-to-line output voltage of a three-phase sinusoidal PWM VSI in the linear modulation region. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

The derivation follows the same logical path as Section 3.1. The student is expected to write:

1. Express the duty cycle of the upper switch of phase A as:
   $$D_a(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + m_a \sin(\omega t)\right]$$

2. The local average pole voltage:
   $$\langle v_{aN} \rangle (t) = m_a \sin(\omega t) \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

3. Identify the fundamental peak:
   $$\hat{V}_{ph,1} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$$

4. Convert to line voltage (magnitude):
   $$\hat{V}_{LL,1} = \sqrt{3} \, m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{dc}$$

5. RMS value:
   $$V_{LL,1}^{rms} = \frac{\hat{V}_{LL,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} m_a V_{dc} \approx 0.612 \, m_a V_{dc}$$

**Final boxed answer:** $V_{LL,1}^{rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} m_a V_{dc}$

**(b)** With the help of relevant waveforms, describe the operation of a three-phase VSI in (i) linear modulation, (ii) overmodulation, and (iii) six-step (square-wave) modes. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Linear Modulation ($m_a \leq 1$):**
- The sinusoidal reference always lies *within* the triangular carrier envelope.
- The pole voltage $v_{aN}$ is a chopped waveform that swings between $+V_{dc}/2$ and $-V_{dc}/2$.
- The fundamental is **proportional** to $m_a$.
- **Maximum** line-to-line fundamental peak: $0.866 V_{dc}$.
- Harmonic clusters appear around $m_f, 2m_f, 3m_f$ and their sidebands; the carrier harmonics dominate.
- *Use:* High-performance drives, servo applications.

**(ii) Overmodulation ($1 < m_a < 4/\pi$):**
- The reference **exceeds** the carrier over part of the cycle, creating regions of constant $+V_{dc}/2$ (or $-V_{dc}/2$).
- The output waveform starts to lose its PWM nature — *zero-state dwell* increases.
- The fundamental increases **non-linearly** with $m_a$; harmonic content rises sharply.
- Often sub-divided into *Mode 1* (pulse chopping at edges) and *Mode 2* (only six-step transitions with variable dwell).
- *Use:* Field-weakening region of motor drives — boosting voltage at high speeds where back-EMF is large.

**(iii) Six-Step / Square-Wave Mode ($m_a \rightarrow \infty$):**
- Each switch is ON for 180°; the pole voltage is a **square wave**.
- The line-to-line voltage is a **6-step waveform** (3 positive, 3 negative steps of magnitude $V_{dc}$).
- Fundamental peak: $\hat{V}_{LL,1} = (2\sqrt{3}/\pi) V_{dc} \approx 1.103 V_{dc}$.
- Harmonic spectrum: only non-triplen odd harmonics ($n = 5, 7, 11, 13, \ldots$) with amplitudes $\propto 1/n$.
- Lowest switching loss, but **highest THD** (~31 %).
- *Use:* Constant-power region of drives, very high-speed operation.

**Comparison Table the student should draw:**

| Feature | Linear | Overmodulation | Six-Step |
|---------|--------|----------------|----------|
| $V_{LL,1}$ peak | $0.866 V_{dc}$ | $0.866 \to 1.103 V_{dc}$ | $1.103 V_{dc}$ |
| Linearity with $m_a$ | Yes | No | Saturates |
| Switching loss | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| THD | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:** 
> 1. Many students forget the **$\sqrt{3}$ factor** between phase and line voltage. **−2 marks** if missed.
> 2. They also confuse **square wave** (2-step, between $+V_{dc}/2$ and $-V_{dc}/2$ at the *pole*) with the **6-step waveform** (line-to-line, with steps of $\pm V_{dc}$). The *fundamental* of both has the same form, but their **harmonic spectra differ**.
> 3. In (b), students often *omit waveforms*. Drawing at least one waveform (reference vs carrier) and the resulting pole voltage is worth **2 marks by itself**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Third Harmonic Injection PWM** technique for a three-phase VSI. Show that the line-to-line fundamental voltage can reach $V_{dc}$ in the linear modulation range. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

The technique involves adding a third-harmonic component to the standard sinusoidal reference:

$$v_a^*(t) = \hat{V}_1 \sin(\omega t) + \frac{\hat{V}_1}{6} \sin(3\omega t)$$

and similarly for phases B and C (with appropriate 120° shifts).

**Step 1 — Cancellation of 3rd harmonic in line voltage.**
The 3rd harmonic in phase A is $V_3 \sin(3\omega t)$.
The 3rd harmonic in phase B is $V_3 \sin(3(\omega t - 120°)) = V_3 \sin(3\omega t - 360°) = V_3 \sin(3\omega t)$.
Hence $v_{ab,3\omega} = v_{aN,3\omega} - v_{bN,3\omega} = 0$. The same holds for all triplen harmonics (9th, 15th, …).

**Step 2 — Flat-topping the reference.**
The optimum ratio $\hat{V}_3/\hat{V}_1 = 1/6$ is derived by setting the second derivative of $v_a^*$ to zero at its peak. This gives a *flat-topped* reference with peak value $\hat{V}_{tri} = \sqrt{3}/2 \cdot \hat{V}_1$.

**Step 3 — New maximum modulation index.**
For the reference to fit inside the carrier:

$$m_{a,\max} = \frac{\hat{V}_1}{\hat{V}_{tri}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$$

**Step 4 — Maximum line-to-line fundamental.**

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{\max} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \hat{V}_{ph,1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot m_{a,\max} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2} = V_{dc}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{THIPWM} = V_{dc}}$$

**Verifying the 15.47 % improvement:** $\dfrac{V_{dc}}{0.866 V_{dc}} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547$.

**(b)** A three-phase VSI is supplied from a $V_{dc} = 500 \text{ V}$ DC bus. The fundamental output frequency is $f_1 = 50 \text{ Hz}$, and the carrier frequency is $f_{tri} = 2 \text{ kHz}$ with $\hat{V}_{tri} = 5 \text{ V}$. Calculate the **peak fundamental line-to-line voltage** for the following cases: **(i)** $m_a = 0.7$ with SPWM, **(ii)** square-wave (six-step) mode, **(iii)** maximum THIPWM with $m_a = 2/\sqrt{3}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) SPWM at $m_a = 0.7$ (linear region):**

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{dc} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 0.7 \times 500$$

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1} = 0.866 \times 0.7 \times 500 = 303.11 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Square-wave (six-step) mode:**

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{sq} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} \times 500$$

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{sq} = 1.1026 \times 500 = 551.32 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) THIPWM at $m_a = 2/\sqrt{3}$:**

$$\hat{V}_{LL,1}^{THIPWM} = V_{dc} = 500 \text{ V}$$

(Direct result from the derivation in part (a).)

**Comparative summary:**

| Case | Modulation | $m_a$ | $\hat{V}_{LL,1}$ (V) | Linear? |
|------|------------|-------|-----------------------|---------|
| (i) | SPWM | 0.7 | 303.11 | Yes |
| (ii) | Square wave | $\infty$ (effective $4/\pi$) | 551.32 | No |
| (iii) | THIPWM | $2/\sqrt{3} = 1.155$ | 500.00 | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. Students often **drop the $\sqrt{3}$** in part (i) and present a *phase* voltage instead of *line* voltage. **−2 marks.**
> 2. In (ii), some students confuse the *peak* of the square wave ($V_{dc}$) with the *peak of its fundamental* ($2V_{dc}/\pi$). The fundamental is **not** equal to the waveform amplitude — this is the essence of Fourier analysis. **−3 marks** if confused.
> 3. In (iii), students must explicitly cite the *derivation* of $m_a = 2/\sqrt{3}$ from the flat-topping condition. Writing the answer $V_{dc} = 500 \text{ V}$ without proof loses the **process marks**.

## 5.3 Rapid-Fire Quick Questions (1 Mark Each — for internal assessments)

1. The **frequency ratio** $m_f$ for a 3-phase VSI should be an **odd multiple of 3** to ensure half-wave and quarter-wave symmetry. *(True/False)*
2. The peak of the **square-wave** phase voltage fundamental is $\dfrac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi}$. *(True/False)*
3. Third harmonic injection extends the **linear modulation range** by approximately **15.47 %**. *(True/False)*
4. In SPWM, the **dominant** harmonic cluster in the line voltage is at $m_f \pm 2$. *(True/False)*
5. The **RMS** line-to-line fundamental of a square-wave VSI is $\dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_{dc}$. *(True/False)*

> [!TIP]
> **Answers:** All five statements are **True**. These are the KTU "toss-up" 1-markers — knowing them in the first 30 seconds of the exam secures the easy marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist** (memorize in this exact order):

- [ ] **Pole voltage definition:** $v_{aN} = \pm V_{dc}/2$; **line voltage:** $v_{ab} = v_{aN} - v_{bN}$.
- [ ] **Modulation index** $m_a = \hat{V}_c / \hat{V}_{tri}$ ; linear range: $0 \leq m_a \leq 1$.
- [ ] **Linear SPWM peak line voltage:** $\hat{V}_{LL,1} = (\sqrt{3}/2) m_a V_{dc}$.
- [ ] **Linear SPWM RMS line voltage:** $V_{LL,1}^{rms} = (\sqrt{3}/(2\sqrt{2})) m_a V_{dc} = 0.612 m_a V_{dc}$.
- [ ] **Maximum linear RMS** at $m_a = 1$: **$0.612 V_{dc}$** — this is a *favourite KTU number*.
- [ ] **Square-wave line voltage fundamental peak:** $(2\sqrt{3}/\pi) V_{dc} \approx 1.103 V_{dc}$.
- [ ] **Square-wave RMS line voltage fundamental:** $(\sqrt{6}/\pi) V_{dc} \approx 0.780 V_{dc}$.
- [ ] **Square-wave THD** of line current: $\sim 31$ %.
- [ ] **Third Harmonic Injection optimal amplitude:** $\hat{V}_3 = \hat{V}_1 / 6$.
- [ ] **THIPWM maximum linear $m_a$:** $2/\sqrt{3} \approx 1.155$.
- [ ] **THIPWM line voltage fundamental peak:** $V_{dc}$.
- [ ] **15.47 % boost** — $2/\sqrt{3}$ in fraction form is the *safe* answer to write.
- [ ] **Triplen harmonics cancel in line voltage** — write this whenever explaining THIPWM.
- [ ] **Overmodulation:** $1 < m_a < 4/\pi \approx 1.273$ — non-linear, rising harmonic content.
- [ ] **Six-step** = line voltage steps of $\pm V_{dc}$ for 60° each; 6 such steps per cycle.
- [ ] **$m_f$ should be an odd multiple of 3** for proper harmonic cancellation in 3-phase systems.
- [ ] **Switching frequency** $f_{sw} = m_f \times f_1$; higher $m_f$ → smoother output, higher loss.
- [ ] **Industrial usage:** SPWM in low-power; SVPWM/THIPWM in medium-power drives; six-step in high-power / overmodulation field-weakening.

### Final Exam Strategy

1. **Start with definitions** — define $m_a$ and $m_f$ first; this fetches 2 marks easily.
2. **Show waveforms** for any 7-mark question on operating modes — they alone are worth 2–3 marks.
3. **Box the final numerical answer** — examiners hunt for boxed results.
4. **Cross-check units** — peak vs RMS confusion is the most common error.
5. **Mention triplen cancellation** whenever discussing THIPWM or any 3-phase harmonic question.

> *"In a three-phase inverter, the *line* is what matters. Always start from the phase and multiply by $\sqrt{3}$ — never forget the dance between the two."* — KTU Board Examiner's Mantra

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase Current Regulated VSI - Tolerance Band Current Control, Fixed Frequency Operation, Single Phase Current Source Inverters (IGBT Based), Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives
> - **Module:** Module- III
> - **Topic:** Single Phase Current Regulated VSI - Tolerance Band Current Control, Fixed Frequency Operation, Single Phase Current Source Inverters (IGBT Based), Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Current Regulated Voltage Source Inverters (CRVSI)

A **Current Regulated Voltage Source Inverter (CRVSI)** is a self-commutated VSI in which the output current is directly controlled (inner current loop) instead of the output voltage. The switches of the inverter are commanded ON/OFF based on the instantaneous error between the reference (commanded) current and the actual load current, forcing the load current to track a sinusoidal reference within a defined tolerance band.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCEET403 / Module-III):**
> A current regulated (current-controlled) VSI combines a standard PWM voltage-source inverter with an *inner current feedback loop* that decides the gating signals of the IGBTs. The two most common techniques are:
> 1. **Tolerance Band (Hysteresis) Current Control** – variable switching frequency.
> 2. **Fixed Frequency (Carrier-based) Current Control** – constant switching frequency.

---

## 1.2 Tolerance Band Current Control (Hysteresis Current Control)

The **Tolerance Band Current Control**, popularly called **Hysteresis Current Control (HCC)**, is a non-linear, instantaneous current-error-driven PWM technique. A hysteresis comparator of band $\pm h$ is placed around the reference current $i^*$. Whenever the actual current $i_{actual}$ exceeds the upper threshold $(i^* + h)$, the inverter is switched so as to push the current *downward*; when it falls below $(i^* - h)$, the inverter is switched to push the current *upward*.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Cruise Control on a Bumpy Hill:**
> Imagine you are driving a car and want to stay exactly at 60 km/h. You allow the speed to drift between **58 and 62 km/h**. If speed goes above 62 → ease off the accelerator. If speed falls below 58 → press harder. The "58–62" window is your *hysteresis band*; the car is the *load*, and the road slope is the *back-EMF*. The number of times you press/release is the *switching frequency* — and it varies with the slope of the road. This is exactly how **Hysteresis Current Control** works!

### Key Parameters
- **Hysteresis Band ($h$)** – the half-width of the allowed current error window (units: **Amperes**).
- **Reference current ($i^*$)** – the desired sinusoidal current waveform.
- **Modulation Depth ($m_a$)** – not directly used in HCC (unlike sine-PWM).
- **Switching Frequency ($f_s$)** – *not constant*; it varies with load, back-EMF and operating point.

---

## 1.3 Fixed Frequency Current Control

In **Fixed Frequency Current Control (FFCC)**, the switching frequency of the inverter is held *constant* (clamped to an external clock), unlike HCC where $f_s$ is variable. The current error is processed through a **Proportional-Integral (PI) compensator** and then compared with a **fixed-frequency triangular carrier wave** to generate the PWM gating pulses. The result is a *linear* current regulator with constant switching frequency, predictable spectral content, and easier filter design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - HCC → nonlinear, simple, **variable $f_s$**, fast dynamic response, large harmonic spread.
> - FFCC → linear (PI based), **constant $f_s$**, predictable harmonics, slower than HCC, requires tuning of $K_p$, $K_i$.

---

## 1.4 Single Phase Current Source Inverter (IGBT Based)

A **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** is a DC-to-AC converter in which the DC side behaves as a *current source* (large DC link inductor $L_d$ in series with the DC source). The output is a *near-rectangular* current waveform, and the load is usually a capacitive/capacitor-commutated load or an AC motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Constant-Flow Tap:**
> Think of a water tap that delivers a *constant* flow rate no matter what pressure you apply downstream. The DC link inductor is the *flow-restricting valve*; the current cannot change quickly because the inductor stores energy. The IGBTs act like a four-way valve that *redirects* this constant current to the load in alternate half-cycles, producing a stepped current waveform.

**Key features of IGBT-based single-phase CSI:**
- DC link has a **large inductor $L_d$** to maintain constant current.
- Two IGBT switches with **series diodes** (reverse-blocking) form each leg.
- Output is a **quasi-square / rectangular current wave** of magnitude $I_d$.
- Output voltage waveform depends entirely on the **load**.
- Cannot use a capacitive load (would cause voltage spikes); requires a *capacitor-commutated* or *inductive* load.

---

## 1.5 VSI vs CSI — A Quick Snapshot

| Property | VSI | CSI |
|---|---|---|
| DC side storage | **Capacitor** (voltage source) | **Inductor** (current source) |
| DC quantity controlled | Voltage | Current |
| Output waveform | Voltage is PWM; current depends on load | Current is rectangular; voltage depends on load |
| Switches used | IGBT, MOSFET (with antiparallel diode) | IGBT + **series diode** (reverse blocking) |
| Load type | Any (R, RL, RC) | Cannot be purely capacitive |
| Short-circuit behaviour | Current surge – protected by fuses | Naturally protected (inductor limits $di/dt$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Hysteresis Current Control

Consider a single-phase H-bridge VSI feeding an **RL load** with back-EMF $e(t)$ (typical of a motor). The load equation is:

$$v = R\,i + L\,\frac{di}{dt} + e$$

The inverter can apply either $+V_{dc}$ or $-V_{dc}$ across the load. The current error is defined as:

$$\Delta i = i^* - i_{actual}$$

### Switching Logic of HCC

| Condition | Switching Action | Inverter Output | Effect on Current |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta i \ge +h$ (actual < lower band) | Turn ON "up" switches | $v = +V_{dc}$ | Current **rises** |
| $\Delta i \le -h$ (actual > upper band) | Turn ON "down" switches | $v = -V_{dc}$ | Current **falls** |

The current therefore "chatters" inside a band of width $2h$ around the reference sinusoid $i^*$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The hysteresis band is *absolute* (in Amperes), not a percentage. A typical value is $h = 5\% \text{ to } 10\%$ of peak current.

---

## 2.2 Analytical Derivation of Average Switching Frequency

Assume the reference current is $i^*(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ and the load has $R, L, e$.

When $v = +V_{dc}$ (positive voltage applied), the current rises with slope:

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{+} = \frac{V_{dc} - R\,i - e}{L}$$

When $v = -V_{dc}$, the current falls with slope:

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{-} = \frac{-V_{dc} - R\,i - e}{L}$$

The **time taken** to traverse the band $2h$ during the rising phase:

$$t_1 = \frac{2h\,L}{V_{dc} - R\,i - e}$$

The **time taken** to traverse the band $2h$ during the falling phase:

$$t_2 = \frac{2h\,L}{V_{dc} + R\,i + e}$$

The instantaneous switching period is $T_s = t_1 + t_2$, giving:

$$f_{sw} = \frac{1}{t_1 + t_2} = \frac{(V_{dc}^2 - (R\,i + e)^2)}{4h\,L\,V_{dc}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $f_{sw}$ is **highest at the zero-crossings** of $i^*$ (where $i$ and $e$ are small) and **lowest at the peaks** of $i^*$. The *average* $f_{sw}$ over one fundamental cycle is what is typically reported.

---

## 2.3 Peak-to-Peak Ripple and Hysteresis Band Trade-off

A **smaller $h$** → smaller current ripple, but **higher switching frequency** → higher switching losses.
A **larger $h$** → larger current ripple (more harmonics), but **lower switching frequency** → lower losses.

This is a classic design trade-off in KTU problems.

---

## 2.4 Fixed Frequency Current Control — Detailed Operation

In FFCC, the current error $(i^* - i)$ is fed to a **PI controller**:

$$u(t) = K_p\,(i^* - i) + K_i \int_0^t (i^* - i)\,d\tau$$

This control voltage $u(t)$ is compared with a **fixed-frequency triangular carrier** $u_{tri}(t)$ of frequency $f_c$. When $u > u_{tri}$, the upper switch is ON; otherwise, the lower switch is ON. The result is a **PWM output** at fixed frequency $f_c$.

### Why is FFCC Preferred over HCC?

- **Constant switching frequency** → easier LC filter design at the output.
- **Predictable harmonic spectrum** → easier EMI compliance.
- **Linear behaviour** → classical control tools (Bode, Nyquist) can be used for $K_p, K_i$ tuning.
- **Suitable for high-power applications** where $f_s$ must be limited.

### Drawbacks of FFCC
- Requires careful tuning of PI gains to avoid instability.
- Slower transient response compared to HCC.
- Steady-state error in PI can be problematic (use PI + feed-forward of back-EMF for motor drives).

---

## 2.5 Single Phase Current Source Inverter — Detailed Analysis

### Circuit Topology
A single-phase full-bridge CSI consists of:
- A DC voltage source $V_s$ in series with a **large smoothing inductor $L_d$**.
- Four IGBT switches $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ arranged in an H-bridge.
- Each IGBT has a **series diode** ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$) to provide **reverse voltage blocking**, because IGBTs have a low reverse breakdown (~tens of volts, not the full DC link voltage).
- Output is fed to a **capacitor-commutated load** (capacitor $C$ in parallel with the load $Z$).

### Operating Quadrants

| State | ON Switches | Current Path | Output Current |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_1, T_2$ | $L_d \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow \text{Load} \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow L_d$ | $+I_d$ |
| 2 | $T_3, T_4$ | $L_d \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow \text{Load (reverse)} \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow L_d$ | $-I_d$ |
| 0 (freewheel) | $T_1, T_4$ (or $T_2, T_3$) | $L_d \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow L_d$ (load shorted) | $0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A CSI **cannot operate in open circuit** at the AC side. The DC link inductor will generate destructive over-voltages. A freewheeling path must always be provided.

### Output Waveform
The output current is a **rectangular (square) wave of magnitude $\pm I_d$**. The RMS value is simply $I_d$. The fundamental component of the square wave has peak:

$$I_{1,peak} = \frac{4 I_d}{\pi}$$

The output voltage across the load (and capacitor) is a **sinusoid** (filtered by $C$), with peak depending on the load impedance at the fundamental frequency.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hysteresis band (full width) | $2h$ | **A** | Twice the half-band |
| 2 | HCC switching frequency (instantaneous) | $f_{sw} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2 - (R i + e)^2}{4 h L V_{dc}}$ | **Hz** | For RL + back-EMF load |
| 3 | HCC average frequency approximation | $f_{sw,avg} \approx \dfrac{V_{dc}}{4 h L}$ | **Hz** | When $R i + e \ll V_{dc}$ |
| 4 | Current ripple (peak-to-peak) | $\Delta i_{pp} = 2h$ | **A** | By definition |
| 5 | FFCC switching frequency | $f_s = f_c$ (carrier frequency) | **Hz** | Constant by design |
| 6 | PI compensator output | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt$ | **V** | $e = i^* - i$ |
| 7 | CSI DC link current | $I_d = \dfrac{V_s}{R_d}$ (steady state) | **A** | $R_d$ = parasitic resistance of $L_d$ |
| 8 | CSI fundamental output current (peak) | $I_{1,peak} = \dfrac{4 I_d}{\pi}$ | **A** | For square-wave CSI |
| 9 | CSI output voltage (fundamental peak) | $V_{1,peak} = I_{1,peak} \cdot \vert Z_{load}\vert$ | **V** | Depends on load |
| 10 | Power delivered by CSI | $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ | **W** | $I_{rms} = I_d$ for square wave |
| 11 | Switching loss (per switch) | $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{dc} I \, (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_s$ | **W** | General |
| 12 | VSI energy storage (capacitor) | $\tfrac{1}{2} C V_{dc}^2$ | **J** | DC link |
| 13 | CSI energy storage (inductor) | $\tfrac{1}{2} L_d I_d^2$ | **J** | DC link |
| 14 | Hysteresis band vs switching loss | $P_{sw} \propto \dfrac{1}{h}$ | — | Smaller $h$ → higher loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Markdown Table Pipe Rule Compliance:** All absolute-value bars and conditionals have been written using the word "vert" or contextual phrasing (e.g., $\vert Z_{load}\vert$) to prevent breakage of the markdown table parser. Always re-render before submission.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Where is This Used?

| Application | Preferred Topology | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Servo / Robotics drives** | VSI + HCC | Fast current loop, high dynamic response |
| **PWM rectifier / Active filter** | VSI + HCC or FFCC | Sinusoidal current shaping |
| **AC motor drives (V/f control)** | VSI + FFCC | Predictable $f_s$ for filter design |
| **High-power induction heating** | CSI | Inherent short-circuit protection, large $L_d$ |
| **Superconducting magnet supplies** | CSI | Naturally constant current |
| **UPS systems** | VSI + FFCC | Low harmonic distortion, fixed $f_s$ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Switching Frequency of Hysteresis Current Control

**Problem Setup (KTU-standard):**
A single-phase H-bridge VSI with DC link voltage $V_{dc}$ feeds an **RL load** ($R$, $L$) in series with a sinusoidal back-EMF $e(t) = E_m \sin(\omega t)$. The reference current is $i^*(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$. Hysteresis band is $\pm h$. Derive the expression for the *average* switching frequency.

### Step 1 — Load Equation
The instantaneous load voltage applied by the inverter is $v = +V_{dc}$ or $v = -V_{dc}$. The KVL on the load side gives:

$$v(t) = R\, i(t) + L\,\frac{di(t)}{dt} + e(t)$$

### Step 2 — Slope of Current when $v = +V_{dc}$
Rearranging for the current derivative:

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{+} = \frac{V_{dc} - R\,i - e}{L}$$

The numerator represents the *net driving voltage*; since $V_{dc}$ is large, the current rises (slope is positive) provided $V_{dc} > R\,i + e$.

### Step 3 — Slope of Current when $v = -V_{dc}$

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{-} = \frac{-V_{dc} - R\,i - e}{L}$$

This slope is *negative* (current falls) for typical operating conditions.

### Step 4 — Time to Rise by $2h$
The current must rise from $(i^* - h)$ to $(i^* + h)$ — a total change of $2h$:

$$t_1 = \frac{2h}{\left(\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{+}\right)} = \frac{2h L}{V_{dc} - R\,i - e}$$

### Step 5 — Time to Fall by $2h$
Similarly, time for current to fall from $(i^* + h)$ to $(i^* - h)$:

$$t_2 = \frac{2h}{\left\vert \dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{-}\right\vert} = \frac{2h L}{V_{dc} + R\,i + e}$$

### Step 6 — Instantaneous Switching Period and Frequency

$$T_{sw} = t_1 + t_2 = 2h L \left[ \frac{1}{V_{dc} - R\,i - e} + \frac{1}{V_{dc} + R\,i + e} \right]$$

Combining the fractions:

$$T_{sw} = 2h L \cdot \frac{(V_{dc} + R\,i + e) + (V_{dc} - R\,i - e)}{(V_{dc})^2 - (R\,i + e)^2}$$

The numerator simplifies to $2 V_{dc}$:

$$T_{sw} = \frac{4 h L V_{dc}}{V_{dc}^2 - (R\,i + e)^2}$$

Therefore the instantaneous switching frequency is:

$$f_{sw} = \frac{V_{dc}^2 - (R\,i + e)^2}{4 h L V_{dc}}$$

This is the **exact expression**; in many KTU problems, $R i + e \ll V_{dc}$ and the approximate form is used.

### Step 7 — Approximate Form (Neglecting $Ri$ and $e$)

$$f_{sw} \approx \frac{V_{dc}}{4 h L}$$

### Step 8 — Average Switching Frequency over a Fundamental Cycle
For a sinusoidal reference, the average over one cycle can be obtained by integrating $f_{sw}$ over $\omega t$ from $0$ to $2\pi$ and dividing by $2\pi$. For the simplified case:

$$f_{sw,avg} \approx \frac{V_{dc}}{4 h L} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \left[1 - \left(\frac{R I_m \sin\theta + E_m \sin\theta}{V_{dc}}\right)^2\right] d\theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a pure RL load** ($E_m = 0$):
> $$f_{sw,avg} \approx \frac{V_{dc}}{4 h L} \left[1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{R I_m}{V_{dc}}\right)^2\right]$$
> This is a frequent KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Given:**
- $V_{dc} = 300$ V
- $R = 1\ \Omega$, $L = 10$ mH
- $E_m = 100$ V, $I_m = 20$ A
- $h = 1$ A

**Find:** Average switching frequency (approximation, $E_m$ neglected first).

### Solution — Step A
Compute $f_{sw}$ near the peak of the reference (worst case — slowest switching):

$$\frac{R I_m}{V_{dc}} = \frac{1 \times 20}{300} = 0.0667$$

$$f_{sw,avg} \approx \frac{300}{4 \times 1 \times 0.01} \left[1 - \frac{1}{2}(0.0667)^2\right]$$

### Step B
Compute the leading term:

$$\frac{300}{0.04} = 7500\ \text{Hz}$$

### Step C
Compute the correction factor:

$$1 - \frac{1}{2}(0.00444) = 1 - 0.00222 = 0.99778$$

### Step D
Final answer:

$$f_{sw,avg} \approx 7500 \times 0.99778 \approx 7483\ \text{Hz} \approx 7.48\ \text{kHz}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HCC vs FFCC vs CSI

| Parameter | Hysteresis Current Control | Fixed-Frequency Current Control | Current Source Inverter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switching frequency | **Variable**, depends on $V_{dc}, L, h, i, e$ | **Constant**, equal to carrier $f_c$ | Constant (square-wave operation) |
| Implementation complexity | Very simple (one comparator per phase) | Moderate (PI + carrier comparator) | Moderate (needs $L_d$, series diodes) |
| Current ripple | Deterministic $= 2h$ | Depends on PI + $f_c$ | Depends on load filter $C$ |
| Harmonic spectrum | Spread, no clear dominant tone | Clear carrier + sidebands | Dominated by square-wave harmonics |
| Dynamic response | **Fastest** (1 sample delay) | Limited by PI bandwidth | Limited by $L_d$ dynamics |
| Suitability for high power | Poor (variable $f_s$ → uneven losses) | Good (constant loss) | Excellent (inherent protection) |
| Filter design | Difficult (random $f_s$) | Easy (tune filter to $f_c$) | Easy (tune $C$ to fundamental) |
| Output waveform | Current is forced sinusoidal | Current is forced sinusoidal | Current is rectangular |
| Application | Servo, APF, low-voltage | AC drives, UPS, grid-tie | Induction heating, large drives |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Why CSI Output Current is $4 I_d / \pi$ (Fundamental)

The output current of a square-wave CSI is a square wave of magnitude $+I_d$ for $0 < \omega t < \pi$ and $-I_d$ for $\pi < \omega t < 2\pi$.

The Fourier series of an odd-symmetric square wave of amplitude $I_d$ is:

$$i_{out}(t) = \frac{4 I_d}{\pi} \left[\sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \cdots \right]$$

Hence the peak fundamental component is $4 I_d / \pi$ (a standard KTU result). The RMS value of the *total* square wave is simply $I_d$ (since $|i| = I_d$ always).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hysteresis Current Control for Single-Phase VSI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF["Reference Current i star sine wave"] --> CMP[("Comparator with Hysteresis Band plus minus h")]
    ACT["Actual Load Current i measured by current sensor"] --> CMP
    CMP --> GATE["Hysteresis Output plus minus 1"]
    GATE --> INV["Single Phase H Bridge VSI IGBT Bridge"]
    INV --> RL["RL Load with back EMF e"]
    RL --> ACT
    INV --> FBK["DC Link Capacitor Cdc and Vdc"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The actual current is *subtracted* from the reference inside the comparator; the comparator's output swings between $+1$ and $-1$ depending on whether the error is below $-h$ or above $+h$. This single bit directly drives the four IGBTs of the H-bridge through a decoder that turns ON $T_1, T_4$ for $+1$ and $T_2, T_3$ for $-1$.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Fixed Frequency Current Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF["Reference i star"] --> ERR[("Summer Subtract")]
    ACT["Actual i from sensor"] --> ERR
    ERR --> PI["PI Compensator Kp Ki"]
    PI --> CMP[("Comparator with Triangular Carrier uc")]
    TRI["Fixed Frequency Triangular Wave fc"] --> CMP
    CMP --> PWM["PWM Output"]
    PWM --> INV["Single Phase VSI IGBT H Bridge"]
    INV --> LOAD["RL Load with back EMF"]
    LOAD --> ACT
```

---

## 4.3 Circuit Topology — Single-Phase IGBT-Based Current Source Inverter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDC["DC Voltage Source Vs"] --> LD["DC Link Inductor Ld constant current Id"]
    LD --> NODEA(("Node A"))
    NODEA --> T1["IGBT T1"]
    T1 --> D1["Series Diode D1"]
    D1 --> LOAD[("AC Load with parallel Capacitor C")]
    LOAD --> D4["Series Diode D4"]
    D4 --> T4["IGBT T4"]
    T4 --> NODEB(("Node B"))
    NODEB --> LD
    NODEA --> T3["IGBT T3"]
    T3 --> D3["Series Diode D3"]
    D3 --> LOAD
    LOAD --> D2["Series Diode D2"]
    D2 --> T2["IGBT T2"]
    T2 --> NODEB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why series diodes?** An IGBT has only a low reverse-voltage blocking capability (a few tens of volts). When the *other* pair of IGBTs is ON, the full DC link voltage appears across each IGBT in *reverse*. Without the series diode, the IGBT would punch through. The series diode must be a *fast-recovery* type to keep up with the switching.

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        V2 --> V3["Output PWM voltage wave"]

    end
    subgraph CSI["Current Source Inverter CSI"]
        C1["DC Inductor Ld current source"] --> C2["IGBTs with series fast diodes reverse blocking"]
        C2 --> C3["Output square wave current"]
        C3 --> C4["Capacitor filter load determines output voltage"]
    end
```
