# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and explain the principle of tolerance band (hysteresis) current control in a single-phase VSI. Mention its main advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Tolerance band current control, also called hysteresis current control (HCC), is a non-linear PWM method in which the actual load current is forced to remain within a *tolerance band* of width $\pm h$ around the sinusoidal reference current $i^*$. The inverter switches are toggled whenever the current attempts to leave this band.

**Principle (1 mark):** A hysteresis comparator continuously checks $(i^* - i_{actual})$. If the error exceeds $+h$, the inverter applies $+V_{dc}$ to push the current up; if it falls below $-h$, $-V_{dc}$ is applied to push it down. The current therefore "chatters" between the two thresholds, with a peak-to-peak ripple of exactly $2h$.

**Advantages & Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- **Advantages:** Simple implementation (no carrier), fastest dynamic response, inherent peak-current limiting.
- **Disadvantages:** Variable switching frequency (no fixed $f_s$), uneven switching losses, spread harmonic spectrum, difficult filter design.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare Voltage Source Inverters (VSI) and Current Source Inverters (CSI) in terms of DC link energy storage, switch configuration, and output waveforms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | VSI | CSI |
|---|---|---|
| DC link storage | Capacitor $C_{dc}$ (acts as voltage source) | Inductor $L_d$ (acts as current source) |
| Switch configuration | IGBT/MOSFET with **antiparallel diode** | IGBT with **series fast-recovery diode** (reverse blocking) |
| Output waveform | PWM **voltage**; current depends on load | Rectangular **current**; voltage depends on load |
| Short-circuit protection | Needs external protection | Inherent (inductor limits $di/dt$) |
| Open-circuit behaviour | Safe (capacitor holds voltage) | **Unsafe** — inductor generates over-voltage |
| Load type | Any R, RL, RC | Cannot be purely capacitive |

(All 5 points — full 3 marks as a table covers the required 3 sub-asks.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Hysteresis + Fixed Frequency Control

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module III, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **single-phase hysteresis current-controlled VSI** feeding an RL load. Derive the expression for the *instantaneous switching frequency* and discuss its variation over a fundamental cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **fixed-frequency current control** using a PI regulator and triangular carrier. Compare it with hysteresis control in terms of switching frequency, complexity, and harmonic spectrum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Hysteresis Control (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *See Section 4.1 above*. The reference $i^* = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ is compared with the actual current $i$ in a hysteresis comparator of band $\pm h$. The comparator output directly drives the H-bridge.

**Operating Logic (2 marks):**
- If $i < (i^* - h)$: turn ON $T_1, T_4$ → apply $+V_{dc}$ → current rises (since $V_{dc} > R i + e$).
- If $i > (i^* + h)$: turn ON $T_2, T_3$ → apply $-V_{dc}$ → current falls.
- The current is therefore "trapped" within the band $\pm h$ around $i^*$.

**Derivation of Instantaneous Switching Frequency (2 marks):**
Starting from the load equation $v = R i + L \frac{di}{dt} + e$, with $v = \pm V_{dc}$:

$$t_{rise} = \frac{2h L}{V_{dc} - R i - e}, \quad t_{fall} = \frac{2h L}{V_{dc} + R i + e}$$

$$f_{sw} = \frac{1}{t_{rise} + t_{fall}} = \frac{V_{dc}^2 - (R i + e)^2}{4 h L V_{dc}}$$

**Variation over a Cycle (1 mark):** $f_{sw}$ is **maximum near the zero-crossings of $i^*$** (where $R i + e$ is small) and **minimum near the peaks** (where $R i + e$ is large). Hence the switching frequency *modulates* over the fundamental cycle — there is no single $f_s$.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Fixed-Frequency Current Control (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** *See Section 4.2 above*. The current error is processed by a PI compensator and then compared with a *fixed-frequency* triangular carrier.

**PI Output and PWM (2 marks):** The control signal is:

$$u(t) = K_p (i^* - i) + K_i \int_0^t (i^* - i)\, d\tau$$

This is compared with a triangular carrier of frequency $f_c$. When $u > u_{tri}$, the upper switch is ON; when $u < u_{tri}$, the lower switch is ON. The inverter therefore switches at exactly $f_c$ every cycle.

**Comparison with HCC (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HCC | FFCC |
|---|---|---|
| Switching frequency | Variable | Constant ($= f_c$) |
| Implementation | Very simple (hysteresis) | Moderate (PI tuning) |
| Dynamic response | Fastest | Slower (PI limited) |
| Harmonic spectrum | Spread, no dominant tone | Carrier + sidebands (predictable) |
| Filter design | Difficult | Easy (design for $f_c$) |
| Stability analysis | Nonlinear, no Bode plot | Linear, Bode/Nyquist applicable |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Current Source Inverter

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module III, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **single-phase IGBT-based current source inverter (CSI)** with a capacitor-commutated load. Explain its operating modes and derive the expression for the **fundamental component of the output current**. Why are series diodes required with the IGBTs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **single-phase VSI with hysteresis current control** and the **single-phase CSI** in terms of DC link energy storage, output waveforms, switching devices, load compatibility, and applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Single-Phase IGBT-Based CSI (7 marks)

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** *See Section 4.3 above*. The CSI consists of:
- DC source $V_s$, smoothing inductor $L_d$ (DC link current $I_d$).
- Four IGBTs $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ in an H-bridge, each with a series fast-recovery diode ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$) for reverse-voltage blocking.
- A capacitor $C$ in parallel with the load $Z$ (capacitor-commutated load).

**Operating Modes (2 marks):**
- **Mode 1 (positive half-cycle):** $T_1, T_2$ ON → current $+I_d$ flows through the load in one direction.
- **Mode 2 (negative half-cycle):** $T_3, T_4$ ON → current $-I_d$ flows through the load in the opposite direction.
- **Mode 0 (freewheeling):** $T_1, T_4$ (or $T_2, T_3$) ON → load is short-circuited, $I_d$ circulates in the DC link only.

**Fundamental Output Current (2 marks):** The output is a square wave of magnitude $\pm I_d$. Its Fourier series is:

$$i_{out}(t) = \frac{4 I_d}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega t) + \tfrac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \tfrac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \cdots\right]$$

The fundamental component has peak $4 I_d / \pi \approx 1.273 I_d$.

**Why Series Diodes (1 mark):** IGBTs have a low reverse-voltage blocking capability (a few tens of volts). When the complementary pair is ON, the full DC link voltage is impressed across each IGBT in the *reverse* direction. The series fast-recovery diode blocks this reverse voltage and prevents device failure.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — VSI-HCC vs CSI (7 marks)

| Feature | VSI with Hysteresis Control | CSI |
|---|---|---|
| **DC link storage** | Capacitor $C_{dc}$ — acts as a voltage source | Inductor $L_d$ — acts as a current source |
| **Output waveform (current)** | Sinusoidal, with controllable ripple $= 2h$ | Square wave of magnitude $\pm I_d$ |
| **Output waveform (voltage)** | Depends on load (PWM at terminals) | Sinusoidal (filtered by load capacitor $C$) |
| **Switching devices** | IGBT with antiparallel diode | IGBT with **series** fast-recovery diode |
| **Switching frequency** | Variable (hysteresis-driven) | Constant (square-wave operation) |
| **Load compatibility** | Any (R, RL, RC) | Cannot drive a purely capacitive load |
| **Fault behaviour** | Sensitive to short-circuit | Naturally protected (inductor limits $di/dt$) |
| **Open-circuit behaviour** | Safe (capacitor maintains voltage) | Unsafe — $L_d$ generates destructive over-voltage |
| **Harmonic spectrum of current** | Spread, but low ripple | Dominated by $3^{rd}, 5^{th}, 7^{th}$ harmonics of square wave |
| **Typical applications** | Servo drives, APF, PWM rectifiers | Induction heating, large motor drives, superconducting magnets |
| **Power level** | Low to medium (kW to ~MW) | Medium to high (MW class) |

(All 11 points — full 7 marks if presented as a structured table with relevant elaborations.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing HCC with FFCC:** Many students write "hysteresis gives constant frequency" — *false*. HCC has *variable* $f_s$. This is a 2-mark killer.
> 2. **Forgetting the series diode in CSI:** IGBTs *need* a series diode for reverse blocking in a CSI. Drawing a CSI with just IGBTs (no diode) will lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the DC link inductor in CSI:** A CSI without $L_d$ is incomplete. $L_d$ is the *defining* element.
> 4. **Mixing up output waveforms:** VSI output is a *voltage* waveform (PWM); CSI output is a *current* waveform (rectangular). This is a classic confusion point.
> 5. **Sign error in HCC derivation:** The switching-frequency expression has $V_{dc}^2 - (Ri + e)^2$ in the numerator — a minus, not a plus. Wrong sign → wrong numerical answer → 0 marks for the derivation.
> 6. **Using $I_d$ instead of $4I_d/\pi$ for the fundamental:** When asked for the *fundamental* component of CSI output current, the answer is $4I_d/\pi$, not $I_d$. $I_d$ is the *total* (RMS) value.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Hysteresis Current Control (HCC)** is a *non-linear*, *variable-frequency* current control method based on a comparator with a tolerance band of $\pm h$.
- **HCC switching frequency** varies as: $f_{sw} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2 - (R i + e)^2}{4 h L V_{dc}}$ — highest near zero-crossings, lowest near peaks.
- **Smaller hysteresis band $h$** → lower current ripple, higher switching frequency, higher switching losses.
- **Fixed-Frequency Current Control (FFCC)** uses a **PI regulator + triangular carrier** to maintain a *constant* switching frequency $f_c$.
- **FFCC** is preferred over HCC in applications needing *predictable harmonic spectrum* and *easy filter design*.
- **PI compensator** in FFCC: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt$, where $e = i^* - i$.
- **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** uses a *DC link inductor* $L_d$ as energy storage — defines the constant DC current $I_d$.
- **CSI switches** are IGBTs with *series fast-recovery diodes* for reverse-voltage blocking.
- **CSI output current** is a *square wave* of magnitude $\pm I_d$; its **fundamental component** has peak $\dfrac{4 I_d}{\pi}$.
- **CSI cannot feed a purely capacitive load** and **cannot operate in open circuit** at the AC side — both cause destructive over-voltages.
- **VSI vs CSI quick rule:**
  - VSI: **C**apacitor in DC link → **C**ontrols **V**oltage, output is a PWM *v*oltage wave.
  - CSI: **L**arge inductor in DC link → **C**ontrols **C**urrent, output is a rectangular *c*urrent wave.
- **HCC vs FFCC quick rule:**
  - HCC: *H*ysteresis → *H*ighly variable $f_s$, fastest, simplest.
  - FFCC: *F*ixed $f_c$ → *F*ilter-friendly, linear, PI-tunable.
- **Most important KTU formula to memorize:** $f_{sw} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2 - (R i + e)^2}{4 h L V_{dc}}$ and $I_{1,peak} = \dfrac{4 I_d}{\pi}$ for the CSI fundamental.
- **Typical 14-mark KTU pattern:** one sub-question on *derivation / waveform* (7 marks) + one sub-question on *comparison / block diagram* (7 marks). Master both formats.
- **Energy storage identities:** VSI stores energy in the capacitor ($\tfrac{1}{2} C V_{dc}^2$); CSI stores energy in the inductor ($\tfrac{1}{2} L_d I_d^2$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Improved Utility Interface - Generation of Current Harmonics, Power Factor, Harmonics and IEEE 519 Standard, Active Shaping of the Input Line Current, Single-phase Front End Boost Converter (Circuit Diagram, Operation, Block Diagram of the Control Scheme)

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives
> - **Module:** Module- III
> - **Topic:** Need for Improved Utility Interface - Generation of Current Harmonics, Power Factor, Harmonics and IEEE 519 Standard, Active Shaping of the Input Line Current, Single-phase Front End Boost Converter (Circuit Diagram, Operation, Block Diagram of the Control Scheme)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Need for an Improved Utility Interface

In an ideal AC power system, electrical loads should draw power from the utility as a **pure sinusoidal current at unity power factor** — perfectly in phase with the supply voltage. However, the widespread use of **power electronic converters** (rectifiers, SMPS, motor drives, battery chargers) has polluted the utility interface with **non-sinusoidal currents**, leading to **harmonic distortion, poor power factor, and electromagnetic interference**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module III**
> The "Utility Interface" refers to the point of common coupling (PCC) where a power electronic load connects to the AC mains. An "improved" interface means drawing a near-sinusoidal, in-phase current, complying with IEEE 519 and other power-quality standards.

### Real-World Analogy
Imagine a water tap (utility) connected to a hose (load). An ideal load draws water **smoothly and continuously**. A diode rectifier, however, draws water in **short, violent bursts** (only near the voltage peaks). This pulsating demand causes the upstream pipes (utility feeders, transformers) to vibrate, heat up, and waste energy. An "improved utility interface" is like installing a **buffer tank with a smart valve (PFC converter)** that draws water smoothly while satisfying the actual demand downstream.

## 1.2 Generation of Current Harmonics

A **harmonic** of a periodic waveform is a sinusoidal component whose frequency is an integer multiple of the fundamental frequency. For a 50 Hz supply, the 3rd harmonic is at 150 Hz, the 5th at 250 Hz, the 7th at 350 Hz, and so on.

### Why Do Harmonics Arise?
Power electronic circuits use **non-linear elements** (diodes, thyristors, transistors) that draw current in a non-sinusoidal manner even when energized by a sinusoidal voltage. By **Fourier's theorem**, any periodic, non-sinusoidal current can be decomposed into a sum of sinusoids (a fundamental + harmonics).

$$ i(t) = I_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n) $$

Common sources of harmonics in power electronic systems:
- **Diode bridge rectifiers** with capacitive filter (used in SMPS, adapters) — these draw narrow, peaked current pulses.
- **Thyristor-controlled rectifiers** at firing angles other than 0° — current is chopped, producing rich harmonic content.
- **Switch-mode power converters** — operate at high frequency but draw non-sinusoidal mains current.
- **Arc furnaces, fluorescent lamps, saturated transformers** — also non-linear loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Current Harmonic Distortion**
> Current harmonic distortion is the deviation of the line current waveform from a pure sinusoid, quantified by the **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**. It is a direct consequence of non-linear loading of the utility.

## 1.3 Power Factor — Definition and Significance

**Power Factor (PF)** is the ratio of **real (active) power** delivered to the load to the **apparent power** drawn from the utility:

$$ PF = \frac{P_{real}}{S_{apparent}} = \frac{V_{rms} \cdot I_{1,rms} \cdot \cos \phi_1}{V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}} = \frac{I_{1,rms}}{I_{rms}} \cdot \cos \phi_1 $$

where:
- $I_{1,rms}$ = RMS value of the fundamental component of current
- $I_{rms}$ = true RMS value of the total current
- $\cos \phi_1$ = **displacement power factor (DPF)** — the cosine of the angle between the fundamental voltage and fundamental current.

A **distorted current waveform** (high THD) and a **phase shift** (poor DPF) both reduce the overall PF.

## 1.4 IEEE 519 Standard — At a Glance

**IEEE Std 519-2014** ("IEEE Recommended Practice and Requirements for Harmonic Control in Electric Power Systems") sets the **limits on voltage and current harmonics** that a load may inject into the utility. It is enforced at the **Point of Common Coupling (PCC)**.

Key concepts:
- **TDD (Total Demand Distortion)** = ratio of harmonic current to the maximum demand load current.
- Limits depend on the **short-circuit ratio ($I_{sc}/I_L$)** at the PCC and the **load's size relative to the utility**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IEEE 519 is **not** a per-appliance standard; it is a **system-level** standard. The utility, designer, and consumer all share responsibility for keeping harmonics within bounds.

## 1.5 Active Shaping of the Input Line Current

**Active current shaping** (also called **Active Power Factor Correction — Active PFC**) is the technique of inserting a **switch-mode converter** between the rectifier and the bulk DC capacitor. The converter is controlled so that the input current is forced to be **sinusoidal and in phase** with the line voltage, regardless of the load.

**Advantages of Active Shaping:**
- Power factor approaches **unity** (PF > 0.99).
- THD reduced to **< 5%**, complying with IEEE 519, IEC 61000-3-2.
- Better utilization of utility feeders and transformers.
- Smaller, cheaper passive filter components.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Active vs Passive PFC**
> Passive PFC uses only L-C filters; it is bulky, only partially corrects PF, and has poor dynamic response. **Active PFC** uses a controlled switch-mode converter — typically a **boost converter** — and is the industry standard for compliance with harmonic standards.

## 1.6 Single-phase Front-End Boost Converter — The Industry Workhorse

A **front-end boost converter** is a DC-DC boost (step-up) chopper placed at the **front end** (input side) of a power electronic system, after the diode bridge rectifier. Its specific job is to **shape the input current** into a sinusoid in phase with the line voltage, while stepping up the rectified DC bus voltage to a regulated higher level (typically 380–400 V DC from 230 V AC mains).

**Key characteristics:**
- Operates in **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** at high power (>300 W); in **DCM or CRM (Critical Conduction Mode)** at low power.
- The **boost inductor** is in series with the AC line, carrying the full shaped current.
- Control is via **average current mode** or **peak current mode** with an input voltage feed-forward.
- Output is held at a **constant DC voltage** higher than the peak of the AC input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The boost topology is preferred for PFC because its **input current is non-pulsating and continuous**, allowing easy current-mode control and naturally providing inrush current limiting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Diode Rectifier with Capacitive Filter — A Harmonic Polluter

A standard **uncontrolled diode bridge rectifier** feeding a large smoothing capacitor is the most common harmonic source in consumer and industrial electronics.

**Operating principle (briefly):**
- The capacitor charges only when the instantaneous AC voltage exceeds the capacitor voltage.
- This happens in a narrow window near each voltage peak.
- The result: the **input current is a series of narrow, peaked pulses** (1–2 ms wide for a 50 Hz system).

**Fourier analysis** of these pulses shows that the current contains:
- A fundamental component (50 Hz) — at a small phase angle with the voltage.
- Strong **odd harmonics** (3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, 13th, …).
- For symmetrical, half-wave-shaped pulses, the **3rd harmonic is often the largest**.

**Consequences of this harmonic pollution:**
1. **Overheating** of distribution transformers, neutral conductors (especially with 3rd harmonic triplen currents), and circuit breakers.
2. **Voltage distortion** at the PCC, affecting other consumers.
3. **Malfunction** of sensitive equipment (PLCs, protection relays, communication systems).
4. **Resonance** with power factor correction capacitor banks, leading to capacitor failure.
5. **Increased RMS current** without a corresponding increase in real power → wasted capacity, higher losses.

## 2.2 Quantifying Harmonic Distortion

### 2.2.1 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

$$ THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_{n,rms}^2}}{I_{1,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{I_{rms}^2 - I_{1,rms}^2}}{I_{1,rms}} $$

where $I_{n,rms}$ is the RMS value of the $n$-th harmonic current.

### 2.2.2 Total Demand Distortion (TDD)

$$ TDD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_{n,rms}^2}}{I_L} $$

where $I_L$ is the **maximum demand load current** (fundamental) at the PCC.

### 2.2.3 Relationship Between PF, DPF, and THD

The total power factor of a non-linear load is:

$$ PF = \frac{I_{1,rms}}{I_{rms}} \cdot \cos \phi_1 = \frac{\cos \phi_1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}} $$

If the fundamental current is in phase with the voltage ($\cos \phi_1 = 1$, i.e., DPF = 1):

$$ PF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}} $$

**Engineering Insight:** To achieve PF ≥ 0.95, THD must be ≤ 31% (with unity DPF). To achieve PF ≥ 0.99, THD must be ≤ 14%. This is why modern Active PFC designs target **THD < 5%**.

## 2.3 IEEE 519-2014 Current Harmonic Limits (Simplified)

The standard specifies **maximum individual harmonic current** and **TDD** as a function of the **short-circuit ratio** $I_{SC}/I_L$ at the PCC.

### 2.3.1 Simplified Current Distortion Limits (for 120 V–69 kV systems, odd harmonics)

| $I_{SC} / I_L$ | h < 11 | 11 ≤ h < 17 | 17 ≤ h < 23 | 23 ≤ h < 35 | 35 ≤ h | TDD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ≤ 20 | 4.0% | 2.0% | 1.5% | 0.6% | 0.3% | 5.0% |
| 20–50 | 7.0% | 3.5% | 2.5% | 1.0% | 0.5% | 8.0% |
| 50–100 | 10.0% | 4.5% | 4.0% | 1.5% | 0.7% | 12.0% |
| 100–1000 | 12.0% | 5.5% | 5.0% | 2.0% | 1.0% | 15.0% |
| > 1000 | 15.0% | 7.0% | 6.0% | 2.5% | 1.4% | 20.0% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** The larger the load relative to the utility (lower $I_{SC}/I_L$), the **stricter** the harmonic limit. Even harmonics are limited to 25% of the odd harmonic limits.

### 2.3.2 Voltage Distortion Limits

| PCC Voltage | Individual Harmonic | THD |
|---|---|---|
| V ≤ 1 kV | 5.0% | 8.0% |
| 1 kV < V ≤ 69 kV | 3.0% | 5.0% |
| 69 kV < V ≤ 161 kV | 1.5% | 2.5% |
| V > 161 kV | 1.0% | 1.5% |

## 2.4 Active Shaping — Operating Principles

Active shaping of the line current is achieved by inserting a **switch-mode DC-DC converter** (typically boost) between the bridge rectifier and the bulk capacitor, controlled so that the **input current tracks the input voltage waveform**.

Two fundamental requirements:
1. **Waveform tracking** — the input current must be a **scaled, in-phase replica** of the rectified input voltage.
2. **Output regulation** — the output DC voltage must be **regulated to a constant value** (usually 380–400 V for 230 V AC input).

The boost converter operates in either:
- **CCM (Continuous Conduction Mode)** — fixed frequency (typically 50–100 kHz), high power, lower inductor ripple.
- **DCM (Discontinuous Conduction Mode)** — variable frequency, simpler control, used at low power (< 200 W).
- **CRM (Critical Conduction Mode)** — boundary between CCM and DCM, popular for medium-power PFC.

## 2.5 Single-phase Front-End Boost Converter — Circuit Description

### 2.5.1 Power Circuit

The standard PFC boost front-end consists of:
- **Diode bridge rectifier** ($D_1$–$D_4$) — converts AC mains to full-wave rectified DC.
- **Boost inductor** ($L$) — placed in series with the AC line, carries the shaped current.
- **Power switch** ($S$, usually a MOSFET) — connected from the inductor-DC bus node to ground/return.
- **Boost diode** ($D_b$) — prevents reverse current from output to input.
- **Output filter capacitor** ($C$) — bulk capacitor at the DC bus (typically 1–4.7 µF per watt for hold-up time).
- **DC load** — the actual load (e.g., a downstream DC-DC converter feeding a computer, motor drive, etc.).

### 2.5.2 Operation in CCM — Switching Cycle

During one high-frequency switching period $T_s$:

**Switch ON (duration $DT_s$):**
- The MOSFET conducts; the boost diode $D_b$ is reverse-biased.
- The inductor is connected across the **input** (rectified AC). Voltage across $L$ is $V_{in} = \vert v_{ac}(t) \vert$.
- Inductor current **rises** linearly: $\dfrac{d i_L}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{L}$.
- The output capacitor $C$ alone supplies the load; output voltage droops slightly.

**Switch OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$):**
- The MOSFET blocks; the boost diode $D_b$ conducts.
- The inductor releases its stored energy to the load and capacitor. Voltage across $L$ is $V_{in} - V_o$ (negative).
- Inductor current **falls** linearly: $\dfrac{d i_L}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_o}{L}$.
- The capacitor is recharged.

**Volt-second balance** on the inductor over one switching period (assuming $V_{in}$ and $V_o$ constant over $T_s$):

$$ V_{in} \cdot D T_s + (V_{in} - V_o)(1-D)T_s = 0 $$

$$ \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 - D} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the standard **boost gain** — but here $V_{in}$ is a slowly varying (50 Hz) rectified sinusoid, while $D$ is adjusted rapidly (at switching frequency) to control the **input current shape**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Boost voltage gain | $\dfrac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $D$ = duty ratio |
| Inductor current ripple (CCM) | $\Delta i_L = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D T_s}{L} = \dfrac{V_o(1-D)T_s}{L}$ | Peak-to-peak |
| Critical inductance (boundary) | $L_{crit} = \dfrac{V_{in} D (1-D) T_s}{2 I_{L,avg}}$ | Boundary of CCM/DCM |
| Output voltage ripple | $\Delta V_o = \dfrac{I_o D T_s}{C} = \dfrac{P_o D T_s}{V_o C}$ | Capacitor sizing |
| PF (general) | $PF = \dfrac{\cos \phi_1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$ | $cos\phi_1$ = DPF |
| PF (unity DPF) | $PF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}$ | When $\phi_1 = 0$ |
| THD | $THD_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{I_{rms}^2 - I_{1,rms}^2}}{I_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$ | Per cent |
| TDD | $TDD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} I_{n,rms}^2}}{I_L} \times 100\%$ | Per cent |
| Crest factor | $CF = \dfrac{I_{peak}}{I_{rms}}$ | = 1.414 for pure sine |
| DC bus voltage (target) | $V_{DC} \approx 1.05 \times V_{ac,rms} \times \sqrt{2}$ | ~ 380–400 V from 230 V AC |
| Power throughput | $P_{in} = V_{in,rms} \cdot I_{in,rms} \cdot PF$ | Average real input power |
| Average input current (target) | $\langle i_{in}(t) \rangle = \dfrac{V_m \vert \sin \omega t \vert \cdot K}{R_{eq}(t)}$ | Sinusoidal envelope |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the formula sheet:** The vertical bar `\vert` (NOT the pipe character `\vert`) is used for absolute value. In handwritten or typed exam scripts, students should clearly distinguish **modulus** from **the number 1**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Active PFC front-end boost converters are used in essentially every high-power single-phase electronic load:
- **Personal computers and servers** (80 PLUS compliant SMPS).
- **LED lighting drivers** (> 25 W in many regions).
- **Telecom rectifiers** (-48 V systems with PFC front end).
- **Variable-speed drives** for HVAC, washing machines, small industrial motors.
- **EV battery chargers** (Level 1 and Level 2 onboard chargers).
- **Welding power supplies, induction heating, plasma cutters**.

The economic impact is enormous: utility companies worldwide save billions in avoided infrastructure upgrades because modern Active PFC keeps total harmonic pollution within IEEE 519 / IEC 61000-3-2 limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Factor in Terms of THD and DPF

We start from the fundamental definitions. The instantaneous power delivered to a single-phase load is:

$$ p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) $$

The **real (average) power** over one fundamental cycle $T$ is:

$$ P = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt $$

Let the supply voltage be a pure sinusoid:

$$ v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) $$

The current, being non-sinusoidal, is expressed via Fourier series (no DC component for symmetric loads):

$$ i(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} I_{n} \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n) $$

where $I_n$ is the amplitude of the $n$-th harmonic and $\phi_n$ is its phase relative to the fundamental voltage.

**Step 1:** Orthogonality of sinusoids means the average of $\sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$ over a full period is zero for $n \neq 1$. Therefore, only the $n = 1$ term contributes to the average real power.

$$ P = V_m \cdot I_1 \cdot \frac{\cos \phi_1}{2} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{1,rms} \cdot \cos \phi_1 $$

**Step 2:** The **apparent power** is the product of the true RMS voltage and the true RMS current:

$$ S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} $$

The RMS value of the distorted current is, by Parseval's theorem:

$$ I_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} I_{n,rms}^2} = \sqrt{I_{1,rms}^2 + I_{2,rms}^2 + I_{3,rms}^2 + \cdots} $$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$ PF = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{V_{rms} \cdot I_{1,rms} \cdot \cos \phi_1}{V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}} $$

$$ \boxed{PF = \frac{I_{1,rms}}{I_{rms}} \cdot \cos \phi_1} $$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the ratio $I_{1,rms} / I_{rms}$ is the **distortion factor (DF)**, and the definition of THD gives:

$$ THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{I_{rms}^2 - I_{1,rms}^2}}{I_{1,rms}} $$

Squaring and rearranging:

$$ \frac{I_{1,rms}}{I_{rms}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}} $$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$ \boxed{PF = \frac{\cos \phi_1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}}} $$

This is the **master equation** for power factor in non-linear single-phase systems.

### 3.1.1 Worked Example

A single-phase rectifier has $THD_I = 30\%$ and the fundamental current lags the voltage by $10^\circ$.

**Step 1:** Compute DPF.

$$ \cos \phi_1 = \cos 10^\circ = 0.9848 $$

**Step 2:** Compute PF.

$$ PF = \frac{0.9848}{\sqrt{1 + (0.30)^2}} = \frac{0.9848}{\sqrt{1.09}} = \frac{0.9848}{1.0440} $$

$$ \boxed{PF = 0.9433 } $$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the dramatic effect: even with near-unity DPF (0.985), a 30% THD pulls PF down to 0.94. This is why **harmonic control is essential for power quality**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required THD for a Given Target PF (Unity DPF)

Assume the controller is good enough to keep the fundamental in phase with the voltage ($\cos \phi_1 = 1$). For a target power factor $PF_{target}$:

$$ 1 + THD_I^2 = \left(\frac{1}{PF_{target}}\right)^2 $$

$$ THD_I = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{PF_{target}}\right)^2 - 1} $$

### 3.2.1 Design Table — THD Required to Meet Various PF Targets (Unity DPF)

| Target PF | Required THD |
|---|---|
| 0.90 | 48.4% |
| 0.95 | 31.0% |
| 0.97 | 24.5% |
| 0.99 | 14.2% |
| 0.995 | 10.0% |
| 0.999 | 4.5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **0.99 PF** is the typical design target for Active PFC; it requires THD ≤ 14% — easily achievable with a well-designed boost PFC at moderate cost.

## 3.3 Derivation: Boost Converter Average Input Current Model

For the boost PFC in CCM, the instantaneous average input current (averaged over one switching period) is:

$$ \langle i_L(t) \rangle = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_0^{T_s} i_L(\tau) \, d\tau $$

Inductor volt-second balance gives the small-signal input-output relationship. For slowly varying $V_{in}(t) = V_m \vert \sin \omega t \vert$:

**Switch ON** (duration $DT_s$): voltage across L = $V_{in}$

$$ \frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}}{L} $$

**Switch OFF** (duration $(1-D)T_s$): voltage across L = $V_{in} - V_o$

$$ \frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_{in} - V_o}{L} $$

Applying volt-second balance:

$$ V_{in} \cdot D + (V_{in} - V_o)(1-D) = 0 $$

Solving for $D$:

$$ D = 1 - \frac{V_{in}}{V_o} = 1 - \frac{V_m \vert \sin \omega t \vert}{V_o} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The duty ratio is **time-varying** at twice the line frequency (100 Hz) — this is the signature of a PFC boost converter.

The average input current, in steady state, is determined by the power balance. The **output power** is $P_o$ and is approximately constant (because $V_o$ is regulated and the load is DC). Therefore, the **input power** averaged over a half-line cycle is also $P_o$ (lossless assumption):

$$ P_{in,avg} = \frac{1}{T_{line}/2} \int_0^{T_{line}/2} v_{in}(t) \cdot i_{in}(t) \, dt = P_o $$

For a purely resistive equivalent input:

$$ i_{in}(t) = \frac{v_{in}(t)}{R_{eq}} = \frac{V_m \vert \sin \omega t \vert}{R_{eq}} $$

Then:

$$ P_{in,avg} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_{eq}} = P_o \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_{eq} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 P_o} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The "emulated resistance" $R_{eq}$ is a key concept: the PFC controller makes the converter **look like a resistor** to the AC mains, drawing a current proportional to the instantaneous voltage.

### 3.3.1 Numerical Example

A boost PFC operates from $V_{ac,rms} = 230\,\text{V}$ at 50 Hz, delivering $P_o = 1\,\text{kW}$ to a DC load at $V_o = 400\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1:** Peak input voltage.

$$ V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 = 325.27 \,\text{V} $$

**Step 2:** Equivalent emulated resistance.

$$ R_{eq} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 P_o} = \frac{(325.27)^2}{2 \cdot 1000} = \frac{105800}{2000} = 52.9 \,\Omega $$

**Step 3:** Peak input current.

$$ I_{m} = \frac{V_m}{R_{eq}} = \frac{325.27}{52.9} = 6.15 \,\text{A} $$

**Step 4:** RMS input current (assuming ideal sinusoid).

$$ I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = 4.35 \,\text{A} $$

**Step 5:** Verify PF = 1 (ideal case).

$$ PF = \frac{P_{in}}{V_{rms} I_{rms}} = \frac{1000}{230 \cdot 4.35} = 1.00 \;\checkmark $$

**Step 6:** Maximum duty ratio (at the input voltage zero crossing).

$$ D_{max} = 1 - \frac{0}{400} = 1.0 $$

> [!WARNING]
> $D = 1$ at zero crossing is theoretical. In practice, the duty cycle is **clamped** to a maximum (e.g., 0.95) to leave margin for current control. The current loop bandwidth and switching frequency determine how close to the zero crossing the controller can track.

**Step 7:** Minimum duty ratio (at the input voltage peak).

$$ D_{min} = 1 - \frac{325.27}{400} = 0.187 $$

> [!NOTE]
> The duty ratio therefore varies sinusoidally in time between approximately 0.19 (at peak) and ~1.0 (at zero crossing) at twice the line frequency.

## 3.4 Derivation: Inductor Sizing for CCM Boost PFC

The inductor must limit the **switching-frequency ripple** to an acceptable value (typically 20–40% of the peak line current). The peak-to-peak ripple during the switch-ON interval is:

$$ \Delta i_L = \frac{V_{in} \cdot D T_s}{L} $$

Using $D = 1 - V_{in}/V_o$:

$$ \Delta i_L = \frac{V_{in} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_o}\right) T_s}{L} = \frac{V_{in}(V_o - V_{in}) T_s}{L \cdot V_o} $$

Maximum ripple occurs at $V_{in} = V_o/2$ (i.e., when the boost operates at 50% duty):

$$ \Delta i_{L,max} = \frac{V_o T_s}{4 L} $$

Therefore, the minimum inductance for a specified maximum ripple $\Delta i_{L,max}$ is:

$$ L_{min} = \frac{V_o T_s}{4 \, \Delta i_{L,max}} = \frac{V_o}{4 f_s \, \Delta i_{L,max}} $$

### 3.4.1 Numerical Example (continued)

Take $V_o = 400\,\text{V}$, switching frequency $f_s = 50\,\text{kHz}$, target ripple $\Delta i_{L,max} = 1.5\,\text{A}$ (≈ 25% of $I_m = 6.15\,\text{A}$).

**Step 1:** Compute $L_{min}$.

$$ L_{min} = \frac{400}{4 \cdot 50000 \cdot 1.5} = \frac{400}{300000} = 1.33 \,\text{mH} $$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, a slightly larger inductor (e.g., 1.5–2 mH) is chosen to provide margin and account for tolerance, temperature, and worst-case input voltage variations.

## 3.5 Derivation: Output Capacitor Sizing from Hold-Up Requirement

A critical design constraint for AC-DC converters is **hold-up time**: the converter must keep the output within regulation for a specified time (typically 16.7 ms — one full 50 Hz cycle) after loss of AC input.

During hold-up, the output capacitor supplies the load. The energy balance is:

$$ \frac{1}{2} C \left( V_o^2 - V_{o,min}^2 \right) = P_o \cdot t_{hold} $$

Solving for $C$:

$$ C = \frac{2 P_o \cdot t_{hold}}{V_o^2 - V_{o,min}^2} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A larger output capacitor improves hold-up but **degrades PF and increases bulk** — a key design trade-off in PFC converters. The boost topology's high output voltage (≈ 400 V) actually **reduces** the required capacitance for a given hold-up energy.

## 3.6 Summary of Key Engineering Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Benefit | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Higher $f_s$ | Smaller L, C, faster response | Higher switching loss, EMI |
| Higher $V_o$ | Smaller $C$ for hold-up, smaller ripple | Higher voltage stress on switch & diode |
| Larger L | Lower ripple, easier control | Bigger, heavier, more copper loss |
| CCM (vs DCM) | Lower peak/rms current, easier filtering | Requires current sensor, complex control |
| Average current mode | Excellent noise immunity, low distortion | Two-loop control, slower transient |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a Single-Phase Boost PFC

This diagram shows how the boost PFC sits between the AC mains and the DC load, with its control system forming a closed loop around input current and output voltage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACMAINS["AC MAINS 230 V 50 Hz"]
        VS["V_sin Supply"]
    end

    subgraph FRONT["FRONT END POWER STAGE"]
        RECT["Diode Bridge Rectifier"]
        LBOOST["Boost Inductor L"]
        SW["Power Switch MOSFET"]
        DB["Boost Diode D_b"]
        CBULK["Output Capacitor C_bus"]
    end

    subgraph LOAD["DC LOAD"]
        RLOAD["DC Load V_o 400 V"]
    end

    subgraph SENSE["SENSING NETWORK"]
        VIN_S["V_in Sense"]
        IIN_S["I_in Sense via Shunt"]
        VO_S["V_o Divider"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL IC Average Current Mode"]
        MULT["Multiplier X V_in V_err"]
        VERR["Voltage Error Amplifier V_err"]
        IERR["Current Error Amplifier I_err"]
        PWM["PWM Modulator"]
        GD["Gate Driver"]
    end

    VS --> RECT
    RECT --> LBOOST
    LBOOST --> SW
    LBOOST --> DB
    DB --> CBULK
    CBULK --> RLOAD
    SW -.-> GND1["Return"]

    VIN_S --> MULT
    VO_S --> VERR
    VERR --> MULT
    MULT --> IERR
    IIN_S --> IERR
    IERR --> PWM
    PWM --> GD
    GD --> SW

    style VS fill:#cce5ff
    style RECT fill:#ffe0b3
    style LBOOST fill:#ffe0b3
    style SW fill:#ffe0b3
    style DB fill:#ffe0b3
    style CBULK fill:#ffe0b3
    style RLOAD fill:#d4edda
    style MULT fill:#fff3cd
    style VERR fill:#fff3cd
    style IERR fill:#fff3cd
    style PWM fill:#fff3cd
    style GD fill:#fff3cd
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The power path flows top-to-bottom (AC → Rectifier → Boost stage → DC load). The control path flows right-to-left (sensors → error amplifiers → PWM → gate driver → switch). Note the **multiplier** — its role is to multiply the (slow) voltage error signal by an instantaneous sample of the **rectified input voltage** so that the current reference has the **sinusoidal shape** of the line.

## 4.2 Control Scheme Block Diagram — Average Current Mode

This is the canonical control structure of a boost PFC using **average current mode control** (used in industry-standard controllers such as the UC3854).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VREF["V_ref 3.5 V typical"]
    VOSNS["V_o Sense kV"]
    VERRAMP["Voltage Error Amplifier Gv_s"]
    VCOMP["V_comp Compensated Error Signal DC"]

    VMUL["Multiplier"]
    VINSNS["V_in Sense Squared v_in_squared"]
    ISQR["V_in_squared Divider"]

    IREF["i_ref Sinusoidal Reference"]
    ISNS["i_L Sense via Shunt Resistor"]
    IERRAMP["Current Error Amplifier Gi_s"]
    ICOMP["PWM Comparator Input"]
    RAMP["50 to 100 kHz Sawtooth Carrier"]
    PWM["PWM Output"]
    DRV["Isolated Gate Driver"]
    SWNODE["Power Switch Gate of Q_boost"]

    VREF --> VERRAMP
    VOSNS --> VERRAMP
    VERRAMP --> VCOMP
    VCOMP --> VMUL
    VINSNS --> ISQR
    ISQR --> VMUL
    VMUL --> IREF
    IREF --> IERRAMP
    ISNS --> IERRAMP
    IERRAMP --> ICOMP
    RAMP --> ICOMP
    ICOMP --> PWM
    PWM --> DRV
    DRV --> SWNODE
    SWNODE -.->|"Feedback through L"| ISNS

    style VREF fill:#f8d7da
    style VERRAMP fill:#fff3cd
    style VMUL fill:#d1ecf1
    style IERRAMP fill:#fff3cd
    style PWM fill:#d4edda
    style DRV fill:#d4edda
    style SWNODE fill:#ffe0b3
```

### 4.2.1 How the Control Loop Works

**1. Outer (slow) voltage loop:**
- Samples output voltage $V_o$ through a divider.
- Compares to an internal reference (e.g., 3.5 V).
- The voltage error amplifier (with low bandwidth — typically 10–20 Hz) produces $V_{comp}$, a slowly varying DC signal proportional to the **power demanded** by the load.

**2. Multiplier stage:**
- Squares the (rectified, scaled) input voltage sample: $v_{in}^2$.
- Divides $V_{comp}$ by $v_{in}^2$ — this is the **feed-forward** function. It makes the loop gain independent of AC input voltage.
- Output: $i_{ref}(t) = k \cdot v_{in}(t) \cdot V_{comp}$ — a **sinusoidal reference current** whose amplitude is controlled by $V_{comp}$.

**3. Inner (fast) current loop:**
- Compares the actual inductor current $i_L$ to $i_{ref}$.
- The current error amplifier (high bandwidth — typically a few kHz to one-tenth of $f_s$) generates the control voltage for the PWM comparator.
- A fixed-frequency sawtooth (50–100 kHz) is compared to this error voltage to generate the PWM gate signal.

**4. Result:**
- The inductor current is forced to track the **shape of the line voltage**, scaled to deliver the demanded power.
- The output voltage is regulated to a constant value.
- PF approaches unity, THD < 5%.

## 4.3 Harmonic Spectrum Comparison — Uncorrected vs Active PFC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NA["BEFORE: Diode Rectifier with C Filter"]
        F1A["Fundamental 50 Hz Large"]
        H3A["3rd 150 Hz Large 70 to 80 percent"]
        H5A["5th 250 Hz Large 50 to 60 percent"]
        H7A["7th 350 Hz Notable 30 percent"]
        HHA["Higher order smaller but many"]
    end
    subgraph NB["AFTER: Active PFC Boost"]
        F1B["Fundamental 50 Hz Same magnitude"]
        H3B["3rd 150 Hz Below 2 percent"]
        H5B["5th 250 Hz Below 2 percent"]
        H7B["7th 350 Hz Below 1 percent"]
        HHB["Higher order negligible"]
    end
    NA -->|"Active PFC"| NB
    style NA fill:#f8d7da
    style NB fill:#d4edda
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the spectrum:** A standard capacitive-filtered diode rectifier has a THD of 65–100% — the fundamental is often less than the 3rd harmonic in magnitude! An Active PFC boost reduces each individual harmonic to below 3% and the THD to < 5%, with the fundamental becoming overwhelmingly dominant — exactly what IEEE 519 and IEC 61000-3-2 require.

## 4.4 Waveform Sketch — Voltage and Current (Before vs After PFC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE PFC: Distorted Current"]
        VSB["V_sine 50 Hz pure sinusoid"]
        ISB["I_line narrow pulse near each peak of V_sine"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER["AFTER PFC: Shaped Current"]
        VSA["V_sine 50 Hz pure sinusoid"]
        ISA["I_line in phase sinusoid, near unity PF"]
    end
    BEFORE -->|"PFC Controller"| AFTER
    style BEFORE fill:#f8d7da
    style AFTER fill:#d4edda
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key visual takeaway:** Both waveforms use the **same** supply voltage. The only change is the **shape of the current** drawn from the mains. Before PFC, the current consists of short, high-amplitude peaks. After PFC, the current is a smooth sinusoid in phase with the voltage — a near-ideal "resistive" load.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of current. Why is it a critical parameter for utility interface design?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) of current is the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components (excluding the fundamental) to the RMS value of the fundamental component, expressed as a percentage.
>
> $$ THD_I = \frac{\sqrt{I_{2,rms}^2 + I_{3,rms}^2 + \cdots + I_{n,rms}^2}}{I_{1,rms}} \times 100\% $$
>
> **Significance for utility interface:**
> 1. **Direct measure of waveform pollution** injected into the AC mains.
> 2. **Governs true RMS current** for a given real power → determines feeder, transformer, and cable sizing.
> 3. **Determines true power factor** through the relation $PF = \cos\phi_1 / \sqrt{1+THD^2}$.
> 4. **Standardized by IEEE 519** and **IEC 61000-3-2** — non-compliance makes equipment illegal to sell in regulated markets.
> 5. High THD causes **overheating, voltage distortion, resonance, and protection misoperation** on the utility side.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Distinguish between Displacement Power Factor (DPF) and True Power Factor. Give one example where DPF = 1 but true PF is poor.**

**Model Answer:**

> | Parameter | Displacement Power Factor (DPF) | True Power Factor (PF) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Defined using | Fundamental voltage and **fundamental** current | **Total** voltage and **total** current |
> | Formula | $\cos \phi_1$ | $P / (V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms})$ |
> | Captures | Phase shift between fundamental voltage and current | Both phase shift **and** waveform distortion |
> | Range | 0 to 1 | 0 to 1 |
> | Affected by | Reactance, firing angle, line frequency | Reactance + all harmonics |
>
> **Example:** A **single-phase diode bridge rectifier with a large smoothing capacitor** has the fundamental current in phase with the voltage (DPF ≈ 1) but a very peaked, non-sinusoidal current waveform. THD may be 80–120%, giving a true PF of 0.6–0.7 even with DPF = 1.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7 across two sub-parts. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**A (a) (7 Marks):** Explain the need for an improved utility interface in modern power electronic systems. Discuss, with relevant equations, how current harmonics degrade power factor.

**A (b) (7 Marks):** State the salient features of the **IEEE 519-2014** standard. With a neat sketch, explain how harmonic limits vary with the short-circuit ratio $I_{SC}/I_L$ at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC).

#### Model Solution — A (a)

**Step 1 — Context.** Modern power electronic loads (SMPS, motor drives, LED drivers, EV chargers) interface the AC utility through a **rectifier** followed by a **smoothing capacitor**. The capacitor charges only near the voltage peaks, drawing **short, high-amplitude current pulses**. The Fourier decomposition of this current contains a fundamental plus strong odd harmonics (3rd, 5th, 7th, …).

**Step 2 — Why it matters.** The non-sinusoidal current causes:
- Overheating of utility transformers and neutral conductors (especially with 3rd-order triplen harmonics in three-phase systems).
- Voltage distortion at the PCC, propagating to other consumers.
- Resonance with PF-correction capacitor banks.
- Higher RMS current for a given real power → wasted capacity, higher $I^2R$ losses.
- Failure to comply with IEEE 519 / IEC 61000-3-2.

**Step 3 — Power Factor Equation.** The true power factor of a non-linear load is:

$$ PF = \frac{\cos \phi_1}{\sqrt{1 + THD_I^2}} $$

For a typical diode-capacitor rectifier, DPF is near 1, but THD is 60–120%, giving a true PF of 0.45–0.85. The relationship shows that **even with a unity-displacement angle, harmonic distortion alone can pull PF well below 1**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** An "improved utility interface" must force the input current to be a clean sinusoid in phase with the line voltage. This is achieved by **Active Power Factor Correction** — typically using a **front-end boost converter** with closed-loop current shaping.

#### Model Solution — A (b)

**Salient Features of IEEE 519-2014:**

1. **System-level standard** — applies at the Point of Common Coupling (PCC), not at individual appliances.
2. **Limits both current and voltage harmonics.**
3. **Uses TDD (Total Demand Distortion)** instead of THD for current — TDD is referenced to the **maximum demand load current** $I_L$, not the instantaneous fundamental current.
4. **Harmonic limits depend on the short-circuit ratio** $I_{SC}/I_L$:
   - Higher ratio (stiffer utility, smaller load) → more lenient limits.
   - Lower ratio (weaker utility, larger load) → stricter limits.
5. **Voltage distortion limits** depend on the system voltage level (stricter for higher voltages).
6. **Odd harmonics** are limited more leniently than even harmonics; sub-multiples of 3 (triplens) get special attention in three-phase systems.

**Variation of current limits with $I_{SC}/I_L$ (illustrative table):**

| $I_{SC}/I_L$ | Max individual harmonic (h < 11) | Max TDD |
|---|---|---|
| < 20 | 4.0% | 5.0% |
| 20–50 | 7.0% | 8.0% |
| 50–100 | 10.0% | 12.0% |
| 100–1000 | 12.0% | 15.0% |
| > 1000 | 15.0% | 20.0% |

> [!NOTE]
> A simple sketch should show $I_{SC}/I_L$ on the x-axis (log scale) and the TDD limit on the y-axis, showing a **monotonically increasing** allowed limit as the load becomes smaller relative to the utility's short-circuit capacity. The board examiner typically expects the shape and the numeric values at the breakpoints.

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B (a) (7 Marks):** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **single-phase front-end boost converter** used for active power factor correction. Derive the expression for the voltage gain in CCM.

**B (b) (7 Marks):** Draw the block diagram of the **average current mode control** scheme for the boost PFC and explain the function of each block.

#### Model Solution — B (a)

**Circuit Diagram (described in words, to be drawn in exam):**

The single-phase boost PFC consists of:
- AC mains (230 V, 50 Hz).
- **Diode bridge rectifier** ($D_1$–$D_4$) → full-wave rectified DC, $\vert v_{ac} \vert = V_m \vert \sin \omega t \vert$.
- **Boost inductor $L$** in series with the positive DC rail from the rectifier.
- **Power switch $S$** (MOSFET) connected from the node between $L$ and the boost diode to the negative DC rail.
- **Boost diode $D_b$** in series, anode at the switch node, cathode at the DC bus.
- **Output capacitor $C$** across the DC bus, holding $V_o \approx 400\,\text{V}$.
- DC load (the downstream system).

**Operation in CCM (Continuous Conduction Mode):**

Let the instantaneous rectified input voltage be $V_{in}(t) = V_m \vert \sin \omega t \vert$.

**Mode 1 — Switch ON (duration $DT_s$):**
- The MOSFET conducts; $D_b$ is reverse-biased.
- The inductor is connected across $V_{in}(t)$. Current rises linearly:
  $$ \frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}(t)}{L} $$
- The output capacitor supplies the load. $V_o$ droops slightly.

**Mode 2 — Switch OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$):**
- The MOSFET blocks; $D_b$ conducts.
- The inductor releases energy to the load and capacitor. Voltage across $L$ is $V_{in}(t) - V_o$ (negative).
- Inductor current falls linearly:
  $$ \frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_{in}(t) - V_o}{L} $$
- The capacitor is recharged.

**Steady-State Volt-Second Balance on $L$:**

$$ V_{in} \cdot D T_s + (V_{in} - V_o)(1-D)T_s = 0 $$

Dividing by $T_s$ and expanding:

$$ V_{in} D + V_{in} - V_{in} D - V_o (1-D) = 0 $$

$$ V_{in} - V_o(1-D) = 0 $$

$$ \boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1-D}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For PFC, $V_{in}(t) = V_m \vert \sin \omega t \vert$ varies slowly (50 Hz), and the duty ratio $D(t) = 1 - V_{in}(t)/V_o$ is adjusted rapidly (at $f_s = 50$–$100$ kHz) so that the average input current is forced to be **sinusoidal and in phase** with $v_{ac}(t)$.

#### Model Solution — B (b)

**Block Diagram (Average Current Mode Control):**

The closed-loop has two cascaded loops:

**Outer voltage loop (slow, ~10 Hz bandwidth):**
1. **$V_o$ sensor** (resistive divider + scaling) → scaled $V_o$ sample.
2. **Voltage error amplifier** — compares sample to internal reference $V_{ref}$ (e.g., 3.5 V). Output: $V_{comp}$, a slowly varying DC signal proportional to load power demand.
3. **Feed-forward block** — squares a sample of $\vert v_{ac}(t) \vert$ to produce $v_{in}^2$, dividing $V_{comp}$ by this to **decouple** the current reference from line voltage variations.

**Multiplier:** Computes $i_{ref}(t) = (V_{comp}/v_{in}^2) \cdot v_{in}(t) = k \cdot v_{in}(t) \cdot V_{comp}$ — a **sinusoidal reference** whose amplitude is set by the load demand.

**Inner current loop (fast, ~few kHz bandwidth):**
4. **Current sensor** — typically a low-side shunt resistor in the source of the MOSFET, or a Hall-effect sensor.
5. **Current error amplifier** — compares $i_{ref}(t)$ to $i_L(t)$. Produces a control voltage for the PWM.
6. **PWM modulator** — compares the current error to a fixed-frequency (50–100 kHz) sawtooth carrier. Generates the gate signal.
7. **Gate driver** — provides level-shifting and high-current drive to the MOSFET gate.

**Function summary table (must be reproduced in the answer):**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| $V_o$ sensor | Scale down DC bus voltage to reference level |
| Voltage error amplifier | Generate $V_{comp}$ — sets power demand |
| Feed-forward | $V_{comp}/v_{in}^2$ — independence from line voltage |
| Multiplier | Shape current reference as sinusoid |
| Current sensor | Measure inductor current |
| Current error amplifier | Force $i_L$ to track $i_{ref}$ |
| PWM + sawtooth | Convert error to duty ratio |
| Gate driver | Drive MOSFET gate |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse THD and TDD** — THD uses $I_{1,rms}$ in the denominator, TDD uses $I_L$ (maximum demand). Mixing these up is a common 2-mark loss in Part A and 3-mark loss in Part B.
> 2. **Always show the input voltage and the inductor current on the same axes** when drawing boost PFC waveforms. Examiners deduct marks if the current shape is not visibly **sinusoidal and in phase**.
> 3. **State the assumption of CCM** explicitly in any derivation of voltage gain — in DCM the gain expression is different.
> 4. **Do not forget the multiplier block** in the control diagram; it is the heart of active shaping and is often the most-marked item.
> 5. **Use \vert or \mid, not the pipe character**, when writing absolute values in formulas during the exam — a typographical error here can cost clarity marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Core Concepts
- **Need for improved utility interface:** Diode-capacitor rectifiers draw non-sinusoidal, peaky currents that pollute the mains with harmonics and degrade power factor.
- **Current harmonics:** Generated by non-linear loads; quantified by **THD** (relative to fundamental) or **TDD** (relative to maximum demand).
- **Power factor** has two components: **DPF** (phase shift of fundamental) and **DF** (distortion from harmonics). $PF = DPF \cdot DF = \cos\phi_1 / \sqrt{1+THD^2}$.
- **IEEE 519-2014:** System-level standard setting **current and voltage harmonic limits** at the PCC; limits tighten as $I_{SC}/I_L$ decreases.
- **Active shaping:** Use of a **switch-mode converter** (typically boost) controlled so that the input current tracks the input voltage waveform → unity PF, low THD.
- **Single-phase front-end boost converter:** Diode bridge → boost L → MOSFET switch → boost diode → output capacitor → DC load. Output regulated to ~ 400 V from 230 V AC mains.

### Critical Equations to Memorize
- Boost voltage gain: $V_o/V_{in} = 1/(1-D)$.
- Inductor ripple: $\Delta i_L = V_{in} D T_s / L$.
- Critical inductance: $L_{crit} = V_{in} D(1-D) T_s / (2 I_{L,avg})$.
- Power factor: $PF = \cos\phi_1 / \sqrt{1+THD^2}$.
- TDD: $TDD = \sqrt{\sum I_{n,rms}^2} / I_L$.
- Emulated resistance: $R_{eq} = V_m^2 / (2 P_o)$.
- Output capacitor (hold-up): $C = 2 P_o t_{hold} / (V_o^2 - V_{o,min}^2)$.

### Control Loop Essentials
- **Two-loop structure:** Slow voltage loop (sets power demand) + fast current loop (shapes waveform).
- **Multiplier** combines $V_{comp}$ with the **rectified input voltage** to create a **sinusoidal current reference**.
- **Feed-forward** ($V_{comp}/v_{in}^2$) makes the loop gain independent of AC line voltage — **essential for universal-input (90–265 V AC) PFC**.

### Standards Snapshot
- **IEEE 519-2014** — system-level harmonic limits at PCC.
- **IEC 61000-3-2** — equipment-level harmonic limits (Class A, B, C, D).
- **EN 61000-3-2 Class D** — applies to personal computers, monitors, TVs > 75 W; **requires Active PFC** to comply.

### Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Writing THD with a **pipe character** `|` instead of `\vert` for absolute value.
- ❌ Confusing THD and TDD denominators.
- ❌ Forgetting that in PFC, **D varies at twice the line frequency**, not at a constant value.
- ❌ Drawing the boost switch on the **wrong side** of the inductor (must be on the **load side** of $L$ for proper boost operation).
- ❌ Claiming the output voltage of a boost PFC equals $V_m$ — it is regulated **higher** than $V_m$ (typically 400 V from 325 V peak).
- ❌ Forgetting to mention **feed-forward** when describing average current mode control.

### Quick Self-Check Before the Exam
✅ Can I draw the boost PFC power circuit **from memory** with all labels?
✅ Can I derive $V_o/V_{in} = 1/(1-D)$ using volt-second balance?
✅ Can I state the **PF vs THD** equation and apply it to a numerical problem?
✅ Can I list at least **three** differences between IEEE 519 and IEC 61000-3-2?
✅ Can I explain the **role of the multiplier** in the control loop without looking at notes?
✅ Do I know the **practical output voltage** target (380–400 V) for 230 V AC universal-input PFC?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Reminder:** This topic carries significant weight in Module III and is a **favourite of KTU examiners** — expect at least one Part A (3 marks) and either Part A or Part B (7 or 14 marks) every exam cycle. Master the **circuit diagram, the gain derivation, the PF equation, and the control block diagram** — these four items cover roughly 80% of the marks on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives
> - **Module:** Module- IV
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Drives - Advantages of Adjustable Speed Electric Drives, Block Diagram, Types of Loads, Classification of Load Torque, Motor Torque-load Combination: Characteristics and Dynamic Equation, Steady State Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Drive — Formal Definition

An **Electric Drive** is an electromechanical energy conversion system that uses an electric motor as the prime mover, in conjunction with a power electronic converter, control circuitry, and mechanical transmission, to deliver controlled motion (speed, torque, or position) to a driven load. As per KTU 2024 nomenclature, the drive is a *closed-loop* or *open-loop* integrated package where the **power-processing unit**, **motor**, and **load** interact through a well-defined mechanical shaft and feedback structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim from 2024 Scheme syllabus):**
> *"An electric drive is a system employed for motion control which utilizes an electric motor as the prime mover along with its associated power electronic converter, control equipment and mechanical transmission to achieve the desired speed, torque and position of the driven equipment."*

The four fundamental blocks of **any** electric drive are:
1. **Power Source** — Supply mains, battery, or DC bus.
2. **Power Electronic Converter** — Rectifier, chopper, inverter, or cycloconverter.
3. **Electric Motor** — DC, induction, synchronous, PMSM, SRM, BLDC, stepper.
4. **Mechanical Load** — Coupled to the shaft through gears, belts, or direct coupling.

## 1.2 Adjustable Speed Electric Drive (ASD)

An **Adjustable Speed Drive** is an electric drive in which the **speed of the motor can be varied smoothly over a wide range** (typically 1:20 or higher) and held at a desired set-point with high accuracy, irrespective of load fluctuations. This is achieved using **power electronic converters** (V/f control, vector control, DTC, etc.) and modern digital controllers (DSP/FPGA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from "Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)":** A VFD is a *subset* of ASD — it specifically varies the frequency of AC supply. ASD is the broader term covering VFDs, DC-DC chopper drives, soft starters, and servo drives.

## 1.3 Advantages of Adjustable Speed Electric Drives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-weightage KTU 2024 topic** — frequently appearing as a 3-mark Part-A or 7-mark Part-B question.

| # | Advantage | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Energy Conservation** | Affinity laws: For fans/pumps, power ∝ (speed)³. A 20 % speed reduction → ~49 % power saving. |
| 2 | **Improved Process Control** | Tight regulation of speed, torque, and position improves product quality. |
| 3 | **Soft Starting** | Limits inrush current to **1.5–2×** rated (vs. **6–8×** for DOL starting). |
| 4 | **Reversal & Braking** | Plugging, regenerative, and dynamic braking easily implemented via converter. |
| 5 | **Reduced Mechanical Stress** | Eliminates shock loading on belts, gears, couplings. |
| 6 | **Lower Maintenance Cost** | Fewer moving parts, no contactors, no mechanical speed-changing gearboxes. |
| 7 | **Higher Power Factor** | Modern IGBT-based PWM rectifiers achieve **near-unity** power factor. |
| 8 | **Compact Installation** | Floor space reduction up to **60 %** compared to hydraulic/mechanical drives. |
| 9 | **Better Speed Range** | Turn-down ratio of **1:50** or higher achievable. |
| 10 | **Telemetry & Diagnostics** | Digital buses (Modbus, CAN, Profibus) enable remote monitoring. |

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Cruise Control of a Car

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. Your **right foot on the accelerator** is the *power electronic converter* controlling energy flow. The **engine** is the *electric motor*. The **road slope, wind drag, and car weight** constitute the *load torque*. **Cruise control** automatically adjusts the accelerator to maintain a constant speed (set-point) even when you go uphill (load disturbance).

- In a **DOL (Direct-On-Line) starter** without ASD, your car would always run at "full throttle" — you cannot control speed precisely.
- With an **ASD**, you become the cruise-control system — adjusting power flow to the engine so that the car maintains exactly the speed you want, regardless of the road's demands.
- The **fuel saving** is analogous to the *energy savings* in industrial fans and pumps using ASDs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world quantitative insight:** A **7.5 kW centrifugal pump** with conventional throttling wastes ~**₹1.5 lakh/year** in excess energy. Installing an ASD with 20 % speed reduction recovers ~**₹80,000/year** (assuming industrial tariff of ₹8/unit, 4000 running hours).

## 1.5 Standard Physical Quantities & SI Units

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Range in Drives |
|----------|--------|---------|--------------------------|
| Speed | $\omega$ or $N$ | rad/s or rpm | 0 – 3000 rpm |
| Torque | $T$ | N·m | 0.1 – 10,000 N·m |
| Power | $P$ | W (or kW, MW) | 0.1 kW – 10 MW |
| Moment of Inertia | $J$ | kg·m² | 0.001 – 1000 kg·m² |
| Friction Coefficient | $B$ | N·m·s/rad | 0.001 – 1.0 |
| Slip | $s$ | dimensionless | 0 – 1 |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram of an Electric Drive System

The generalized block diagram of an electric drive consists of **five functional blocks** arranged in a feedback control structure. Each block has a specific role:

### 2.1.1 Functional Description of Each Block

1. **Power Supply Source**
   - Provides raw electrical energy.
   - Types: Single-phase/three-phase AC mains, DC battery, solar PV array, fuel cell.

2. **Power Modulator (Power Electronic Converter)**
   - The *heart* of the drive.
   - Converts fixed-magnitude, fixed-frequency supply to variable-magnitude/variable-frequency form suitable for the motor.
   - Components: Diodes, thyristors (SCR), power BJTs, MOSFETs, **IGBTs**, IGCTs.
   - Topologies: **Rectifier** (AC→DC), **Chopper** (DC→DC), **Inverter** (DC→AC), **Cycloconverter** (AC→AC).

3. **Electric Motor (Prime Mover)**
   - Converts electrical energy into mechanical energy.
   - Categories: DC motor, Induction motor, Synchronous motor, PMSM, SRM, BLDC.

4. **Control Unit (Signal-Level Electronics)**
   - Generates firing pulses / PWM signals for the power modulator.
   - Implements control algorithms: V/f, vector control, DTC, field-oriented control (FOC).
   - Modern controllers: 32-bit microcontrollers, **DSPs** (TMS320F28335), **FPGAs** (Xilinx Spartan-6).

5. **Sensing Unit (Feedback Transducers)**
   - **Speed sensor**: Tacho-generator, incremental encoder (1024 ppr), resolver.
   - **Current sensor**: Hall-effect CT (LEM LA-55P), shunt resistor.
   - **Position sensor**: Absolute encoder, resolver, Hall sensors (for BLDC).
   - **Voltage sensor**: Potential divider, isolation amplifier.

6. **Mechanical Load**
   - The end-application device (pump, fan, compressor, conveyor, robotic arm, EV wheels).

### 2.1.2 Detailed Power Flow Equation

The **input electrical power** is processed by the converter, transferred to the motor (subject to converter efficiency $\eta_c$), and the motor converts it to mechanical power (subject to motor efficiency $\eta_m$):

$$
P_{mech} = \eta_c \cdot \eta_m \cdot P_{electrical}
$$

## 2.2 Types of Loads (Load Classification by Speed-Torque Characteristic)

Loads are categorized into **four principal types** based on the mathematical relationship between load torque $T_L$ and speed $\omega$:

### 2.2.1 Type 1 — Constant Torque Load

$$
T_L = \text{constant} \quad (\text{independent of speed})
$$

- **Examples**: Conveyors, hoists, elevators, extruders, positive-displacement compressors, mixers, cranes.
- **Physical reason**: Work done per revolution is fixed (e.g., lifting a constant weight).
- **Power varies linearly**: $P_L = T_L \cdot \omega \propto \omega$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiner tip:** A constant-torque load demands the *same torque* at all speeds, but the *power* demand grows linearly with speed. Motor rating must be selected based on **maximum torque**, not power.

### 2.2.2 Type 2 — Linearly Increasing Torque Load

$$
T_L = k_1 \cdot \omega
$$

- **Examples**: Calenders (rolling mills), viscous-friction-dominated systems, generators feeding a constant resistance.
- **Power varies quadratically**: $P_L = T_L \cdot \omega = k_1 \omega^2 \propto \omega^2$

### 2.2.3 Type 3 — Parabolic (Quadratic) Torque Load — Most Common in Industry

$$
T_L = k_2 \cdot \omega^2
$$

- **Examples**: Centrifugal pumps, fans, blowers, mixers (fluid-friction), propellers.
- **Physical reason**: Centrifugal force is proportional to $\omega^2$; fluid drag depends on $\omega^2$.
- **Power varies cubically**: $P_L = k_2 \omega^3 \propto \omega^3$ — this is the **Affinity Law** exploited for energy savings.
- **Affinity Law (Fan/Pump Law)**:
  - $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$
  - $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$
  - $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 20 % speed reduction → only $(0.8)^3 = 0.512$ i.e. **~49 % power saving**. This is the dominant economic argument for ASDs in HVAC and water-treatment industries.

### 2.2.4 Type 4 — Constant Power Load

$$
P_L = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_L = \dfrac{P_L}{\omega} \propto \dfrac{1}{\omega}
$$

- **Examples**: Machine tool spindles, winding reels, traction motors at constant kW region.
- **Physical reason**: Material cross-section or tension requires constant power regardless of diameter.
- **Special note**: The drive must operate in **field-weakening region** to deliver constant power at high speeds.

### 2.2.5 Type 5 — Impact / Pulse / Intermittent Loads

- **Examples**: Punch presses, rolling mills (peak load between idle), hammer mills.
- **Characterized by**: Peak torque (up to 4× rated) for short durations; thermal rating of motor must withstand RMS, not peak.
- **RMS torque** (most common KTU expression):

$$
T_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{T_1^2 t_1 + T_2^2 t_2 + T_3^2 t_3 + \ldots}{t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + \ldots}}
$$

### 2.2.6 Combined Load Torque

In many real systems, the total load is a **sum of components**:

$$
T_L(\omega) = T_0 + k_1 \omega + k_2 \omega^2
$$

where:
- $T_0$ = **dry (Coulomb) friction** — speed-independent.
- $k_1 \omega$ = **viscous friction** (linear in speed).
- $k_2 \omega^2$ = **aerodynamic / fluid friction** (quadratic).

## 2.3 Classification of Load Torque (Sign Convention)

This is a **favourite KTU 2024 question** — distinguishes the *sign behaviour* of torque under motoring vs. generating.

### 2.3.1 Passive Load Torque

- **Direction** is **opposing motion** (always against $\omega$).
- **Sign** reverses when $\omega$ reverses.
- **Examples**: Friction, fan, pump, wind-load, conveyor.
- **Characteristic**: $T_L = f(\omega)$ with $T_L$ opposite to $\omega$.

### 2.3.2 Active Load Torque (Potential Energy Driven)

- **Magnitude** is **independent of speed** (and often of $\omega$).
- **Sign** is **fixed** by the physical setup — does *not* reverse with $\omega$.
- **Examples**:
  - **Hoist lifting weight**: $T_L$ always opposes motor (acts downward on load).
  - **Loaded train going down a gradient**: $T_L$ actually *assists* motion (acts in the direction of $\omega$).
- **Implication**: Active torque can **drive the motor in generator mode** (regenerative braking, four-quadrant operation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Four-Quadrant Operation:** A hoist has: (Q1) Hoisting up, (Q3) Lowering down, (Q2/Q4) Braking during up/down. Active load torque is the reason a **4-quadrant drive** is needed.

### 2.3.3 Summary Table — Passive vs. Active Load

| Property | Passive Load Torque | Active Load Torque |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Speed dependence | Strongly speed-dependent | Independent of speed |
| Sign with $\omega$ reversal | Reverses | Does **not** reverse |
| Energy interaction | Always absorbs energy | Can **release** energy |
| Typical example | Fan, friction | Hoist, elevator |
| Drive requirement | 1- or 2-quadrant | **4-quadrant** |
| Regenerative braking | Not possible (load cannot push motor) | Possible (load drives motor) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Power-torque-speed relationship (W) |
| 2 | $T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$ | **Fundamental dynamic equation** of motor-load system |
| 3 | $T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Simplified form (neglecting $B\omega$ for high-inertia systems) |
| 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Conversion from rpm to rad/s |
| 5 | $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L \left(\dfrac{N_L}{N_m}\right)^2$ | Reflected load inertia to motor shaft |
| 6 | $T_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum T_i^2 t_i}{\sum t_i}}$ | RMS torque for cyclic duty (thermal rating) |
| 7 | $T_L = k \omega^n$ | Generalized load characteristic ($n = 0, 1, 2, -1$) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ | Fan/Pump affinity law (cubic) |
| 9 | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Flow affinity law (linear) |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega} \bigg\vert_{op} > \dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega} \bigg\vert_{op}$ | **Steady-state stability criterion** |
| 11 | $T_{eq} = T_e - T_L - B\omega = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Net accelerating torque |
| 12 | $J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow T_e = T_L$ | Steady-state condition |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Dynamic Equation

The dynamic equation $T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ is the **single most important equation in drive engineering**. It enables:

- **Selection of motor rating** (peak torque, RMS torque).
- **Estimation of starting/braking time**:
  $$
  t_s = \int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} \dfrac{J \, d\omega}{T_e(\omega) - T_L(\omega)}
  $$
- **Stability analysis** (Section 2.6 below).
- **Controller design** (PI tuning of speed loop, current loop bandwidth).
- **Energy consumption calculation** in EV/HEVs (driving cycles).
