# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Motor-Load Dynamic Equation

This derivation is the **mathematical heart** of Module IV and is asked almost every KTU exam cycle.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Newton's Second Law for Rotational Motion

For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, the angular acceleration $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ is caused by the **net torque** acting on it:

$$
\tau_{net} = J \alpha = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}
$$

where:
- $\tau_{net}$ = algebraic sum of all torques (N·m),
- $J$ = moment of inertia about the axis of rotation (kg·m²),
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s).

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Identify the Torques Acting on the Motor-Load Shaft

Three distinct torque components act on the common shaft:

| Torque | Symbol | Origin |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Electromagnetic torque developed by motor | $T_e$ | Electrical input to motor (drives the shaft) |
| Load torque (resistive + active) | $T_L$ | Mechanical load (opposes motion) |
| Frictional torque (rotational loss) | $T_f = B \omega$ | Bearing friction, windage, brush friction |

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Apply Sign Convention

We adopt the **motor-convention** where $T_e$ is treated positive (drives the shaft in the positive direction) and $T_L$ is treated as a load opposing $T_e$:

$$
\tau_{net} = T_e - T_L - T_f
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Substitute into Newton's Law

Combining steps 1 and 3:

$$
T_e - T_L - T_f = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Express Friction as Viscous Damping

For most practical drives, friction is approximated as a **linear viscous term** with damping coefficient $B$ (units: N·m·s/rad):

$$
T_f = B \omega
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Final Form of the Dynamic Equation

$$
\boxed{\; T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega \;}
$$

**Special cases:**
- If $B \approx 0$ (high-inertia, low-friction systems, e.g., EV drivetrain): $\; T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$
- At **steady state** ($\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$): $\; T_e = T_L + B\omega \approx T_L$ (when $B\omega$ is small)

### 3.1.7 Multi-Step Matrix — Physical Interpretation of Each Term

| Term | Physical Meaning | Units | Effect on Motion |
|------|------------------|-------|------------------|
| $T_e$ | Motor electromagnetic torque | N·m | Accelerates the shaft |
| $T_L$ | Load torque requirement | N·m | Decelerates the shaft |
| $J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Inertial torque (rate of change of kinetic energy) | N·m | Determines acceleration |
| $B \omega$ | Frictional (viscous) torque | N·m | Always opposes motion |

## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Stability Criterion

### 3.2.1 Statement of the Problem

At any operating point, the system is in **dynamic equilibrium** only if small perturbations in speed decay over time (i.e., the system returns to the original operating point). Mathematically, this requires the **slope of the motor torque characteristic** to exceed the **slope of the load torque characteristic** at the operating point.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the operating point be $(\omega_0, T_0)$ where $T_e(\omega_0) = T_L(\omega_0)$.

Consider a small perturbation $\Delta \omega$ about $\omega_0$. The perturbed speed is $\omega = \omega_0 + \Delta \omega$.

Using **first-order Taylor series expansion** about $\omega_0$:

$$
T_e(\omega) \approx T_e(\omega_0) + \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} \Delta \omega
$$

$$
T_L(\omega) \approx T_L(\omega_0) + \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} \Delta \omega
$$

The perturbed accelerating torque is:

$$
T_{acc} = T_e(\omega) - T_L(\omega) - B\omega
$$

Substituting the linearized expressions (and using $T_e(\omega_0) = T_L(\omega_0)$):

$$
T_{acc} \approx \left( \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} - \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} - B \right) \Delta \omega
$$

But by the dynamic equation, $T_{acc} = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = J \dfrac{d(\Delta\omega)}{dt}$. So:

$$
J \dfrac{d(\Delta\omega)}{dt} = \left( \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} - \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} - B \right) \Delta \omega
$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** in $\Delta \omega$ with the general solution:

$$
\Delta \omega(t) = \Delta\omega(0) \, e^{\lambda t}, \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \dfrac{1}{J} \left[ \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} - \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} - B \right]
$$

### 3.2.3 Condition for Stability

For the perturbation to **decay** (system returns to operating point), the **time constant must be negative**:

$$
\lambda < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} - \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_{\omega_0} - B < 0
$$

In most cases $B$ is small, and this reduces to the **classic steady-state stability criterion**:

$$
\boxed{\; \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_{op} > \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_{op} \quad \text{(stable)} \;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_{op} < \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_{op} \quad \text{(unstable)} \;}
$$

### 3.2.4 Graphical Interpretation — Slope Test

At the intersection of $T_e(\omega)$ and $T_L(\omega)$:
- If $T_e$ curve is **steeper** (rising) than $T_L$ → **STABLE**
- If $T_e$ curve is **flatter** (or falling) than $T_L$ → **UNSTABLE**

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU exam tip:** DC shunt motor has a **falling** $T_e$ vs. $\omega$ characteristic (with armature reaction neglected, $T_e \propto 1/\omega$). For a **constant-torque load** ($\partial T_L/\partial \omega = 0$), the system is **unstable in the open loop** — that's why **closed-loop speed control** is mandatory for DC shunt drives.

## 3.3 Derivation of Acceleration / Deceleration Time

Starting from $T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$, we can integrate to obtain the time taken for a speed change from $\omega_1$ to $\omega_2$:

$$
dt = \dfrac{J \, d\omega}{T_e(\omega) - T_L(\omega)}
$$

Integrating both sides:

$$
t_{acc} = \int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} \dfrac{J \, d\omega}{T_e(\omega) - T_L(\omega)}
$$

For the special case where $T_e$ and $T_L$ are approximately constant (large inertia, small variation):

$$
t_{acc} \approx \dfrac{J (\omega_2 - \omega_1)}{T_e - T_L}
$$

Similarly, **deceleration time** (during braking, $T_e$ is replaced by $-T_b$ for a typical braking torque):

$$
t_{dec} \approx \dfrac{J (\omega_1 - \omega_2)}{T_b + T_L}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Key Derivations

| # | Derived Result | Governing Equation | Application |
|---|----------------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Dynamic equation | $T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$ | Motion analysis |
| 2 | Steady-state | $T_e = T_L$ (when $d\omega/dt = 0$) | Operating-point finding |
| 3 | Stability condition | $\partial T_e/\partial \omega \vert_{op} > \partial T_L/\partial \omega \vert_{op}$ | Closed-loop design |
| 4 | Acceleration time | $t_{acc} = \int \dfrac{J \, d\omega}{T_e - T_L}$ | Starting-time calculation |
| 5 | Energy in inertia | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} J \omega^2$ | Braking resistor sizing |
| 6 | Reflected inertia | $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L (N_L/N_m)^2$ | Multi-shaft drives |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generalized Electric Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    POW[POWER SOURCE\nAC Mains or DC Battery] -->|Electrical Energy| CONV[POWER MODULATOR\nRectifier Chopper Inverter Cycloconverter]
    CONV -->|Variable Magnitude or Frequency| MOT[ELECTRIC MOTOR\nDC IM PMSM SRM BLDC]
    MOT -->|Mechanical Shaft| LOAD[MECHANICAL LOAD\nPump Fan Conveyor Hoist]
    LOAD -->|Torque Demand TL| MOT
    SENSE[SENSING UNIT\nSpeed Current Position Voltage] -->|Feedback Signals FB| CTRL[CONTROL UNIT\nDSP FPGA Microcontroller]
    CTRL -->|Firing Pulses PWM Gating Signals| CONV
    MOT -.->|Speed Torque Signals| SENSE
    LOAD -.->|Mechanical Feedback| SENSE
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Flow and Control Loops

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[SPEED REFERENCE Nref] --> SUM[COMPARATOR Summing Junction]
    SUM -->|Error Signal e| PI[PI CONTROLLER]
    PI -->|Control Signal uc| PWM[PWM GENERATOR]
    PWM -->|Gate Pulses| INV[VOLTAGE SOURCE INVERTER]
    INV -->|Variable Frequency AC| IM[INDUCTION MOTOR]
    IM -->|Mechanical Output| MLOAD[Mechanical Load]
    IM -.->|Actual Speed Nact| TACH[Speed Sensor Tachogenerator Encoder]
    TACH --> SUM
    IM -.->|Stator Current| CS[Current Sensor LEM CT]
    CS --> PI
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Classification Tree of Load Torque

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LT[LOAD TORQUE TL] --> PAS[Passive Load Torque]
    LT --> ACT[Active Load Torque]
    PAS --> P1[Constant Torque Type n equals 0]
    PAS --> P2[Linear Torque Type n equals 1]
    PAS --> P3[Parabolic Torque Type n equals 2]
    PAS --> P4[Inverse Torque Type n equals minus 1]
    ACT --> A1[Weight of Hoist Load]
    ACT --> A2[Tension in Winding Reel]
    ACT --> A3[Vehicle on Down Gradient]
    P1 --> EX1[Conveyor Extruder Compressor]
    P2 --> EX2[Calender Viscous Friction Load]
    P3 --> EX3[Centrifugal Pump Fan Blower]
    P4 --> EX4[Machine Tool Spindle Traction Motor]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Motor Torque-Load Combination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MOT[Te versus omega] --> JEQ[Combined Inertia J eq]
    LOAD[TL versus omega] --> JEQ
    JEQ --> DYN[Dynamic Equation Te minus TL equals J d omega by dt plus B omega]
    DYN --> ACC[Acceleration Phase d omega by dt greater than zero]
    DYN --> DEC[Deceleration Phase d omega by dt less than zero]
    DYN --> SS[Steady State d omega by dt equals zero Te equals TL]
    SS --> STAB[Stability Check dTe by d omega greater than dTL by d omega]
    STAB --> STABLE[Stable Operating Point]
    STAB --> UNSTABLE[Unstable Needs Closed Loop Control]
```

## 4.5 Graphical Description — Torque-Speed Characteristics Curves (KTU Board Sketch Template)

Since the four characteristic curves cannot be drawn in Mermaid syntax, the following description is provided for the student to reproduce on the answer script:

- **X-axis**: Speed $\omega$ (rad/s) — labels at 0, $\omega_1$, $\omega_2$, $\omega_3$.
- **Y-axis**: Torque $T$ (N·m) — labels at 0, $T_1$, $T_2$.
- **Curve A** (Constant Torque Load): Horizontal line parallel to X-axis at $T = T_L$.
- **Curve B** (Linear Load): Straight line through origin, slope = $k_1$.
- **Curve C** (Parabolic Load): Upward-opening parabola through origin, $T = k_2 \omega^2$.
- **Curve D** (Constant Power): Rectangular hyperbola, $T = P_0 / \omega$.
- **Motor Curve $T_e(\omega)$** (DC Shunt): Falling rectangular hyperbola, $T_e \propto 1/\omega$.
- **Motor Curve $T_e(\omega)$** (Induction): Approximately linear in operating region, slight droop.

**Intersection points** of motor and load curves are the natural operating points. Use the **slope test** at each intersection to determine stability.

## 4.6 Mermaid Quadrant Chart — Four-Quadrant Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[FOUR QUADRANT OPERATION] --> Q1[Quadrant 1 Forward Motoring omega greater than zero Te greater than zero]
    Q --> Q2[Quadrant 2 Forward Braking omega greater than zero Te less than zero]
    Q --> Q3[Quadrant 3 Reverse Motoring omega less than zero Te less than zero]
    Q --> Q4[Quadrant 4 Reverse Braking omega less than zero Te greater than zero]
    Q1 --> E1[Hoist Raising Load]
    Q2 --> E2[Hoist Lowering Load with Active Torque]
    Q3 --> E3[Reverse Conveyor]
    Q4 --> E4[Plugging During Forward to Reverse Transition]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B, Q1(a)]**
*List any six advantages of Adjustable Speed Electric Drives over conventional fixed-speed drives.*

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Energy conservation** — Power follows cube law in fans/pumps; 20 % speed reduction saves ~49 % power.
2. **Soft starting** — Inrush current limited to 1.5–2× rated (vs. 6–8× in DOL).
3. **Improved process control** — Tight regulation of speed/torque improves product quality.
4. **Reversal and regenerative braking** — Easy four-quadrant operation.
5. **Reduced mechanical stress** — No shock loading on belts, gears, couplings.
6. **Better power factor and lower harmonics** — Modern IGBT-PWM drives achieve near-unity PF.
7. *(Bonus — if listed)* Lower maintenance, compact installation, telemetry support.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often confuse "Adjustable Speed Drive" with "VFD" specifically. Use the broader term **ASD** in the answer, even if the question uses VFD, to demonstrate syllabus-level understanding.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A, Q1(b)]**
*Distinguish between active and passive load torques. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (in tabular form for full 3 marks):**

| Property | Passive Load Torque | Active Load Torque |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Speed dependence | Function of $\omega$ | Independent of $\omega$ |
| Sign with reversal of $\omega$ | Reverses | Does **not** reverse |
| Energy role | Always absorbs power | Can **release** power (regenerative) |
| Example | Centrifugal fan, conveyor | Hoist (loaded), vehicle on down-gradient |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "active load torque is always positive" — this is **wrong**. Active torque is *fixed in sign* by the geometry, but may be positive or negative depending on whether it assists or opposes motor torque. E.g., a load on a down-gradient has a *negative* active torque (assists motion).

---

## 5.2 PART-B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice is provided between Question A and Question B.*

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A, Q4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Draw the block diagram of an electric drive system and explain the function of each block.*

**Model Answer:**

The generalized electric drive system consists of the following **five functional blocks** (as shown in the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

**1. Power Source** (1 mark)
- Provides raw electrical energy.
- May be AC mains (single-phase/three-phase), DC battery, or solar PV.
- Example: 3-phase 415 V, 50 Hz AC supply for industrial drives.

**2. Power Modulator / Power Electronic Converter** (2 marks)
- The *central element* of the drive; conditions the supply to suit the motor.
- Performs AC→DC (rectifier), DC→DC (chopper), DC→AC (inverter), or AC→AC (cycloconverter) conversion.
- Uses power semiconductor switches: SCR, IGBT, IGCT, MOSFET.
- Example: Three-phase VSI for induction motor drive; full-bridge chopper for DC motor.

**3. Electric Motor** (1.5 marks)
- Prime mover; converts electrical energy to mechanical form.
- Types: DC shunt/series, induction (squirrel-cage/wound rotor), synchronous, PMSM, BLDC, SRM.
- Selection criteria: Power rating, speed range, torque characteristic, duty cycle.

**4. Control Unit** (1.5 marks)
- Generates gating pulses for the power modulator.
- Implements control strategies: V/f, FOC, DTC, sensorless control.
- Hardware: DSP, FPGA, microcontroller.
- Inputs: Speed reference, feedback from sensors.

**5. Sensing Unit** (0.5 marks)
- Provides feedback signals: speed, current, position, voltage.
- Sensors: Encoder, Hall-effect CT, resolver, tacho-generator.

**6. Mechanical Load** (0.5 marks)
- The end-application: pump, fan, conveyor, hoist, robotic arm.
- Driven via mechanical coupling: direct, belt, gearbox.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Derive the fundamental dynamic equation of a motor-load system: $T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$. Explain the physical significance of each term.*

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Applying Newton's second law for rotational motion:

$$
J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \sum \text{torques on shaft}
$$

The torques on the shaft are: motor electromagnetic torque $T_e$ (driving), load torque $T_L$ (opposing), and frictional torque $T_f = B\omega$ (opposing).

$$
J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = T_e - T_L - T_f
$$

Substituting $T_f = B\omega$:

$$
T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega
$$

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**

| Term | Meaning | Effect |
|------|---------|--------|
| $T_e$ | Torque developed by motor from electrical input | Accelerates shaft |
| $T_L$ | Resistive + active torque required by load | Decelerates shaft |
| $J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Inertial torque (= rate of change of rotational KE) | Determines acceleration |
| $B\omega$ | Viscous friction torque (bearing, windage) | Always opposes motion |

**Steady state condition:** $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow T_e = T_L + B\omega$

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B, Q4]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Classify the different types of loads based on their speed-torque characteristics. Sketch typical curves and give two industrial examples for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Equation | Curve | Examples |
|------|----------|-------|----------|
| **1. Constant Torque** | $T_L = \text{const}$ | Horizontal line | Conveyor, hoist, extruder, crane |
| **2. Linear Torque** | $T_L = k_1 \omega$ | Straight line through origin | Calender, viscous-friction system |
| **3. Parabolic (Quadratic)** | $T_L = k_2 \omega^2$ | Upward parabola | Centrifugal pump, fan, blower |
| **4. Constant Power** | $T_L = P_0 / \omega$ | Rectangular hyperbola | Machine tool spindle, traction motor (high speed), winder |
| **5. Impact / Pulse** | Variable, with peaks | Intermittent profile | Punch press, rolling mill, hammer mill |

**Combined (general) load:**

$$
T_L = T_0 + k_1 \omega + k_2 \omega^2
$$

where $T_0$ = Coulomb friction, $k_1 \omega$ = viscous friction, $k_2 \omega^2$ = aerodynamic/fluid friction.

**Energy-saving insight for parabolic loads:**

$$
\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
$$

A 30 % speed reduction → $0.7^3 = 0.343$ → **~66 % power saving** (with flow reduced to 70 %).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*What is steady-state stability of a motor-load system? Derive the condition for steady-state stability and explain it graphically.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Steady-state stability is the ability of a motor-load system to return to its original operating point after a small perturbation in speed, without any external corrective action. Mathematically, the perturbation must decay exponentially with time.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Linearizing $T_e(\omega)$ and $T_L(\omega)$ about operating point $\omega_0$ using Taylor's series:

$$
\Delta T_e \approx \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_0 \Delta\omega, \quad \Delta T_L \approx \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_0 \Delta\omega
$$

Substituting into the dynamic equation (perturbation form):

$$
J \dfrac{d(\Delta\omega)}{dt} = \left(\left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_0 - \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_0 - B\right) \Delta\omega
$$

Solution: $\Delta\omega(t) = \Delta\omega(0) \, e^{\lambda t}$, with

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{J}\left[ \left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_0 - \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_0 - B \right]
$$

For decay ($\lambda < 0$) and with $B$ small:

$$
\boxed{\left.\dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\right|_0 > \left.\dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\right|_0 \quad \text{(stable)}}
$$

**Graphical Explanation (1.5 marks):**

At the intersection of motor torque curve $T_e(\omega)$ and load torque curve $T_L(\omega)$:
- If the **motor curve is steeper** (rising) than the load curve → **STABLE** (perturbation decays).
- If the **motor curve is flatter or falling** than the load curve → **UNSTABLE** (perturbation grows).
- A **DC shunt motor** with a constant-torque load is **inherently unstable in open loop** — hence closed-loop speed control (armature voltage / field current) is essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (14 marks):**
> 1. **Missing the $B\omega$ friction term** in the dynamic equation → lose **1 mark**.
> 2. **Not converting rpm to rad/s** when using $\omega$ in SI units → lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing "stability" with "dynamic response"** — stability is about *recovery from perturbation*; dynamic response is about *transient behaviour* (rise time, overshoot).
> 4. **Not labelling axes** in torque-speed sketches → lose **0.5–1 mark** in Part (a) of Q.A.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the formula sheet (e.g., $J$ in kg·m², $\omega$ in rad/s) → lose **0.5 mark** cumulatively.
> 6. **Forgetting the affinity law** in energy-saving explanation for fans/pumps → lose **1 mark** if question involves variable-torque loads.
> 7. **Not mentioning "active load torque"** while discussing 4-quadrant operation → lose **1 mark** in stability/quadrant questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU 2024 Points (rapid revision checklist):**

- [x] **Electric drive** = motor + power converter + control + load (mandatory four blocks).
- [x] **Adjustable Speed Drive (ASD)** = drives with smooth, wide-range speed control (1:20 or higher).
- [x] **Key ASD advantage**: Energy saving via cube law in fans/pumps; soft start; precise control; regenerative braking.
- [x] **Block diagram order**: Source → Power Modulator → Motor → Load → Sensors → Controller (closed loop).
- [x] **Five types of loads**: Constant torque, linear, parabolic (quadratic), constant power, impact/pulse.
- [x] **Parabolic load** ($T_L \propto \omega^2$) → **most common in industry** (fans, pumps) → maximum ASD benefit.
- [x] **Combined load**: $T_L = T_0 + k_1 \omega + k_2 \omega^2$.
- [x] **Load torque classification**: **Active** (sign independent of $\omega$, e.g., hoist weight) vs. **Passive** (sign reverses with $\omega$, e.g., fan).
- [x] **Active load torque** requires **four-quadrant drive** (regenerative braking possible).
- [x] **Dynamic equation** (most important equation of Module IV): $\; T_e - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B\omega$
- [x] **Steady state condition**: $\; \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow T_e = T_L + B\omega \approx T_L$
- [x] **Steady-state stability criterion**: $\; \dfrac{\partial T_e}{\partial \omega}\big\vert_{op} > \dfrac{\partial T_L}{\partial \omega}\big\vert_{op}$
- [x] **Stability slope test**: Motor curve steeper than load curve at intersection = **STABLE**.
- [x] **DC shunt motor + constant-torque load = unstable in open loop** → needs closed-loop control.
- [x] **Induction motor + parabolic load = stable in open loop** (natural stability).
- [x] **Acceleration time formula**: $t_{acc} = \int \dfrac{J \, d\omega}{T_e - T_L}$
- [x] **Reflected inertia** (for geared systems): $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L (N_L/N_m)^2$
- [x] **Affinity law** (fan/pump): $P \propto N^3$, $H \propto N^2$, $Q \propto N$.
- [x] **RMS torque** for intermittent duty: $T_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2 t_i / \sum t_i}$
- [x] **Unit conversions**: $1 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ rad/s ≈ 0.1047 rad/s.
- [x] **Four-quadrant operation**: Q1 (forward motoring), Q2 (forward braking), Q3 (reverse motoring), Q4 (reverse braking).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory mnemonic for stability:**
> **"Steeper Motor = Stable; Flatter Motor = Unstable"** — always check the slope of $T_e(\omega)$ at the operating point.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module-IV: DC Drives — Chopper, Controlled Rectifier & Dual Converter Fed SEDC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a DC Drive?

A **DC Drive** is an electronic power converter system used to control the speed, torque, and direction of a DC motor by varying the voltage and/or current supplied to its armature or field winding. The **Separately Excited DC Motor (SEDC)** has its field winding energized from an independent DC source, allowing independent (decoupled) control of flux and armature current.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Rigid)**
> A DC drive is a closed-loop or open-loop power electronic converter-fed system that regulates the speed/torque of a DC motor by controlling the average armature voltage using devices such as **DC-DC choppers**, **controlled rectifiers**, or **dual converters**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle. Your leg power is the **armature voltage**, the road slope is the **load torque**, and your body posture (leaning forward = field weakening) decides how hard the pedals must push per revolution.

- **Chopper** = a fast on-off switch that delivers "pulses" of power (like intermittent pedaling) — average power controls speed.
- **Controlled Rectifier** = a controlled valve from AC mains that gives smooth, adjustable DC voltage (like a continuously variable throttle).
- **Dual Converter** = two such valves connected back-to-back, allowing the motor to push energy **back into the supply** (regenerative braking).

### 1.3 Four-Quadrant Operation — The Heart of KTU Module-IV

Every DC drive operates in one of four quadrants on the **Speed (ω) vs Torque (T) plane**:

| Quadrant | Speed (ω) | Torque (T) | Mode | Application |
|----------|-----------|------------|------|-------------|
| **I** | + (Forward) | + (Motoring) | Forward Motoring | Vehicle acceleration |
| **II** | + (Forward) | − (Braking) | Forward Braking/Regen | Decelerating a downhill car |
| **III** | − (Reverse) | − (Motoring) | Reverse Motoring | Car reversing |
| **IV** | − (Reverse) | + (Braking) | Reverse Braking/Regen | Stop while moving backward |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module-IV, students are restricted to **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** only. Discontinuous conduction (DCM) is omitted.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Equations of a Separately Excited DC Motor

The SEDC motor is governed by the following three pillars:

### (a) Armature Circuit Equation (KVL around armature loop)

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$

### (b) Back EMF Equation

$$E_b = K \Phi \omega$$

### (c) Developed Electromagnetic Torque

$$T = K \Phi I_a$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants:** $K$ is the machine constant (V·s/rad or Nm/A), $\Phi$ is flux per pole in **Weber (Wb)**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**, and $T$ in **Nm**.

---

## 2.2 Steady-State Speed Equation (CCM)

In steady-state, $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$. Hence:

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$$

$$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi} \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi}$$

Where **N** is speed in **RPM**.

---

## 2.3 Chopper Control of SEDC Motor (DC-DC Converter)

A **chopper** is a DC-to-DC switch-mode converter that varies the average output voltage via **Time Ratio Control (TRC)** or **Current Limit Control (CLC)**.

### Duty Cycle (α)

$$\alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

### Step-Down (Buck) Chopper Output Voltage

$$V_a = \alpha V_s \quad \text{(average)}$$

Where $V_s$ is the fixed DC input supply voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range:** $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ means $V_a$ varies from **0 V** to **$V_s$**.

---

## 2.4 Quadrant Chopper Configurations (CCM Analysis)

### 2.4.1 One-Quadrant Chopper (Class A)
- Chopper **T** in series with motor.
- **Quadrant I only** (forward motoring).
- Average voltage: $V_a = \alpha V_s$
- Average current: $I_a > 0$

### 2.4.2 Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class B — Forward Motoring + Forward Braking)
- Two switches T1 and T2 (or T1 + freewheel diode).
- Operates in **Quadrants I and II**.
- Motoring: T1 chopped, T2 OFF.
- Regenerative braking: T1 OFF, T2 chopped → current reverses.

### 2.4.3 Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class C — Forward Motoring + Reverse Motoring)
- Operates in **Quadrants I and III** (no braking).
- T1 + D2 → forward motoring
- T2 + D1 → reverse motoring

### 2.4.4 Four-Quadrant Chopper (Class D — Full H-Bridge)
- Four switches T1, T2, T3, T4 + four diodes D1–D4.
- All four quadrants accessible.
- T1T4 ON → forward motoring (Q-I)
- T2T3 ON → reverse motoring (Q-III)
- T1T3 ON → forward regen braking (Q-II)
- T2T4 ON → reverse regen braking (Q-IV)

---

## 2.5 Controlled Rectifier Fed SEDC Motor (AC-to-DC)

### 2.5.1 Single-Phase Fully-Controlled Rectifier (Continuous Conduction)

$$V_a = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

$$I_a = \frac{V_a - E_b}{R_a}$$

$$P = V_a I_a \quad ; \quad \text{Power Factor} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

### 2.5.2 Single-Phase Half-Controlled (Semi-converter)

$$V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)$$

### 2.5.3 Three-Phase Fully-Controlled Rectifier (Continuous Conduction)

$$V_a = \frac{3 \sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{3 V_{L-L,rms} \sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

### 2.5.4 Three-Phase Half-Controlled (Semi-converter)

$$V_a = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_{L-L,rms} \sqrt{2}}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$$

---

## 2.6 Dual Converter Fed SEDC Motor (Single Phase)

A **dual converter** consists of two fully-controlled rectifiers (Converter-1 P and Converter-2 N) connected in **anti-parallel** across the DC motor.

### Two Modes:
1. **Circulating Current Mode (CCM)** — both converters conduct simultaneously; a reactor $L_r$ limits circulating current.
2. **Non-Circulating Current Mode (NCM)** — only one converter conducts at a time; current must pass through zero before firing the other.

$$V_a = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha_1 = -\frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha_2$$

Where $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = \pi$ ensures smooth handover.

---

## 2.7 🔥 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Quadrant/Use** |
|-------|------------|-------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Back EMF | $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ | K (Vs/rad), Φ (Wb), ω (rad/s) | All |
| 2 | Torque | $T = K \Phi I_a$ | T (Nm), Ia (A) | All |
| 3 | Speed | $\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ | ω (rad/s) | All |
| 4 | Armature KVL | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | All V (Volts) | All |
| 5 | Step-Down Chopper | $V_a = \alpha V_s$ | α = duty cycle | Q-I, Q-II |
| 6 | Step-Up Chopper | $V_a = \frac{V_s}{1-\alpha}$ | α ∈ (0,1) | Regen braking |
| 7 | 1-Φ Full Converter | $V_a = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | α = firing angle | Q-I motoring |
| 8 | 1-Φ Semi-Converter | $V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | — | Q-I motoring |
| 9 | 3-Φ Full Converter | $V_a = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | Vm = peak phase voltage | Q-I motoring |
| 10 | 3-Φ Semi-Converter | $V_a = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | — | Q-I motoring |
| 11 | Dual Converter (CCM) | $V_a = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha_1$ | α1 + α2 = π | All 4 quadrants |
| 12 | Circulating Current | $I_c = \frac{V_m}{\pi X_L}(\cos\alpha_1 + \cos\alpha_2)$ | X_L = reactance of Lr | Dual Conv. |
| 13 | Regen Braking Power | $P_{regen} = E_b I_a$ | — | Q-II, Q-IV |
| 14 | Power Factor (1Φ full) | $\cos\phi = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | — | Rectifier drives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations power nearly every industrial DC drive — from **steel rolling mills** (4-Q operation) to **electric locomotives** (regen braking) to **CNC machine tools** (precise speed control).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Speed-Torque Characteristics of SEDC Motor under Chopper Control

### Given
- DC supply voltage $V_s$
- Chopper duty cycle $\alpha$
- Motor parameters: $R_a$, $L_a$, $K$, $\Phi$

### Goal
Derive $\omega$ as a function of $T$ in steady-state (CCM).

### Step 1 — Average Armature Voltage from Chopper
A Class-A (step-down) chopper supplies average voltage equal to the product of duty cycle and supply:

$$\boxed{V_a = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} v_a(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{\alpha T} V_s\, dt + \int_{\alpha T}^{T} 0\, dt\right]}$$

$$V_a = \frac{V_s \cdot \alpha T}{T} = \alpha V_s$$

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Armature KVL
In CCM, current ripple is ignored (averaged analysis), so $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} \approx 0$:

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back EMF and Torque Relations
From $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ and $T = K \Phi I_a$:

$$\alpha V_s = K \Phi \omega + \left(\frac{T}{K \Phi}\right) R_a$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Speed $\omega$

$$K \Phi \omega = \alpha V_s - \frac{T R_a}{K \Phi}$$

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{\alpha V_s}{K \Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K \Phi)^2} T}$$

### Step 5 — Identify No-Load and Ideal No-Load Speeds
At $T = 0$:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{\alpha V_s}{K \Phi} \quad \text{(No-load speed)}$$

At $\omega = 0$ (stall):

$$T_{stall} = \frac{\alpha V_s (K \Phi)}{R_a} \quad \text{(Stall torque)}$$

### Step 6 — Slope of Speed-Torque Curve

$$\frac{d\omega}{dT} = -\frac{R_a}{(K \Phi)^2}$$

> The characteristic is a **straight line** with negative slope, intercepting the ω-axis at $\omega_0$ and the T-axis at $T_{stall}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Average Output Voltage of Single-Phase Full Converter (Continuous Conduction)

### Step 1 — Output Voltage Expression in Time Domain
For a single-phase full-bridge fully-controlled rectifier with firing angle $\alpha$ (measured from the zero crossing of the supply), the output voltage waveform in CCM is:

$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi+\alpha \\ -V_m \sin(\omega t), & \pi+\alpha \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi+\alpha \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Average

$$V_o = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha}$$

$$V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\pi+\alpha) + \cos\alpha\right]$$

### Step 3 — Use Trigonometric Identity
Since $\cos(\pi+\alpha) = -\cos\alpha$:

$$V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha\right] = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha}$$

### Step 4 — RMS Value (for power calculations)

$$V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{2\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$$

Where $V_s = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ is the RMS supply voltage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Three-Phase Full Converter Average Voltage (CCM)

### Step 1 — Phase Voltage Reference
Let $v_{an} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. The line-to-line voltage feeding the converter is:

$$v_{ab} = \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°)$$

### Step 2 — Average of Three Pulses per Cycle
Each thyristor pair conducts for $120°$. Over one full cycle ($2\pi$), there are three such pulses:

$$V_o = \frac{3}{\pi}\int_{\pi/6+\alpha}^{\pi/2+\alpha} \sqrt{3}\, V_m \sin\theta\, d\theta$$

### Step 3 — Integrate

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos\theta\right]_{\pi/6+\alpha}^{\pi/2+\alpha}$$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\left[\cos(\pi/6+\alpha) - \cos(\pi/2+\alpha)\right]$$

### Step 4 — Apply Identities
$\cos(\pi/2+\alpha) = -\sin\alpha$ and $\cos(\pi/6+\alpha) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha$:

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha + \sin\alpha\right]$$

$$V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha$$

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\, V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\, V_{L-L,rms}}{\pi}\cos\alpha}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Speed-Torque Curve Slope under Rectifier Supply

Starting from:

$$\omega = \frac{V_o - I_a R_a}{K \Phi} = \frac{V_o}{K\Phi} - \frac{R_a}{(K\Phi)^2}T$$

Differentiating w.r.t. torque:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dT} = -\frac{R_a}{(K\Phi)^2} \quad \text{(constant slope — independent of firing angle)}$$

> **KTU Implication:** All speed-torque lines for varying $\alpha$ are **parallel straight lines** with the same negative slope. Changing $\alpha$ shifts the line vertically (i.e., changes no-load speed).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quadrant Chopper Switching Truth Table

| **State** | **T1** | **T2** | **T3** | **T4** | **D1** | **D2** | **D3** | **D4** | **Va** | **Ia** | **Quadrant** |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|-------|-------|-------------|
| Forward Motoring | CHOP | OFF | OFF | ON | OFF | × | × | × | + | + | I |
| Forward Regen | OFF | ON | CHOP | OFF | × | ON | × | × | + | − | II |
| Reverse Motoring | OFF | ON | ON | CHOP | × | × | × | ON | − | − | III |
| Reverse Regen | ON | OFF | OFF | CHOP | × | × | ON | × | − | + | IV |
| Freewheel (Fwd) | OFF | × | OFF | × | × | ON | × | × | 0 | + | — |
| Freewheel (Rev) | × | OFF | × | OFF | ON | × | × | × | 0 | − | — |

Where **CHOP** = switched at high frequency, **ON** = continuously conducting, **OFF** = blocking, **×** = state determined by current direction.

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Circulating Current in Dual Converter

### Step 1 — Counter-EMF Between Two Converters
For CCM operation with $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = \pi$, the instantaneous voltages differ:

$$v_{P}(t) - v_{N}(t) = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha_1 - \frac{2\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha_2 = \frac{4\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha_1$$

Wait — let me redo. Since $\alpha_2 = \pi - \alpha_1$, then $\cos\alpha_2 = -\cos\alpha_1$. The circulating current flows through the inter-group reactor $L_r$ under the driving voltage:

$$\Delta v = v_{P} - v_{N} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}(\cos\alpha_1 - \cos\alpha_2) = \frac{4\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha_1$$

### Step 2 — Fundamental RMS Circulating Current

$$\boxed{I_c = \frac{\Delta v_{rms}}{\omega L_r} = \frac{4 V_s \cos\alpha_1}{\pi \omega L_r}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reactor $L_r$ must limit $I_c$ to **5–10 %** of rated load current. This is why CCM dual converters are bulky and rare in practice — NCM is more common.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Single-Quadrant Chopper Fed SEDC Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Source Vs] --> B[Chopper Switch T]
    B --> C[Freewheel Diode D]
    B --> D[SEDC Motor Armature]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Load Coupling]
    F[Separate DC Field Supply] --> G[Field Winding]
    D -.-> H[Speed Sensor Tachogenerator]
    H --> I[Controller Alpha Generator]
    I --> B
    G --> D
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Four-Quadrant Chopper (Class D H-Bridge) SEDC Drive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Bus Vs+] --> B1[T1]
    A --> B2[T3]
    B1 --> M1[Motor Terminal A]
    B2 --> M2[Motor Terminal B]
    B1 -.-> D2[D2]
    B2 -.-> D4[D4]
    M1 --> D1[D1]
    M1 --> D3[D3]
    D1 --> C[DC Bus Vs-]
    D3 --> C
    M1 --> E[SEDC Motor]
    E --> M2
    E --> F[Field Winding]
    G[Field Supply] --> F
    M2 --> T2[T2]
    M1 --> T4[T4]
    T2 --> C
    T4 --> C
    H[Controller Four Quadrant Logic] --> B1
    H --> B2
    H --> T2
    H --> T4
```

## 4.3 Power Flow in Four-Quadrant Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Quadrant I Forward Motoring"
        Q1A[Supply] -- Power --> Q1B[Motor]
        Q1B -- Mechanical Out --> Q1C[Load]
    end
    subgraph "Quadrant II Forward Regen Braking"
        Q2A[Load] -- Mechanical In --> Q2B[Motor]
        Q2B -- Power Back --> Q2C[Supply]
    end
    subgraph "Quadrant III Reverse Motoring"
        Q3A[Supply] -- Power --> Q3B[Motor]
        Q3B -- Mechanical Out --> Q3C[Load]
    end
    subgraph "Quadrant IV Reverse Regen Braking"
        Q4A[Load] -- Mechanical In --> Q4B[Motor]
        Q4B -- Power Back --> Q4C[Supply]
    end
```

## 4.4 Dual Converter Architecture (Circulating Current Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[AC Mains Single Phase] --> T1[Converter 1 Positive Firing Angle Alpha1]
    T1 --> R1[Reactor Lr]
    R1 --> M[Motor Terminals A and B]
    M --> R2[Reactor Lr]
    R2 --> T2[Converter 2 Negative Firing Angle Alpha2]
    T2 --> A1
    C1[Controller Alpha1 Alpha2 Generator] --> T1
    C1 --> T2
    M --> F[Field Winding]
    G[Field Supply] --> F
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Speed Control of SEDC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Speed Nref] --> S1[Summing Junction]
    S1 --> S2[Speed Controller PI]
    S2 --> S3[Current Limiter]
    S3 --> S4[Power Converter Chopper or Rectifier]
    S4 --> S5[SEDC Motor]
    S5 --> S6[Tachogenerator]
    S6 --> S1
    S5 -.-> S7[Current Sensor Ia]
    S7 -.-> S1
```

## 4.6 Mode Decision Logic for Static Four-Quadrant Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Read Speed Sign and Torque Sign] --> B{Both Positive}
    B -- Yes --> C[Quadrant I Forward Motoring T1 T4 ON]
    B -- No --> D{Speed Positive Torque Negative}
    D -- Yes --> E[Quadrant II Forward Regen T2 T3 ON]
    D -- No --> F{Both Negative}
    F -- Yes --> G[Quadrant III Reverse Motoring T2 T3 ON]
    F -- No --> H[Quadrant IV Reverse Regen T1 T4 ON with Current Reverse]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a chopper. List the four classes of chopper used in DC drives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A chopper is a static power electronic device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by varying the duty cycle of a high-speed semiconductor switch (IGBT/MOSFET/thyristor with commutation circuitry).
- **Four Classes (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  - **Class A** — One-quadrant (forward motoring only)
  - **Class B** — Two-quadrant (forward motoring + forward braking)
  - **Class C** — Two-quadrant (forward motoring + reverse motoring)
  - **Class D** — Four-quadrant (all directions and braking modes)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between circulating current and non-circulating current modes of a dual converter. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Circulating Current Mode | Non-Circulating Current Mode |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Conducting converters | Both P and N always ON | Only one at a time |
| Reactor $L_r$ | Required (limits $I_c$) | Not required |
| Response time | **Fast** (no current zero wait) | **Slow** (must wait for $I_a = 0$) |
| Size & cost | Bulky & costly | Compact & cheaper |
| Harmonics | Higher (continuous current) | Lower |

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Chopper Fed SEDC Drive Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Draw the power circuit of a Class-D four-quadrant chopper fed separately excited DC motor and explain its operation in all four quadrants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V, 1500 RPM, 50 A separately excited DC motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ and field constant $K\Phi = 1.2\ \text{V·s/rad}$. The motor is fed from a chopper with input DC voltage 240 V and duty cycle $\alpha = 0.6$. Calculate the motor speed and developed torque in CCM. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

The Class-D chopper is an **H-bridge** with four controlled switches T1, T2, T3, T4 and four freewheeling diodes D1, D2, D3, D4. Supply is across the diagonal terminals.

| **Quadrant** | **Mode** | **Conducting Switches** | **Direction** | **$V_a$** | **$I_a$** |
|--------------|----------|--------------------------|----------------|----------|----------|
| I | Forward Motoring | T1, T4 | + to A, − to B | +240 V | +ve |
| II | Forward Braking | D2, D3 (or T2, T3) | Current reverses | +ve | −ve |
| III | Reverse Motoring | T2, T3 | − to A, + to B | −240 V | −ve |
| IV | Reverse Braking | D1, D4 | — | −ve | +ve |

**Marking Scheme:**
- Circuit diagram: **2 marks**
- Quadrant I explanation: **2 marks**
- Quadrants II, III, IV (brief): **3 marks**

---

#### **Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $V_s = 240\ \text{V}$, $\alpha = 0.6$, $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $K\Phi = 1.2\ \text{V·s/rad}$

**Step 1 — Average armature voltage (1 mark)**
$$V_a = \alpha V_s = 0.6 \times 240 = 144\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — No-load speed (1 mark)**
$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K\Phi} = \frac{144}{1.2} = 120\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to RPM (1 mark)**
$$N_0 = \omega_0 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 120 \times 9.549 = 1145.9\ \text{RPM}$$

**Step 4 — Assume motor draws rated current at this load** (1 mark)
$$I_a = 50\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate back EMF (1 mark)**
$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 144 - 50 \times 0.5 = 144 - 25 = 119\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Actual speed (1 mark)**
$$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K\Phi} = \frac{119}{1.2} = 99.17\ \text{rad/s}$$
$$N = 99.17 \times 9.549 = 947\ \text{RPM}$$

**Step 7 — Developed torque (1 mark)**
$$\boxed{T = K\Phi \cdot I_a = 1.2 \times 50 = 60\ \text{Nm}}$$

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Controlled Rectifier Fed SEDC + Dual Converter

**[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a single-phase fully-controlled rectifier feeding a separately excited DC motor in continuous conduction mode. Derive the expression for average output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the circulating current mode of a single-phase dual converter fed SEDC drive. Why is a current-limiting reactor needed? Derive the expression for circulating current. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a) — 7 marks**

**Circuit (1 mark):** Single-phase AC source → 4 thyristors in full bridge → DC motor (armature) → Freewheeling diode across armature if half-controlled.

**Operation in CCM (3 marks):**
- For $\omega t \in [\alpha, \pi+\alpha]$: thyristors T1, T2 conduct; $V_a = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- For $\omega t \in [\pi+\alpha, 2\pi+\alpha]$: T3, T4 conduct; $V_a = -V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Since $L_a$ is large, current never falls to zero.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$V_a = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

$$V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}[\cos\alpha - \cos(\pi+\alpha)]$$

$$V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi}[\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha] = \boxed{\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha}$$

---

#### **Solution to (b) — 7 marks**

**Concept (2 marks):** Two full converters P (positive) and N (negative) connected in anti-parallel. Both conduct simultaneously; $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 180°$. Converter P drives positive current; Converter N handles negative current.

**Need for Reactor $L_r$ (2 marks):** Without $L_r$, the instantaneous voltage difference $\Delta v = v_P - v_N$ drives a huge short-circuit current through both converters, destroying them. The reactor limits this to a safe magnitude (5–10% of rated current).

**Circulating Current Derivation (3 marks):**

$$v_P - v_N = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha_1 - \frac{2\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha_2$$

With $\alpha_2 = \pi - \alpha_1$, so $\cos\alpha_2 = -\cos\alpha_1$:

$$\Delta v = \frac{4\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha_1$$

Fundamental RMS value:

$$\Delta v_{rms} = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\cos\alpha_1$$

$$\boxed{I_c = \frac{4 V_s \cos\alpha_1}{\pi \omega L_r}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks (read carefully before exam)**
>
> 1. **Units Confusion (–1 mark):** Mixing $\omega$ in rad/s with $N$ in RPM without the $60/(2\pi)$ factor.
> 2. **Forgetting CCM Assumption (–2 marks):** The single-phase full-converter equation $V_a = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ is valid **only in CCM**. In DCM, the equation is different.
> 3. **Dual Converter Sign Error (–2 marks):** In circulating current mode, $\alpha_2$ is **measured from $\pi$**, not zero. So $\alpha_2 = \pi - \alpha_1$.
> 4. **Quadrant Chopper Current Direction (–1 mark):** In Q-II (forward braking), $V_a$ is positive but $I_a$ is negative — many students wrongly make both negative.
> 5. **Confusing Regen Braking with Dynamic Braking (–1 mark):** Regen braking returns energy to the source; dynamic braking dissipates it in a resistor. Dual converters and Class-D choppers enable regen; Class-A choppers do NOT.
> 6. **Rectifier Firing Angle Range (–1 mark):** For full converters driving SEDC, $\alpha \in [0°, 90°]$ gives motoring; $\alpha \in [90°, 180°]$ gives inversion/regen — students often forget the inversion mode.

---

## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **SEDC Motor** has independent field supply → flux is decoupled from armature.
- [x] **Speed equation:** $\omega = (V_a - I_a R_a)/(K\Phi)$ — drop $I_a R_a$ for no-load.
- [x] **Torque equation:** $T = K\Phi I_a$ — linear relation.
- [x] **Chopper** = high-frequency DC switch → controls $V_a$ via duty cycle $\alpha$.
- [x] **Class A chopper** = Q-I only. **Class D** = all four quadrants via H-bridge.
- [x] **Regenerative braking** = motor acts as generator, energy flows back to source.
- [x] **Speed-torque curves** are parallel straight lines under chopper/rectifier control.
- [x] **1-Φ Full Converter:** $V_a = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ (CCM).
- [x] **1-Φ Semi-Converter:** $V_a = (V_m/\pi)(1+\cos\alpha)$.
- [x] **3-Φ Full Converter:** $V_a = (3\sqrt{3}V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$ (CCM).
- [x] **3-Φ Semi-Converter:** $V_a = (3V_m/2\pi)(1+\cos\alpha)$.
- [x] **Motoring:** $\alpha \in [0°, 90°]$. **Inversion/Regen:** $\alpha \in [90°, 180°]$.
- [x] **Dual Converter** = two anti-parallel converters → enables all 4 quadrants from AC source.
- [x] **Circulating Current Mode** needs $L_r$ to limit $I_c$; NCM does not.
- [x] **Static 4-Q Operation with SEDC** = implemented using Class-D chopper OR dual converter.
- [x] **No-load speed** scales linearly with $V_a$; **stall torque** scales linearly with $V_a$.
- [x] Power factor of 1-Φ full converter is $(2\sqrt{2}/\pi)\cos\alpha \approx 0.9\cos\alpha$.
- [x] Three-phase converters give **lower ripple** and **better PF** than single-phase.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three-phase VSI Fed Induction Motor Drives: Stator Voltage Control, V/F Speed Control, Speed-Torque Characteristics, Speed Control, Operation Below and Above Base Speed, Braking: Dynamic and Regenerative
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET403 - Power Electronics and Drives
> - **Module:** Module- IV
> - **Topic:** Three-phase VSI Fed Induction Motor Drives: Stator Voltage Control, V/F Speed Control, Speed-Torque Characteristics, Speed Control, Operation Below and Above Base Speed, Braking: Dynamic and Regenerative

# Three-Phase VSI Fed Induction Motor Drives

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) Fed Induction Motor Drive** is a variable frequency, variable voltage AC-AC power conversion system in which a DC link (either from a rectifier-fed DC bus or a battery) feeds a three-phase PWM/Stepped-wave Voltage Source Inverter. The VSI synthesizes a three-phase output whose **magnitude and frequency can be independently controlled**, which is then applied to the stator of a squirrel-cage or wound-rotor induction motor. This forms the workhorse of modern industrial variable-speed drives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme, the focus is on **open-loop V/f control** using a VSI. The VSI operates in **square-wave (six-step) mode** at higher frequencies and in **PWM (sine-triangle) mode** at lower frequencies to reduce harmonic losses. The classical **V/Hz = constant** law governs operation below base speed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Accelerator Pedal" 🚗

Think of a VSI-fed IM drive like a **modern car's smart accelerator pedal**:

- The **DC link** is the fuel tank — it stores the raw energy.
- The **VSI** is the *electronic throttle body* — it decides how much "go" (voltage) and how fast to push it (frequency).
- The **induction motor** is the engine that finally drives the wheels.

Just as a good driver keeps the engine's RPM in its **sweet spot** (peak torque region) by choosing the right gear, the VSI keeps the **air-gap flux constant** by maintaining the **V/f ratio** — ensuring the motor produces rated torque at any speed.

If you try to push too much fuel (voltage) at low RPM, the engine chokes. If you push too little fuel at high RPM, the engine starves. The V/f control is exactly the **electronic gearbox** that balances both.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (KTU Standard)

- **Base frequency ($f_b$):** Rated frequency of the motor (**50 Hz** in India, per IS standards).
- **Base speed ($N_b$):** Synchronous speed at rated frequency, $N_b = \dfrac{120 f_b}{P}$ where $P$ = number of poles.
- **Rated air-gap flux ($\phi_m$):** $\phi_m = \dfrac{V}{4.44 \, f \, N_1 K_{w1}}$ — the heart of V/f control.
- **Slip ($s$):** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ — the slip of the rotor relative to the rotating field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept — Constant Flux Region:** The air-gap flux $\phi_m$ is directly proportional to $V/f$. To keep the motor from saturating (too much flux) or under-exciting (too little flux), we hold $V/f$ **constant**. This is the foundation of all VSI-fed IM drives.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Architecture of a VSI-Fed IM Drive

The complete drive consists of four functional blocks:

1. **Rectifier (Uncontrolled/Semi-controlled):** Converts 3-phase 50 Hz mains to a **fixed DC link voltage** ($V_{dc}$).
2. **DC Link Filter ($C$ or $L$-$C$):** Smooths the rectified DC. The capacitor (for VSI) makes the DC bus a **stiff voltage source**.
3. **Three-phase VSI (PWM or Square-wave):** Six IGBT switches ($S_1$–$S_6$) in three legs, switching in a 60° sequence. The switching frequency $f_s$ sets the output frequency $f_o$.
4. **Induction Motor (Squirrel-cage rotor, stator-fed):** The mechanical load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Square-wave vs. PWM Mode:** In VSI-fed drives, the VSI operates in **PWM (PWM-HEX, SPWM)** below ~40 Hz to eliminate low-order harmonics. Above this, it switches to **six-step (square wave)** to maximize DC link utilization (fundamental peak $\approx 0.78 \, V_{dc}$ peak line voltage is replaced by full 120° square pulses giving $V_{L1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{2\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_{dc}/2 = 0.78 V_{dc}$).

### 2.2 Stator Voltage Control — The "Voltage Knob"

**Stator voltage control** is the simplest speed-control method for induction motors, applicable in the **low-speed region only**. By reducing the stator voltage, the **breakdown torque** ($T_{max} \propto V^2$) falls sharply, while the **synchronous speed remains unchanged** (set by frequency $f$).

**How it works:**
- Synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ → fixed by supply frequency.
- Torque is proportional to $V^2$ → as $V$ drops, torque curve flattens.
- Operating point shifts along the load line → motor speed drops.

**Disadvantage (KTU favourite question!):**
- Speed range is **very narrow** (only 10–15% below rated).
- High rotor currents at reduced voltage → high $I^2R$ losses.
- Not suitable for constant-torque loads.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **stator voltage control** (constant f, variable V) with **V/f control** (both V and f varied). They are NOT the same. Voltage control is a single-variable method; V/f control is a two-variable coordinated method.

### 2.3 V/F (Volts-per-Hertz) Speed Control — The Industry Standard

This is the **most widely used open-loop control scheme** for VSI-fed induction motors. The principle is elegantly simple:

$$\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_m = \text{constant}$$

**The "Why":**
- From the EMF equation: $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_1 \, \phi_m \, K_{w1}$
- If $f$ decreases and $V$ is held constant → $\phi_m$ rises → **magnetic saturation**, huge magnetizing current, possible burnout.
- If $f$ decreases and $V$ is reduced proportionally → $\phi_m$ stays constant → **constant torque capability** maintained.

**The "How":**
- The drive's control unit generates a **reference voltage $V^*$** and a **reference frequency $f^*$** in such a way that $V^*/f^* = k$ (a constant).
- A **voltage booster** (low-frequency voltage compensation) is added below ~5 Hz to overcome the stator resistance drop $I_1 R_1$, which would otherwise reduce flux at very low speeds.

### 2.4 Speed-Torque Characteristics — The Motor's "Personality Curve"

The classical induction motor torque equation (per-phase equivalent circuit, approximate):

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

where $\omega_s = 2\pi f / P$ is the synchronous angular speed in rad/s (mechanical).

**Three key points on this curve:**

| Region | Condition | Behaviour |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| **Low slip (linear region)** | $s \cdot (R_2'/s) \ll X_2'$ | $T \propto s$ (linear, useful for normal operation) |
| **Slip at max torque** | $s_m = R_2' / \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2}$ | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s (R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2+(X_1+X_2')^2})}$ |
| **High slip (breakdown)** | $s \to 1$ | $T \to T_{start}$ (starting torque) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** With V/f control, the **breakdown torque remains nearly constant** across the speed range below base speed. This is why V/f drives are excellent for **constant-torque loads** (conveyors, elevators, mixers) in the constant-flux region.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Region of Use |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed (RPM) | All regions |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip | All regions |
| 3 | $\phi_m = \dfrac{V}{4.44 f N_1 K_{w1}}$ | Air-gap flux (per phase EMF form) | EMF definition |
| 4 | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$ | V/f control law | **Below base speed** |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$ | Torque equation (exact) | All speeds |
| 6 | $T \approx \dfrac{3}{2 \pi f} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot s \cdot R_2'}{X_2'^2}$ (low slip) | Torque at low slip approximation | Below breakdown |
| 7 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s \left[R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2}\right]}$ | Maximum (breakdown) torque | All speeds |
| 8 | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$ | Slip at maximum torque | All speeds |
| 9 | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Rotor frequency (Hz) | Braking analysis |
| 10 | $V_{L1,rms,\text{6-step}} = 0.7798 \, V_{dc}$ | Line-to-line RMS, six-step VSI | Inverter output |
| 11 | $P_{mech} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}(1-s)$ | Mechanical power developed | Power flow |
| 12 | $P_{gap} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$ | Air-gap power | Power flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** V/f control with VSI is used in **fan drives, pump drives, HVAC systems, conveyor belts, machine tools, and EV traction** (where field weakening extends the speed range above base speed).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Synchronous Speed from Pole Pair and Frequency

The stator's three-phase winding produces a **rotating magnetic field** whose electrical angular speed is $\omega_e = 2\pi f$ rad/s. With $P$ poles ($P/2$ pole pairs), the mechanical synchronous speed is:

$$N_s = \frac{60 \cdot f}{P/2} = \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{(RPM)}$$

Converting to mechanical angular speed in rad/s:

$$\omega_{sm} = \frac{2\pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2\pi f}{P/2} = \frac{4 \pi f}{P}$$

This is the speed at which the air-gap field rotates, and the rotor always chases it with a slip $s$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Air-Gap Flux from V/f Law

The RMS EMF induced in a stator phase (transformer EMF equation for rotating machines) is:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_1 \cdot \phi_m \cdot K_{w1}$$

Under the simplifying assumption that the stator leakage impedance drop is negligible (valid at moderate frequencies > 5 Hz), $V_1 \approx E_1$:

$$V_1 \approx 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_1 \cdot \phi_m \cdot K_{w1}$$

Rearranging for flux:

$$\phi_m = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_1 \cdot K_{w1}}$$

This is the central equation of V/f control. The denominator $4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_1 \cdot K_{w1}$ is essentially a constant for a given motor, so:

$$\phi_m \propto \frac{V_1}{f}$$

**For constant flux:** $V_1 / f = \text{constant}$ ✅ — this is the V/f law.

**At very low frequency:** The stator resistance drop $I_1 R_1$ is no longer negligible compared to $E_1$, so a **voltage boost** $\Delta V$ is added:

$$V_1 \approx E_1 + I_1 R_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = 4.44 f N_1 \phi_m K_{w1} + I_1 R_1$$

At $f \to 0$, $V_1 \approx I_1 R_1$. So a small offset voltage (typically 2–8% of rated) is added in the V/f curve to maintain flux at low speeds.

### 3.3 Derivation: Torque Equation from Equivalent Circuit

Consider the per-phase equivalent circuit of the induction motor referred to stator:

- $R_1, X_1$: stator resistance and leakage reactance
- $R_2', X_2'$: rotor resistance and leakage reactance (referred to stator)
- $R_m$: magnetizing branch (representing core loss)
- $R_2'/s$: rotor resistance scaled by slip (Thevenin of rotor circuit)

**Step 1:** Air-gap power per phase:

$$P_{gap,\phi} = I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Total air-gap power (3 phases):

$$P_{gap} = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}$$

**Step 3:** Mechanical power developed (rotor copper losses are $s \cdot P_{gap}$):

$$P_{mech} = P_{gap} \cdot (1-s) = 3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s} \cdot (1-s)$$

**Step 4:** Torque = Power / Angular speed. The mechanical synchronous angular speed is:

$$\omega_{sm} = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \frac{4 \pi f}{P}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $P_{mech}$ and $\omega_{sm}$:

$$T = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_{sm}} = \frac{3 \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot R_2' (1-s)/s}{4 \pi f / P} = \frac{3 P \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot R_2' (1-s)}{4 \pi f \cdot s}$$

But in KTU convention, the equation is often written using electrical synchronous speed $\omega_s = 2\pi f / (P/2) = 4\pi f / P$, so:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 \cdot (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

This is the **exact torque expression** derivable directly by substituting $I_2' = V_1 / Z_{total}$ from the equivalent circuit.

### 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Torque and Slip at Max Torque

Differentiate the torque expression w.r.t. slip $s$ and set $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$:

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_{mT} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

Substituting back:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s \left[R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}\right]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neglecting $R_1$** (a common KTU approximation): If $R_1 \ll (X_1+X_2')$, then:
> $$s_{mT} \approx \frac{R_2'}{X_1 + X_2'}, \quad T_{max} \approx \frac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s (X_1 + X_2')}$$
>
> Since $(X_1 + X_2') \propto f$, we get $T_{max} \propto V_1^2 / f^2$. But with $V/f$ constant, $V_1 = k f$, so $T_{max} \propto k^2 f^2 / f^2 = k^2 = $ **constant** ✅

### 3.5 Derivation: Constant Power Region (Above Base Speed)

Above base frequency $f_b$, the stator voltage is **clamped at rated value** $V_1 = V_{rated}$ (you cannot exceed insulation limits). The frequency alone is increased to $f > f_b$:

$$\frac{V}{f} \Big|_{f > f_b} < \frac{V_{rated}}{f_b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi_m \propto \frac{1}{f}$$

This is the **field-weakening region** (analogous to DC motor field weakening). The torque falls as:

$$T \propto \phi_m \cdot I_2 \propto \frac{V}{f} \cdot I_2 \propto \frac{I_2}{f}$$

If we assume the current remains at rated value (limited by inverter and thermal ratings):

$$T \propto \frac{1}{f} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = T \cdot \omega \propto \frac{1}{f} \cdot f = \text{constant}$$

Hence the **constant-power region** above base speed (also called the **constant-horsepower region**).

### 3.6 Region Summary — Three Operating Zones

| Region | Frequency | Voltage | Flux | Torque | Power |
|--------|-----------|---------|------|--------|-------|
| **I. Constant Torque** | $0 < f \le f_b$ | $V = (V_{rated}/f_b) \cdot f$ | $\phi = \phi_{rated}$ (constant) | $T = T_{rated}$ (constant) | $P \propto f$ |
| **II. Constant Power** | $f_b < f \le f_{max}$ | $V = V_{rated}$ (clamped) | $\phi \propto 1/f$ (weakens) | $T \propto 1/f$ | $P = P_{rated}$ (constant) |
| **III. Series (High-speed)** | $f > f_{max}$ (limited) | $V = V_{rated}$ | $\phi \propto 1/f$ | $T \propto 1/f^2$ | $P \propto 1/f$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Special Note:** Most VSI-fed industrial drives operate in **Region I (constant torque)** and **Region II (constant power)**. Region III is rarely used as rotor mechanical stress becomes excessive.

### 3.7 Braking — Dynamic and Regenerative

#### A. Dynamic Braking (Plugging / DC Injection)

**Principle:** Disconnect the AC supply, and inject a **DC current** into any two stator phases (or all three). This creates a **stationary magnetic field** in the stator. As the rotor (still spinning) cuts this stationary field, eddy currents are induced, dissipating kinetic energy as heat in the rotor cage resistance.

- **Power flow:** $P_{mech} \to P_{rotor\ copper\ loss} \to \text{heat}$
- **Slip during braking:** $s = \dfrac{0 - N_r}{0 - N_r} = 1$ wait, since field is stationary, $\omega_s = 0$ and slip is mathematically $s = (0 - N_r)/0 = \infty$. The rotor "sees" effectively a very high slip and behaves like a shorted transformer secondary → high currents → strong braking torque.
- **Braking torque:** $T_b \propto \phi_{DC}^2 \cdot s_{eff}$

**Why the VSI makes this easy:** The same IGBT bridge can be reconfigured to inject DC by firing $S_1$ and $S_4$ simultaneously, with the upper or lower switches pulsed. No external braking resistor connection needed at the stator.

#### B. Regenerative Braking (True Energy Recovery)

**Principle:** When the rotor speed **exceeds the synchronous speed** of the stator field (i.e., $N_r > N_s$, so $s < 0$), the induction motor acts as a **generator**. The mechanical kinetic energy is converted back to electrical energy and fed to the DC link.

**How VSI enables this:**

1. The drive **reduces the stator frequency** $f$ such that $N_s = 120f/P$ falls **below the rotor speed** $N_r$.
2. The slip becomes **negative**: $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} < 0$.
3. The air-gap power becomes **negative** (flows from rotor to stator).
4. The IGBT bridge acts as a **rectifier**, pumping power back to the DC bus.
5. A **regeneration resistor** or a **bidirectional front-end converter** dissipates or feeds back the energy to the mains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why regenerative braking is the holy grail of EV drives:** It recovers up to **20–30%** of the battery energy during deceleration, significantly extending range. The VSI must support bidirectional power flow, which is why EV inverters use IGBTs with anti-parallel diodes and sometimes active front-end rectifiers.

### 3.8 Comparative Analysis: Dynamic vs. Regenerative Braking

| Parameter | Dynamic Braking | Regenerative Braking |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Energy destination | Wasted as heat in rotor/stator resistance | Recovered to DC link / mains |
| Efficiency | Very low (heat loss) | High (energy recovered) |
| Braking torque magnitude | High, can be controlled | Limited by inverter rating |
| Heat dissipation | Requires robust cooling | Minimal heating |
| Slip | $s \to \infty$ (effective) | $s < 0$ |
| VSI complexity | Simple (DC injection) | Complex (frequency reduction + bidirectional) |
| Use case | Emergency stops, hold-on, cranes | EVs, elevators (energy-saving) |
| Control complexity | Open-loop or simple closed-loop | Closed-loop with DC bus voltage management |
