## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **What is meant by V/f control in a three-phase VSI fed induction motor drive? Why is it necessary to maintain V/f constant?**

**Model Answer:**

V/f control refers to the technique of varying the stator voltage $V$ proportionally with the supply frequency $f$ such that the ratio $V/f$ remains constant. From the EMF equation:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_1 \cdot \phi_m \cdot K_{w1} \approx V_1$$

the air-gap flux $\phi_m = \dfrac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_1 K_{w1}}$. Hence $\phi_m \propto V/f$. To keep the flux (and hence torque capability) constant over the speed range, the ratio $V/f$ must be held constant. Below base speed, both $V$ and $f$ are varied together; above base speed, $V$ is clamped at rated value, leading to field weakening. (3 marks)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between dynamic braking and regenerative braking in VSI-fed induction motor drives.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dynamic Braking | Regenerative Braking |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Mode** | Motor acts as a generator dissipating kinetic energy as heat | Motor acts as a generator feeding energy back to the DC link |
| **Mechanism** | DC current is injected into the stator to create a stationary field; rotor energy is dissipated in rotor resistance | Stator frequency is reduced below rotor speed; slip becomes negative; power flows from rotor to DC bus |
| **Slip** | Effective slip is very high | Slip is negative ($s < 0$) |
| **Energy Recovery** | None (wasted as heat) | Energy is recovered |
| **Inverter Operation** | Some IGBTs are fired to inject DC; current is unidirectional into motor | Inverter operates in rectifier mode; bidirectional power flow |
| **Application** | Emergency stops, holding torque | Energy-saving in EVs, elevators, downhill conveyors |

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Instructions:** Answer ANY ONE FULL question from each module. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. (KTU ESE Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a three-phase VSI fed induction motor drive. Discuss the role of PWM in the VSI for low-speed operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 50 Hz, 4-pole, 400 V, 1450 RPM star-connected squirrel-cage induction motor is controlled by a VSI. Calculate: **(i)** Synchronous speed $N_s$, **(ii)** Slip $s$ at rated load, **(iii)** Frequency $f_1$ to be applied to obtain a speed of 1200 RPM with the same slip, **(iv)** The corresponding stator voltage assuming V/f control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Operation:**

A three-phase VSI fed induction motor drive consists of:

1. **Three-phase uncontrolled diode bridge rectifier:** Converts 3-phase 50 Hz AC mains (415 V) to a fixed DC link voltage $V_{dc} \approx 1.35 \cdot V_{L-L,rms} = 1.35 \cdot 415 = 560$ V (assuming ideal).
2. **DC link filter capacitor $C$:** Smooths the DC voltage to a stiff voltage source (essential for VSI operation).
3. **Three-phase VSI:** Six IGBT switches ($S_1$–$S_6$) in three legs. Each leg produces one phase of the AC output. The switches are fired with 60° conduction each, in the sequence $S_1 S_2 S_3 S_4 S_5 S_6$ to generate a six-step or PWM waveform.
4. **Induction motor:** Stator is fed from the VSI; rotor is the squirrel cage.

**PWM at low speeds:**

At low frequencies (below ~40 Hz), the **six-step mode produces low-order harmonics (5th, 7th) with significant amplitude**, causing torque pulsations, cogging, and rotor heating. To eliminate these, the VSI is operated in **SPWM (sinusoidal pulse-width modulation) mode** at low frequencies. The reference sine wave is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier (typically 1–15 kHz). The pulse widths are modulated to shape the fundamental sinusoid and push the harmonics to the high-frequency side (around the carrier frequency), where they are easily filtered by the motor's leakage inductance.

The modulation index $m_a = \dfrac{V_{control,peak}}{V_{carrier,peak}}$ controls the **output voltage magnitude**, while the reference frequency $f_{ref}$ controls the **output frequency**. The DC link voltage $V_{dc}$ remains constant, but by varying $m_a$, the RMS fundamental voltage is varied. This is the **modulation-based voltage control** inside the VSI.

Above ~40 Hz, the VSI switches to six-step mode to maximize the DC link utilization — the fundamental line-to-line RMS becomes $0.7798 \cdot V_{dc}$. The transition is smooth and automatic in modern DSP-controlled drives.

(7 marks)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $P = 4$, $V = 400$ V (line), $N_r = 1450$ RPM, star-connected.

**(i) Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Slip at rated load:**

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \frac{1500 - 1450}{1500} = \frac{50}{1500} = 0.0333 \text{ or } 3.33\%$$

**(iii) Frequency for 1200 RPM at same slip:**

For $N_r' = 1200$ RPM and $s = 0.0333$:

$$N_s' = \frac{N_r'}{1-s} = \frac{1200}{1 - 0.0333} = \frac{1200}{0.9667} = 1241.4 \text{ RPM}$$

$$f_1 = \frac{N_s' \cdot P}{120} = \frac{1241.4 \times 4}{120} = 41.38 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iv) Corresponding stator voltage (V/f control):**

$$\frac{V_1}{f_1} = \frac{V}{f} = \frac{400}{50} = 8 \text{ V/Hz}$$

$$V_1 = 8 \times 41.38 = 331.05 \text{ V (line, RMS)}$$

Per-phase voltage: $V_{1,ph} = \dfrac{331.05}{\sqrt{3}} = 191.1$ V.

(7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the torque of a 3-phase induction motor from the per-phase equivalent circuit. Hence, show that with V/f control, the maximum torque remains approximately constant below base speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the various operating regions of a VSI-fed induction motor drive below and above base speed. Discuss the braking methods used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Torque Derivation:**

From the per-phase equivalent circuit (referred to stator), the rotor current is:

$$I_2' = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}$$

Air-gap power per phase: $P_{gap,\phi} = I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$

Total air-gap power (3 phases): $P_{gap} = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}$

Mechanical power developed: $P_{mech} = P_{gap}(1-s) = 3 I_2'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_2'}{s}(1-s)$

Mechanical synchronous angular speed: $\omega_{sm} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$

Torque:

$$T = \frac{P_{mech}}{\omega_{sm}} = \frac{3 I_2'^2 (R_2'/s)(1-s)}{4 \pi f / P} = \frac{3P}{4\pi f} \cdot I_2'^2 \cdot \frac{R_2'}{s}(1-s)$$

Substituting $I_2'$:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$$

where $\omega_s = \omega_{sm}$ in mechanical rad/s.

**Maximum torque:**

Differentiating and setting $\dfrac{dT}{ds} = 0$:

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2'}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}}, \quad T_{max} = \frac{3 V_1^2}{2\omega_s [R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2')^2}]}$$

Neglecting $R_1$ (valid approximation):

$$T_{max} \approx \frac{3 V_1^2}{2 \omega_s (X_1 + X_2')}$$

Since $(X_1 + X_2') = 2\pi f (L_1 + L_2') \propto f$, and $\omega_s \propto f$:

$$T_{max} \propto \frac{V_1^2}{f^2}$$

With V/f control, $V_1 = k f$, so $V_1^2 = k^2 f^2$:

$$\boxed{T_{max} \propto \frac{k^2 f^2}{f^2} = k^2 = \text{constant}}$$

This proves that with V/f control below base speed, the maximum (breakdown) torque is independent of frequency and remains constant. ✅

(7 marks)

---

**(b) Operating Regions and Braking:**

**Region I — Constant Torque Region (0 < f ≤ f_b):**

- Voltage is varied linearly with frequency: $V = (V_{rated}/f_b) \cdot f$
- Flux $\phi_m$ is held constant.
- Motor can deliver **rated torque** continuously at any speed in this range.
- Suitable for conveyors, mixers, elevators (constant-torque loads).
- The torque-speed curve simply **shifts horizontally** as $f$ changes; the envelope of maximum torque is a horizontal line.

**Region II — Constant Power Region (f_b < f ≤ f_max):**

- Voltage is clamped at $V_{rated}$ (cannot exceed insulation limits).
- Frequency increases, $V/f$ ratio decreases → flux $\phi_m \propto 1/f$ → field weakens.
- Torque falls as $T \propto 1/f$ (since $P = T\omega = $ constant).
- Motor delivers rated power but reduced torque.
- Used in machine tool spindle drives where high speed at lower torque is required (e.g., finishing cuts).

**Region III — Series Region (f > f_max):**

- In practice rarely used.
- Torque falls as $T \propto 1/f^2$, power as $P \propto 1/f$.
- Limited by rotor mechanical stress and inverter current capability.

**Braking Methods:**

1. **Dynamic Braking:** DC current is injected into two stator phases via the IGBT bridge. A stationary magnetic field is established. The spinning rotor cuts this field, inducing currents that dissipate energy as heat in the rotor cage. Braking torque is high initially and decays to zero as rotor stops.

2. **Regenerative Braking:** Stator frequency is reduced below rotor speed. Slip becomes negative. Motor acts as a generator. The IGBT bridge now rectifies the power, sending it back to the DC link. A braking resistor (or bidirectional front-end converter) dissipates or returns this energy.

**V/F curve with braking regions:**

A typical V/f curve is shown below (described verbally since the figure must be hand-drawn or plotted):

- **Below 0 Hz (extended):** Quadrant III, regenerative mode (motor overhauled by load).
- **0 to f_b:** Quadrant I, motoring (constant torque).
- **Above f_b:** Quadrant I, motoring (constant power).
- **Negative direction motoring:** Reverse the phase sequence of the VSI (swap any two phases).

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
>
> 1. **Confusing V/f control with simple V control:** Many students write "voltage is varied to control speed" — this is **incorrect** for V/f. Always emphasize that **both V and f are varied together**.
> 2. **Forgetting the low-frequency voltage boost:** Examiners specifically test whether students know that $V = k f + V_{boost}$ at low frequencies. Missing this loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up field-weakening direction:** Some students incorrectly say flux is increased above base speed. The flux **decreases** (field weakens) above base speed because $V$ is clamped.
> 4. **Stator voltage control mistakes:** Stating that voltage control is efficient or has a wide speed range will cost marks. It has a **narrow** range (~10–15%) and is **inefficient** (high $I^2R$ losses).
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always write **RPM, Hz, V, Nm, W** explicitly. KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units.
> 6. **Regenerative braking — missing the bidirectional inverter:** A VSI with a simple diode rectifier at the front end **cannot feed power back to the mains**; it can only charge the DC link capacitor. Mentioning this nuance scores extra credit.
> 7. **Forgetting the slip sign convention:** In motor mode, $s > 0$; in generator mode (regenerative braking), $s < 0$. Examiners test this specifically.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- [x] A **VSI-fed IM drive** uses a **DC link → VSI → IM** topology. The VSI is a **voltage source** (not current source) — hence the name.
- [x] **Stator voltage control** is a **single-variable** method (only V varied, f fixed). Speed range is **narrow** (~10–15%). Torque varies as $V^2$.
- [x] **V/f control** is a **two-variable coordinated** method. Below base speed, $V/f = $ constant → flux $\phi_m = $ constant → **constant torque** capability.
- [x] **Base speed** $N_b = 120 f_b / P$. At base speed, $V = V_{rated}$ and $f = f_{rated}$ (50 Hz).
- [x] **Below base speed:** Constant torque region ($T = T_{rated}$). Power rises with speed.
- [x] **Above base speed:** Constant power region. $V$ is **clamped at $V_{rated}$**; flux falls as $1/f$ → **field weakening**. Torque falls as $1/f$.
- [x] **Air-gap flux equation:** $\phi_m = V_1 / (4.44 f N_1 K_{w1})$ — memorize this.
- [x] **Torque equation (exact):** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_1^2 (R_2'/s)}{(R_1 + R_2'/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2}$.
- [x] **Maximum torque (approximate):** $T_{max} \approx 3 V_1^2 / [2 \omega_s (X_1 + X_2')] \propto V^2/f^2$.
- [x] **With V/f control:** $T_{max}$ is **constant** below base speed (since $V/f$ is constant).
- [x] **Low-frequency voltage boost:** $V = kf + V_{boost}$ to overcome stator $I_1 R_1$ drop.
- [x] **PWM mode** is used at **low frequencies** to eliminate low-order harmonics; **six-step** mode at high frequencies for maximum DC utilization.
- [x] **Dynamic braking:** DC injection → stationary field → rotor energy → heat. No energy recovery.
- [x] **Regenerative braking:** $f$ reduced below rotor speed → $s < 0$ → generator action → energy back to DC link. Requires **bidirectional** power flow.
- [x] **PWM modulation index** $m_a$ controls voltage; **reference frequency** $f_{ref}$ controls output frequency.
- [x] **Slip at max torque:** $s_{mT} = R_2' / \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2')^2} \approx R_2' / (X_1 + X_2')$ when $R_1$ is neglected.
- [x] **Six-step VSI line voltage RMS:** $V_{L,rms} = 0.7798 \, V_{dc}$.
- [x] **Applications:** HVAC fans, pumps, conveyors, machine tools, EVs, elevators, textile mills, cranes.
